elements.name: Committee for Standardization
elements.documentation: The Committee for Standardization (CS) is the senior NATO committee for Alliance standardization, composed primarily of representatives from all NATO countries. Operating under the authority of the North Atlantic Council (NAC), it issues policy and guidance for all NATO standardization activities. Its mission is to exert domain governance for standardization policy and management within the Alliance to contribute to Allies’ development of interoperable and cost-effective military forces and capabilities.

elements.name: Conference of National Armaments Directors
elements.documentation: The Conference of National Armaments Directors (CNAD) is the senior NATO committee responsible for promoting cooperation between countries in the armaments field. By enabling multinational cooperation, it plays an essential role in ensuring that NATO forces have cutting-edge, interoperable equipment available at all times.

elements.name: Security Committee
elements.documentation: The Security Committee (SC) examines all questions concerning NATO security policy and acts as an advisory body to the North Atlantic Council (NAC). It reviews the NATO security policy, makes recommendations for changes and examines questions related to the subject.

elements.name: Military Committee
elements.documentation: The Military Committee (MC) is the senior military authority in NATO and the oldest permanent body in NATO after the North Atlantic Council, both having been formed only months after the Alliance came into being. It is the primary source of military advice to the North Atlantic Council and the Nuclear Planning Group, and gives direction to the two Strategic Commanders.

elements.name: Resource Policy and Planning Board
elements.documentation:
The Resource Policy and Planning Board (RPPB) is the senior advisory body to the North Atlantic Council (also known as the Council or NAC) on the management of common-funded NATO resources. It has responsibility for the overall management of NATO’s civil and military budgets, as well as the NATO Security Investment Programme (NSIP).
The Budget Committee and the Investment Committee report to the RPPB. The Budget Committee reviews and recommends civil and military budgets, while the Investment Committee is responsible for the implementation of the NSIP, which finances the provision of capabilities needed to support the roles of the two Strategic Commands.

elements.name: Digital Policy Committee
elements.documentation: NATO’s Digital Policy Committee (DPC) is the senior multinational policy committee in the area of NATO’s digital initiatives, and Consultation, Command and Control (C3). It develops policy, and provides guidance and advice in areas such as C3, cyber defence, interoperability standards and digital capabilities to the North Atlantic Council (NAC). Spearheading NATO’s Digital Transformation, the DPC works towards NATO’s digital primacy in the face of an ever-changing technological landscape.

elements.name: Archives Committee
elements.documentation: The Archives Committee assists and advises the North Atlantic Council (NAC) on all archives and records-related matters to ensure the preservation of and public access to information of permanent value held in the NATO Archives. Reporting directly to the NAC, it is the only body tasked with NATO-wide responsibilities related to the corporate management of the Organization’s records and archives.

elements.name: Defence Policy and Planning Committee
elements.documentation:
The Defence Policy and Planning Committee (DPPC) is the senior advisory body to the North Atlantic Council on defence matters concerning all member countries and it also has the lead on defence aspects of partnership.It is a key committee bringing together defence counsellors from all national delegations.  It deals with a broad range of issues such as transformation, defence capabilities, agency reform and common-funded acquisition, and in Reinforced format (DPPC(R)) it manages the NATO Defence Planning Process.
Chairmanship is flexible depending on the topics being discussed, but the DPPC’s permanent chair is the Assistant Secretary General for Defence Policy and Planning; in Reinforced format it is chaired by the Deputy Secretary General of NATO. The deputy chair is the Deputy Assistant Secretary General for Defence Policy and Planning.
This committee has been called the DPPC since the June 2010 committee reform. It replaced both the Executive Working Group and the Defence Review Committee. It has no subordinate committees under its remit.

elements.name: Urgent need to out-match strategic competitors and potential adversaries
elements.documentation: NATO’s edge will be in peril by 2030 if no immediate action is taken to out-match the energy and efforts our strategic competitors and potential adversaries invest in developing and integrating new digital capabilities.

elements.name: 2022 Strategic Concept
elements.documentation: The Strategic Concept sets the Alliance’s strategy. It outlines NATO’s enduring purpose and nature, its fundamental security tasks, and the challenges and opportunities it faces in a changing security environment. It also specifies the elements of the Alliance’s approach to security and provides guidelines for its political and military adaptation.

elements.name: Safeguard the freedom and security of Allies.
Commitment to the purposes and principles of the Charter of the United Nations and the North Atlantic Treaty
Strengthen the Alliance based on our indivisible security, solidarity, and ironclad commitment to defend each other.
elements.documentation: Strengthen our Alliance based on our indivisible security, solidarity, and ironclad commitment to defend each other, as enshrined in Article 5 of the North Atlantic Treaty. Our ability to deter and defend is the backbone of that commitment.

elements.name: Ensure the collective defence and security of all Allies
elements.documentation: NATO will continue to fulfil three core tasks: deterrence and defence; crisis prevention and management; and cooperative security. These are complementary to ensure the collective defence and security of all Allies.

elements.name: Enhance individual and collective resilience and technological edge
North Atlantic Council
elements.documentation: The North Atlantic Council is the principal political decision-making body within NATO. It oversees the political and military process relating to security issues affecting the whole Alliance. It brings together representatives of each member country to discuss policy or operational questions requiring collective decisions, providing a forum for wide-ranging consultation between members on all issues affecting their peace and security.

elements.name: Digital Transformation Vision
elements.documentation: By 2030, NATO Digital Transformation will enable the Alliance to conduct multi-domain operations, ensure interoperability across all domains, enhance situational awareness, and facilitate political consultation and data-driven decision-making.

elements.name: Enhanced Political Consultation and Data-Driven Decision Making
Data Centric Governance
elements.documentation: NATO should shape the information environment to ensure operational integrity and the unencumbered, resilient flow of data stemming from diverse but integrated sources. The exploitation of data requires adequate standards for data management, harmonised standards for data sharing, and it should be protected and guided by an appropriate Data Governance framework and controls, and augmented by data management, data science and AI capabilities.

elements.name: Digital Services for a Data-Driven Alliance
elements.documentation: Enhancing situational awareness, consultation and decision-making processes requires new ways of cooperation and modern and resilient digital services that support political and military leaders. The implementation of such services foresees secure access to trusted data, the availability of a single logical environment for data exploitation - offering advanced analytics and AI capabilities - and a data-centric architecture that provides persistent access to digital services across the Alliance.

elements.name: Digital Backbone
elements.documentation: The Alliance needs data-centric, inter-connected digital systems, which can integrate easily and securely across the various organizational and national boundaries, in peacetime as well as during missions and operations. This implies that the Digital Backbone will have to provide the technical means for providing universal connectivity and data transport not just across the traditional domains of operations (Maritime, Land and Air), but also for the new domains of Space and Cyberspace, including Allies and Partners as described in the Alliance Concept for MDO. The Digital Backbone integrates digital capabilities, connects sensors, effectors and decision makers across military and political spheres, driving integration and interoperability across domains and platforms, and enabling seamless collaboration. It also hosts the Data Fabric that enables users and digital services to access and process data rapidly and securely at every level. The Digital Backbone’s key components include federated networks, cloud computing, and a design based on a service oriented architecture paradigm.

elements.name: Alliance Data Sharing Ecosystem
elements.documentation: Data sharing is an imperative for the Alliance that should be pursued through systematic efforts. Sharing interoperable data to achieve Allied objectives shall become the norm and a shared responsibility for Allies and the NATO Enterprise alike. Initiatives to share data with Allied industry and research partners will be encouraged and strengthened, as appropriate. To achieve success, data sharing must transcend operational silos and build on the willingness to share of established communities. It must be driven by intended uses of the data that support Allied policies and strategies, while respecting NATO’s Principles of Responsible Use of AI and data. In particular, it is vital to stimulate cooperation on sharing training data for realising the Alliance’s data exploitation and AI ambitions, for supporting Allied innovation initiatives (e.g. DIANA and the NATO Innovation Fund), and for strengthening the Alliance’s position vis-à-vis strategic competitors. As result, an Alliance Data Sharing Ecosystem will be established, where data from Allies, the NATO Enterprise, and industry and research partners, as appropriate, is shared, labelled, connected and exploited.

elements.name: Digital-ready Processes
elements.documentation: Setting fit-for-purpose, agile processes to accelerate the design, acquisition, delivery and operation of the Digital Backbone is critical, together with the imperative to find a balance between innovation requirements and efficient control mechanisms. NATO shall redesign processes in areas such as capability planning, procurement, acquisition, and security accreditation to reflect evolving requirements brought about by new trends, such as EDTs, data-centricity, and zero trust. In addition, it is necessary to change policy, doctrine and ways of working in multiple areas, such as automating the authorisation processes for the allocation and use of national capabilities in NATO-led multi domain operations.

elements.name: Alignment of Digital Programmes
elements.documentation: The NATO Enterprise shall work towards aligning and improving the various capability delivery mechanisms to expedite the implementation of digital programmes. To consolidate the array of NATO’s Digital Capabilities, it is of prime importance to include cyber security aspects in the ongoing and upcoming digital transformation initiatives of the Alliance, and to connect them to ongoing NATO’s and Allies’ cyber security and defence initiatives.

elements.name: Alliance Digital Initiatives
elements.documentation: Digital Transformation will leverage the Federated Mission Networking (FMN) Initiative to align and synchronize the planning and development of interoperable capabilities among Allies, the NATO Enterprise, partners and international organizations. Digital Transformation also presents an opportunity for the Allies that have already set out on an innovation path to offer their experience and solutions to the Alliance, with the creation of a forum where NATO staff facilitates the development of bilateral or multinational cooperation programmes, and national solutions could be offered and expanded for the Alliance’s benefit.

elements.name: Cooperation with Industry and academia
elements.documentation: NATO shall broaden cooperation with partners from industry and Academia and develop mechanisms for a mutual enriching collaboration. Leveraging ongoing efforts, such mechanisms could include coordinated staff-level engagements with industry partners, and a framework contract that could address different technology verticals, in areas ranging from data science and AI through to quantum computing and space technology.

elements.name: Digital-ready Combat Forces
elements.documentation: A modern, effective NATO Force Structure requires modernised capabilities and trained personnel, primed through national and collective exercises, prepared to exploit the advantage offered by digital technologies, and ready to successfully operate in MDO scenarios. Allies will ensure the provision of adequate combat forces by adhering to relevant Digital Transformation considerations and requirements as reflected within the NATO Defence Planning Process.

elements.name: Digital-ready Workforce
elements.documentation: Digital transformation, leveraging ongoing efforts under NATO’s Coherent Implementation Strategy on Emerging and Disruptive Technologies (EDTs), will deliver a digital-ready workforce for the NATO Enterprise. This will help develop and implement digital-ready policies, and foster a culture of innovation across the Alliance. In addition to training programmes, the development of a digital-ready workforce will include fellowship programmes, secondments and exchanges between Allies, NATO and the private sector.

elements.name: Digital Interoperability Framework
elements.documentation: It is necessary to improve the governance of Allied digital interoperability activities to establish cross-organisational relationships, to streamline processes supporting end-to-end digital services, and to ensure that both existing and new policies promote interoperability efforts. A common approach will be established for willing Allies to offer digital services, federate them, and address security, data management and protection requirements. Digital Interoperability goes beyond the development of new technologies and includes innovation, acquisition, operations, and sustainment of legacy products. The implementation of a Digital Interoperability Framework will contribute to the quality of data and digital services and will create an environment where allies and NATO Enterprise entities can fruitfully cooperate and federate digital services. In addition, industry’s role is essential in shaping the standards necessary for promoting full digital interoperability across the Alliance.

elements.name: Securely connect sensors, operators and decision makers, and enable digital interoperability across all domains within the NATO Enterprise, and between NATO and Allies’ Consultation, Command and Control systems and services through a resilient Digital Backbone;
Align NATO’s ongoing digital initiatives, adapt them, and develop new initiatives, as necessary;
Build, where relevant, upon the model of Federated Mission Networking and other initiatives which help connect the assets of NATO, Allies, and mission Partners;
Take a holistic approach and embrace significant changes in the way NATO operates
Guide improvements in process, organisation, talent and training, data and analytics capabilities, and governance;
Foster a digital-ready workforce, including a culture shift which encourages and rewards innovation, experimentation, data-sharing and calculated/informed risk-taking.
Ensure the necessary technologies are identified, acquired and secured; and every effort is made to accelerate the delivery and integration of state-of-the-art solutions;
Facilitate harmonisation across Allies’ national efforts, to maximise interoperability and efficiency gains;
NATO isn't developing and integrating digital capabilities fast enough.
elements.documentation: NATO isn't developing and integrating digital capabilities fast enough.

elements.name: Data Exploitation Vision
elements.documentation: NATO's Data Exploitation will achieve information superiority and data-driven decision making at all levels across the Alliance by fully leveraging the value of NATO generated, national, and publicly available data.

elements.name: Forum for Alliance Digital Transformation cooperation programmes
elements.documentation:
Digital Transformation presents an opportunity for the Allies that set out on a innovation path to offer their experience, success criteria and solutions to the Alliance’s wider audience. Allies could create a forum where bilateral or multilateral, multinational DT related cooperation programmes would be established, where national solutions could be offered and expanded for the Alliance’s benefit.
An initial assessment of requirements and options for the establishment of such a forum and cooperation programmes will be made in 2024, and reviewed annually.

elements.name: Leverage, align, and expedite Alliance capability development with FMN
elements.documentation: Allies will align their capability development initiatives to equip the NATO Force Structure and future Alliance Response Force with FMN compliant, interoperable C2 and CIS at the tactical level. Additionally, Allies and NATO Enterprise will consider implementing the FMN spiral specifications within their static networks to ensure interoperability at all levels. Progress will be reported annually starting from Q4 2023.

elements.name: Facilitate inclusion of Digital Transformation capability development requirements within FMN
elements.documentation: Promote inclusion of Digital Transformation requirements, including cloud and data interoperability, Zero Trust/ Data –Centric Security, cyber security, data exploitation, and AI interoperability aspects, within Federated Mission Networking (FMN) spiral specification roadmaps to ensure FMN compliant capabilities will be fit for purpose to support the Strategic Outcomes.

elements.name: Mature a NATO Enterprise Capability Development Plan (CDP) to synchronise NATO 2030 requirements and align capability delivery to support MDO
elements.documentation: Mature and sustain the NATO Enterprise Capability Development Plan (CDP), with operational success criteria, as per ACT/CAPDEV/CAP/TT-6494/SER:NS725, to coherently synchronise NATO 2030 key requirement areas, ongoing capability programmes and enable progress for the Warfare Development Agenda and MDO. This includes aligning the approaches of capability and interoperability development for single operational domains (Air, Cyber, Land, Maritime, Space) and functional areas (Joint C2, Logistics, JISR, etc.), with a focus on supporting the Multi-Joint Operational Area C2 Environment Strategy, taking a common data centric approach, and ensuring interoperability within the Digital Backbone.

elements.name: Augment NATO CDP to align delivery of NATO Enterprise digital capabilities and services
elements.documentation: Establish a coherent overview and facilitate alignment of additional ongoing programmes/ projects (beyond those captured in CDP above, such as those funded by Civil and Military Budgets) for procuring, acquiring, delivering, and/or maintaining capabilities/ ICT services. A first overview will be delivered in Q4 2023, which will be reviewed annually.

elements.name: Align and accelerate data exploitation and AI capability delivery programmes to expedite data exploitation capacity building
elements.documentation:
Continue to monitor the data exploitation and AI technology programmes landscape, as per PO(2022)0409 (INV), and use Agile methodologies to accelerate the delivery of related projects to mature data exploitation capacity to enable the strategic outcomes, including MDO. Initial focus should be in the context of the eight Alliance priority data exploitation use cases and six AI Strategy use cases (described in PO(2022)0409 (INV) and PO(2021)0350), as applicable, building upon (and re-using as appropriate) the experience/knowledge/services from existing prototypes under more mature capability delivery projects (e.g. Information Environment Assessment Capability as per PO(2023)0021 (INV)). 
Progress will be reported annually, informed by the AI Strategy Progress Report to DPRC, capturing the impacts to DT.

elements.name: Ensure NATO Enterprise’s Digital Transformation Action Plan includes Requirements stemming from the Digital Transformation Implementation Strategy
Adapt legacy C2 CIS applications to enable cross-domain C2 and information exchange, and support MDO and DT requirements
elements.documentation: To ensure legacy C2 CIS applications are able to support MDO and DT requirements, this LoE will ensure inclusion of requirements to adapt and digitise legacy/ongoing domain-specific C2 CIS programmes, for example, to enable online analytical processing, data transaction processing, geospatial services, and cross-domain C2 and information exchange. These requirements could include implementing Web Service Interfaces through integration of Application Programming Interfaces (API) (as recommended in AC/322-WP(2022)0059-REV2). This may also include requirements to transition into cloud-native applications, to support the Multi-JOA C2 Environment Strategy, and to be interoperable within the Digital Backbone. These should be done in coordination and aligned with the CDP LoE, and the delivery of these requirements will be facilitated by providing ad-hoc testing and experimentation in digitised environments.

elements.name: Align cybersecurity programmes to improve the cybersecurity posture of the NATO Enterprise
elements.documentation: Sustain the alignment of the implementation of Cyberspace Capabilities Programme Plan (PO(2022)0348-AS1(INV)), including crypto modernization, with the Cyber Adaptation programme (PO(2021)0253) and the implementation of Cyberspace as an Operational Domain (MCM-0013-2023 (INV)) to improve the cybersecurity posture of the NATO Enterprise. This will enhance the coherence and synchronization of NATO’s digital transformation with cyber security initiatives.

elements.name: Align and advance implementation of Space as an Operational Domain
elements.documentation: Align implementation of space-related capability programmes with the implementation of Space as an Operational Domain (PO(2022)0415(INV)), across the Alliance to support the conduct of MDO. Implement the respective NATO Enterprise element through delivery of the required Operational Functions (Space Domain Awareness, Operational Space Support and Space Domain Coordination), fully integrated within ACO’s processes and services, and with well-trained personnel.

elements.name: Align intelligence systems through further development of the NISA Strategy
elements.documentation:
Enhance the coherence and synchronization of intelligence systems within the NATO Intelligence Enterprise (NIE) and between the NIE and national intelligence services through further development of the NATO Intelligence Systems Architecture (NISA) Strategy and Roadmaps
The delivery of the updated NISA Strategy is anticipated for 4QR23.

elements.name: Align implementation of Multi-Domain/Joint Effects Synchronisation, Targeting and Fires’ capabilities
elements.documentation:
Align implementation of Multi-Domain/Joint Effects Synchronisation, Targeting and Fires’ (ETF) capabilities with other C2, Intelligence, and Operations capabilities across the Alliance, by ensuring the:
Development and harmonization of ETF data exchange and interoperability standards, as necessary
Implementation of those ETF data exchange and interoperability standards, via FMN, in NCS and National Capabilities (including Intelligence collection and data, Operational Planning, Electromagnetic Operations, Common Operational Picture)
Planning and coordination of the life cycle and delivery of the NATO Enterprise ETF and related capability elements.

elements.name: Adapt facilities to host critical technology infrastructure
elements.documentation: Enhance/ adapt current facilities to enable survivable and resilient hosting of critical CIS infrastructure and data, and ensure these requirements are captured in relevant capability programme plans.

elements.name: Align consumption of common geospatial services to relevant NATO CIS
elements.documentation:
Sustain and mature the common geospatial services to all NATO CIS (at all classification levels), to include:
 1. Core GIS Increment 3 mitigation delivery (software and hardware upgrade)
 2. two-way sharing of geo-referenced information, delivery and consumption of geospatial web services (including geospatial web processing services); development of roadmap for FAS development with respect to consuming geospatial web services, and
 3. provision of tools to ensure coherent and high speed geospatial fusion and analysis across all domains (through CoreGIS Inc 5 and GECS).

elements.name: Digital Transformation inputs to the NATO-wide HR Strategy
elements.documentation:
Collaborate with stakeholders involved with developing the EDT, AI, data, and cyber literacy lines of effort, under different NATO policies and strategies, to provide digital competency inputs into the upcoming NATO-wide HR Strategy, which is due to be updated by end of 2023. Considerations for inputs could include:
 1. Review and update the Peacetime Establishment numbers and NATO staff Job Descriptions, to ensure workforce capacity and competencies in digital and data skills as well as in supporting functions, such as HR, Procurement, Legal, etc., for implementing digital transformation and EDT agendas.
 2. Develop appropriate training curricula and establish agreements with commercial training providers (online/ on-premises) to develop and upskill staff digital competences, as well as customised NATO training (e.g. in NCI Academy) to be pursued for bespoke training requirements.
 3. Implement a professional digital competency development framework and incentivised capacity building programmes to generate both generic digital literacy and dedicated technical digital skills, based on priority areas, competencies and skills for different roles within the Organization.
 4. Deliver fit-for-purpose talent acquisition and retention strategy for a digital-ready workforce, including considerations to recruit and retain personnel prepared to engage with DT efforts from a SME perspective, and deliver a digitally enabled recruiting process that leverages automation to expedite the process, optimize the time-to-recruit, and assesses the digital competencies of candidates

elements.name: Balance workforce capacity with Digital Transformation demands within NCIA
elements.documentation:
Review, propose way ahead, and implement actions to ensure a balanced workforce capacity – both quantitatively and qualitatively – in NCIA. This should include functions such as Procurement, Vendor management etc., and enable capacity that is commensurate with the needs for Digital Programmes’ implementation, through possibly a combination of reorganization, recruitment and retention of skilled personnel.
Initial review and proposal by 1QR24, with annual reviews and progress reports.

elements.name: Allies increasing availability of digital-ready workforces
elements.documentation:
Allies to consider ways to promote, increase, and expedite the availability of digital-ready workforces for the Alliance, leveraging national and multinational initiatives. Things to be considered may include ways to facilitate fellowship programmes, secondments, and exchanges; the possibility to update their internal processes to expedite the delivery of Security Clearance to NATO selected candidates; and ways to incorporate basic Digital Transformation competencies into the Nations’ military organization.
An options paper, to be developed by IS-ESC, will be submitted to the DPRC for their consideration in 1QR24.

elements.name: Establish Training Disciplines to ensure availability of training programmes on digital technologies and EDTs
elements.documentation:
NMAs to review the requirement for establishing, and then implement actions to establish (if necessary) the requisite Training Disciplines to ensure AI, data, cyber, and/or training programmes can be planned, developed, and rolled out to the NATO military structures.
For training falling under national responsibility, the ACO Forces Standards should be updated, as necessary.

elements.name: Develop and implement a culture change roadmap to transform into a data-driven organisation
elements.documentation: One of the goals of NATO’s Digital Transformation is to foster a digital-ready workforce, including a culture shift that encourages data-driven decision-making. Leveraging related efforts under the EDT Coherent Implementation Strategy, this LoE will deliver a culture change roadmap and actionable interventions to kick start “minimum viable changes” to transform into a digital and data-driven culture. There will be regular reviews and updates to measure progress and adjust, as needed.

elements.name: Ensure requirements captured in NDPP
elements.documentation: Continue to include Digital Transformation considerations and requirements within the NDPP in order to enable Alliance to conduct MDO.

elements.name: Leverage collective exercises to train combat forces to be digital-ready and MDO ready
elements.documentation: Leverage ongoing efforts to incorporate modelling and simulation, including distributed synthetic training into NATO exercises to include requirements for multi-domain strategic, operational and tactical level operations. An initial plan will be developed by 1QR24, and annual reporting and updates.

elements.name: Accelerated delivery of next-generation Modelling and Simulation Services
elements.documentation:
Accelerate the delivery of next generation Modelling and Simulation Services (as agreed with MCM-0205-2022 (INV)) to augment operational and strategic planning, studies, and wargaming capacity
This should include requirements to develop a single synthetic environment (SSE) where all intelligence disciplines feed their data, to be used for training, war gaming, modelling and simulation, AI training and the development of digital twins.

elements.name: Alliance Digital Workplace
elements.documentation:
Develop a Digital Workplace realizing an Alliance-wide collaboration ecosystem (including office automation, audio/visual communication, etc.) for conducting enhanced day-to-day business activities, on various confidentiality classification levels, accessible via desktop systems (e.g. laptops, thin/thick clients, etc.) and integrated as appropriate to NATO HQ’s conference services.
This should also consider:
 1. The Enabling Enhanced Consultations project, led by IS-EM, to enable classified consultations through secure, resilient and redundant communications tools with principals in Allied and partner nations, focused on voice, video and interpretation requirements. Requirements are expected to be gathered by end 2023, with procurement activities to commence in 2024-2025 period.
 2. The requirement to align how military and political Senior Policy Committees consult, provide comments and agree documents across committees and boards. The approach should leverage on best practices and tools (e.g. Military Committee Interaction System) that streamline and enhance (through automation) the consultation process (including cross-committee coordination).
Relevant technical standards shall adhere to NATO Interoperability Standards and Profiles (NISP) Version 14 (AC/322-D(2021)0014), to facilitate interoperability at all levels.

elements.name: Deliver multi-domain alert system
elements.documentation: Design and develop a state-of-the-art (autonomous) multi-domain alert system (to include necessary indicators), as part of an improved response system (MCM-0234-2022) to inform political decision-making and enable effective military preparedness. ACO will set out the requirements for the future MDO Alert System, anticipated to be delivered by 4QR24. Then ACO will work with ACT to specify appropriate mechanisms to design and deliver such a system.

elements.name: Aligned data-centric governance framework overview
elements.documentation:
Provide an overview of the data-centric governance framework, including roles and responsibilities, principles, policies, processes, frameworks, tools, metrics, and oversight required to effectively govern and manage data throughout the data exploitation lifecycle, from creation to disposition, as per NATO’s DEF Strategic Plan (PO(2022)0409 (INV)). This should be aligned with activities and outputs as detailed in the Lines of Effort described in Appendix 3 to Annex 1 of the DEF Strategic Plan.
(The OCIO will be the Lead entity for the actions which are related to the NATO Enterprise)
An appropriate record/flow architecture will be developed under the control of NATO Archives and Information Management (IS-EM(ICTM-AIM)) for the entire lifecycle of NATO information, data and records, including retention, disposition, and long-term preservation.

elements.name: Alliance data-centric governance programme
elements.documentation: Establish an Alliance Data-centricity governance programme to transform NATO into a data-centric organization.

elements.name: Facilitate implementation of NATO Core Metadata Standards
elements.documentation: Allies and NATO Enterprise to pursue Alliance data interoperability by implementing NATO Core Metadata Standards (4774, 4778, and 5636), including location (as per geospatial metadata standards) as the first step towards NATO becoming a data-centric organization.

elements.name: Establish NATO Enterprise processes for registering and managing quality data throughout its lifecycle
elements.documentation:
Develop the necessary management processes and procedures for NATO Enterprise staff to ensure quality data is registered and managed throughout its lifecycle. This will also include exploring evolution of NATO Enterprise Resource Planning.
This will include development and implementation of an Enterprise-wide Directive to ensure Personal Data Protection, in compliance with NATO policies, and protect NATO’s workforce personal data, without adverse impact on the NATO missions and objectives.

elements.name: Deliver technical services to manage taxonomies and semantic references as part of overall master data management
elements.documentation: Deliver a technical solution to develop, manage, store and provide broad, simple human and system access to taxonomies/semantic references. The technical services will be accessible tacross the Alliance but only the taxonomies/semantic references developed collectively by Allies in the TAs will be included. The NSO is to initiate the development of tools and associated services to fulfil this requirement by June 2023, with IOC expected in 4QR24 and FOC in 4QR25.

elements.name: Enable data-centric and semantic interoperability-by-design
elements.documentation:
In order to enable interoperability-by-design, all Standardization Tasking Authorities (TAs) will need to:
 1. Incorporate the NATO Core Metadata Standards into their respective NATO Data Standards to ensure data are labelled with common metadata.
 2. Review and enhance data standards to ensure the right quality in data25 (i.e. data that are: Visible, Accessible, Understandable, Linked, Trustworthy, Interoperable, and Secure) that allows exploitation for data-driven decision-making.
 3. Initiate, share, and maintain the taxonomies and semantic reference models, using the technical services provided in previous LoE
 4. Use the Semantic Reference models for the development/ maintenance of their Standards
 5. Provide semantic mapping of their semantic reference models to NATO’s Core Data Framework, and contribute to their harmonization
 6. Submit a plan to C3B for achieving these activities, and report annually on their progress

elements.name: Alliance Interoperability Coherence Initiative
elements.documentation: Develop an Alliance Digital Interoperability Framework, underpinned by a coherent Interoperability roadmap to: establish cross-organisational relationships, streamline processes supporting end-to-end ICT services, to ensure that both existing and new policies do not affect interoperability effort. A modern API strategy is a critical component the framework.

elements.name: Enable automation in standards interoperability
elements.documentation:
Where appropriate, transition towards adoption/ creation of machine readable standards, in-line with AAP-32, to enhance cooperation with industry by standards. Such standards shall be available in a format that is easily processed by a computer thus minimizing ambiguities and increase efficiencies in the development and automated testing of compliant capabilities.
An initial plan to transition their standards will be submitted by all TAs to the Committee for Standardisation by 2QR25, and annually updated to inform DT activities

elements.name: Review and modernise standards portfolio, maximising adoption and co-development of civil standards, where feasible
elements.documentation:
Promote adoption of standards developed by civil standards developing organizations to modernize aging standards portfolios, improve technical quality of standards, reducing the time to develop new standards and enhance engagement with Industry. Contribute to the development of suitable civil standards to ensure their usability in a military context.
An update on adoptions and co-development to be submitted to the Committee for Standardization by 4Q24, and annually updated to inform DT activities.

elements.name: Ensure interoperability requirements captured in NDPP
elements.documentation: Continue to include Digital Transformation considerations and interoperability requirements within the NDPP in order to ensure interoperability across all domains, and between NATO and Allies’ C3 systems and services.

elements.name: Ensure that innovation projects’ initiation processes address agreed military and business requirements
elements.documentation:
Ensure that project initiation processes and projects that drive innovation, concept development and experimentation are addressing formally recognized and agreed military and business shortfalls.
This would include establishing formal processes (e.g. Directives) to ensure mapping of innovation projects to recognised requirements.

elements.name: Capability Requirements and Capability Programme Planning development process rethink and improvements
elements.documentation: Accelerate the capability requirements development and capability programme planning phases of (PO(2018)0259), for example by leveraging the dynamic sourcing process piloted by OCIO (PO(2022)0417 (INV)). This will enable iterative alignment of operational requirements with available state-of-the-art technology, and refinement of capability requirements to offer the right options-based approach to the sourcing strategy for a fit-for-purpose technical solution, based on industry best practice

elements.name: Obsolescence Management process improvements
elements.documentation: Develop an efficient framework for effectively managing obsolescence of ICT Services, throughout their useful life.

elements.name: Security accreditation regulatory documents and processes review and updates to enable timely adoption of modern technologies, as necessary
elements.documentation: Review and propose updates, where necessary, to the security accreditation regulatory documents and processes to accommodate a timely adoption of modern technologies and approaches, such as EDTs and DevSecOps, following the zero-trust paradigm and “security by design” principle.

elements.name: Establish coherent NATO Enterprise IT Service management processes
elements.documentation: Establish processes to ascertain coherent IT Service management for the NATO Enterprise.

elements.name: Modernize the procurement rules and acquisition processes
elements.documentation: Review and propose modernisation updates, where necessary, to the procurement rules and acquisition processes, so that they can facilitate delivery of military requirements better, faster and smarter to meet ambitious operational requirements in the most timely, efficient and effective way. Based on future Council decision, this should provide detailed proposals for specific revisions to the extant procurement rules and regulations to support the findings and conclusions from AC/335-D(2023)0014 (INV).

elements.name: Standardize and digitise workforce processes
elements.documentation:
Standardize and digitise workforce processes across NATO Enterprise, with initial focus on the NCS
 1. Review the current custom local workforce procedures in order to develop standard BiSC-wide procedures instead, which can be digitized.
 2. Identify/develop the list of workforce processing activities.
 3. Review the related local implementations and develop a single BiSC-wide standard process flow.
 4. Implement the digitized enhanced processes.
 5. Make workforce data available within a single centralized NATO-wide Data Warehouse

elements.name: Establish an Alliance-wide secure and flexible digital network infrastructure
elements.documentation: Establish an Alliance-wide secure and flexible digital network infrastructure to support military operations, and enable efficient interconnection with national network segments thus formulating an Allied core network, by adhering to static (as per Core Comms Capability Programme Plan - CPP) and deployable Protected Core Networking (PCN) NATO Standards.

elements.name: Promote Alliance Federation Core and Community of Interest Services (AFS)
elements.documentation:
Promote Alliance Federation Core and Community of Interest Services (AFS) by: 
 1. Consolidating the federation of those services between NATO Enterprise, Allies and relevant multi-national entities, acting as Alliance Federation participants.
 2. Developing a framework (addressing governance and management, security, standardization and financial aspects) that enables provision of Communications, Core and Community of Interest services from all Federation participants.
 3. Establishing a common approach for Allies to make their ICT services available to NATO and the NATO Enterprise, integrate them end-to-end, manage their information and data sources, as well as security and data protection requirements.

elements.name: Design an Alliance-wide architecture for the Digital Backbone
elements.documentation:
Design an Alliance-wide architecture for the Digital Backbone to inform the development of MDO-enabled command and control services and political consultation that can exchange and share relevant information from the strategic, to operational and tactical, including for nuclear consultation, command and control, levels.
This should consider the following:
 1. modernized security architectures for ICT services/ software applications adopting the Zero-Trust Architecture paradigm, to complement the existing ‘security measures, promoting security by design and streamlining cybersecurity accreditation; the initially anticipated delivery is a respective architecture by NIAG
 2. align with the Alliance federated data fabric architecture and NATO Enterprise cloud-based data architecture being developed as part of the DEF Strategic Plan (PO(2022)0409 (INV)), which should consider a smart data fabric architecture that speeds and simplifies secure access to data across the entire organization;
 3. a federated data fabric architecture and set of data services that provide consistent capabilities across a choice of endpoints spanning across NATO Enterprise and, through FMN, the federation, including gateways to industry. The NATO Data Fabric will standardize data management practices and practicalities across cloud, on premises, and edge devices.
 4. conceptual architecture for federated search across systems that facilitate information exchange across the Alliance, which ACO will be developing by 4QR23.
 5. the integration of sensors (such as AGS, E3-A, AFSC, Surveillance from Space etc.) into the Digital Backbone.

elements.name: Deliver an Alliance secure and scalable cloud environment
elements.documentation:
Deliver an Alliance secure and scalable cloud environment, across multiple confidentiality classifications, to serve as the foundation of the Digital Backbone and to enable MDO. These should include provision of:
 1. Cloud-based innovation platforms, e.g. NATO Software Factory, to develop/ adopt/ co-create with Industry future capabilities in big data, advanced analytics, and modern software development practices (DevSecOps). Foster innovation through enabling outsourced/insourced – as appropriate – development of cloud native applications that can be rapidly deployed to the warfighters.
 2. Federation of cloud services, combined with edge computing that enable resilient, scalable, secure ICT service continuum to connect sensors, actors and effectors, across all domains and multiple Joint Operational Areas, forming an Alliance Combat Cloud, as described in NIAG-D(2022)0003.
 3. Support to the Alliance Data Sharing Ecosystem, enabling the storage, registration, and management of quality data and data pipelines (DataOps). This cloud-based platform will also require value-adding data and AI tools and services to its members, e.g. for accessing, labelling, managing, visualising, and exploiting data.
 4. Accelerated delivery of the NATO Enterprise Cloud solutions per the Digital Transformation Action Plan and Protected Business Network Capability Programme Plan.
 5. Exploration and development of trusted Big Data anonymization/ tokenisation technology allowing for sensitive data to be processed in Commercial Cloud environments, acting as a gap filler for the implementation of Data Centric Security
 6. Revised regulatory documents to enable multi-tenancy to host different security COI on the same infrastructure

elements.name: Develop CIS survivability and resilience requirements
elements.documentation: Develop CIS survivability and resilience requirements based on the PACE (Primary, Alternate, Contingency, Emergency) principles, in accordance with DDA-led emphasis on mission assurance and with the specific requirements for nuclear consultation, command and control.

elements.name: Develop a Strategy for C3B Next Generation Networks
elements.documentation: As per PO(2023)0057 (INV), the C3B will develop the C3B Next Generation Networks Strategy to address how ubiquitous wireless technologies will be adopted at the strategic and operational levels both across Enterprise and in theatre.

elements.name: Develop a concept for an Alliance Data Sharing Ecosystem
elements.documentation:
Allies will develop a concept for an Alliance data sharing ecosystem for defence and security, with an initial focus on selected thematic areas, e.g. geospatial and location data, climate, information environment assessment, energy, space or maritime data, at the non-classified level, guided by the data requirements to support the Alliance priority use cases in NATO’s AI Strategy and Data Exploitation Framework Strategic Plan.
Through this ecosystem, Allies will establish a data sharing imperative and will share data in selected policy areas, in line with NATO’s Principles of Responsible Use. The concept will enable data from Allies, the NATO Enterprise and Allied industry and research partners to be shared, collaboratively labelled, connected, and exploited.
The concept should leverage existing efforts under NATO’s AI Strategy and Data Exploitation Framework Policy, support the MDO concept, and link to related strategic deliverables, such as Data-centric Governance and Alliance Cloud environment activities.

elements.name: Establish a programme and deliver an initial capability for an Alliance data sharing ecosystem
elements.documentation:
The leads will establish an Alliance governance programme and coordinate the implementation of the Alliance data sharing ecosystem concept, and will deliver an IOC by 2QR25.
This will include, at a minimum:
 1. Communities of interest comprising interested Allies, NATO Enterprise stakeholders, relevant companies from the civilian and defence sectors, and academia, whom will commit to sharing data, collaborative labelling of data, connecting and exploiting data, ensuring at any time that these are in line with NATO Principles of Responsible Use of AI and data.
 2. Access to a cloud-based data platform, preferably part of the Alliance secure and scalable cloud environment;
 3. Processes and frameworks to foster cooperation, trusted use and sharing of data in the ecosystem. These could include data sharing processes, agreements and data use conditions to facilitate the use of data within different security and sensitivity domains. This will be facilitated with the delivery of various LoEs of the DEF Strategic Plan, and should clearly communicate data ownership and custodianship roles and responsibilities, as per agreed policy areas.
 4. Programme to enable delivery of the ecosystem.

elements.name: Accelerate innovative uses for data exploitation and AI, whilst leveraging on and contributing to the Alliance Data Sharing Ecosystem
elements.documentation: Guide and connect data exploitation and AI communities to explore innovative ways in which the data can be exploited as an asset, at scale and speed, using advanced analytics, AI and other game-changing technologies. The goal will be to establish an Alliance capacity to find innovative uses of data exploitation and AI, and rapidly integrate them into Alliance capabilities. Initial concept to be established by 2QR25, with annual reports to the lead on progress.

elements.name: Leverage ACT’s Framework For Collaborative Interaction (FFCI) with Industry and Academia
elements.documentation: Increase cooperation with Industry and Academia through ACT’s FFCI to exchange views related to Digital Transformation, explore concepts, conduct experiments and support research and acquisition processes of new technologies to reduce risk and increase cost-effectiveness of capability development efforts

elements.documentation: NATO needs a coherent approach to collaboration with the private sector – including on rapid employment of professionals and services to augment workforce at any NATO body, mapping and developing technology and rapid fielding of technologies. NATO should benefit from a unified Framework Contract pathway designed to address different technology verticals, in areas ranging from data science and AI through to access to space-based ISR services and uncrewed systems. This pathway should be usable across all NATO entities and funding sources.

elements.name: Policy Enforcement Point Services
elements.documentation: The Policy Enforcement Point (PEP) Services protect other services by providing a logical entry point that serves as an intermediary between a call from a service consumer to a service provider. The PEP can either be deployed as a separate device or appliance that sits between the consumer and provider, or as an inline component that is deployed as part of the container infrastructure of the service. The PEP validates the structure of the message, including the digital signature, and the credentials that are provided with the message. This provides a common mechanism to extract and pass on identity information from the service consumer to the service provider so that an Authorization decision can be made, either locally or through the use of a Policy Decision Point (PDP).

elements.name: Data Format Transformation Services
elements.documentation:
The Data Format Transformation Services support the encoding of information in different formats. This is needed when information consumers cannot directly process the information in the format chosen by the information provider. Data Format Transformation Services also play a role when the boundary between one network type to another is crossed (e.g. static IP network to tactical radio network) and a conversion from one data representation to another (e.g. for bandwidth utilization purposes) is required.

The relation between the data and the information which it represents can be changed during a data format transformation. To this regard important aspects of data transformation include format conversion where data is encoded differently using another format. Both data encodings represent the same information and are usually compatible. Typical examples are the conversion of temperature from Celsius to Fahrenheit, or the conversion of the bit representation between Big- and Little-Endian formats.

Not all information can always be preserved during the data transformation (lossy). Reasons can be that some information is deliberately omitted and not captured in the new data (e.g. when compressing data), or that the target format is not capable of representing data in a way that the original information is completely preserved.

elements.name: Information Labeling Services
elements.documentation:
The Information Labeling Services provide functionality required to apply metadata to an information object for the purpose of creating a label or mapping labels of "foreign" information security and management policies.

Labeling facilitates the determination of the protection requirement for an information object, the release of an information object, or the determination of the mission value of an information object, as captured by release and protection policies defined for each metadata entry.

elements.name: Batch Processing (ETL/CDC)
elements.documentation: Extract, transform, and load (ETL) is a data pipeline used to collect data from various sources. It then transforms the data according to business rules, and it loads the data into a destination data store. Implementing ETL Pipeline architecture with the Database Replication property can speed up data processing. Change Data Capture (CDC) is one of the most popular methods of carrying out this Data Replication process. CDC is a data integration capability available with most major databases. It monitors, identifies and captures changes to stored data and delivers them to external systems, commonly for replication. Changes are captured in a transaction log stored in the database and typically include adding, updating and deleting the contents of rows, and modifying the schema of a table.

elements.name: Policy-based Access Control
elements.documentation:
Policy-Based Access Control (PBAC) is a type of access control that defines and enforces security policies. It can be defined as a strategy to manage user access to systems, fusing the business roles of users with policies that define the access privileges they should have. Simply put, Policy-Based Access Control (PBAC) is a security model that determines which users are allowed to access which resources.
In a PBAC system, each type of user is assigned a set of policies that define what they are allowed to do. When they attempt to access a resource, the system checks the policies to see if they are allowed to do so. If the user’s policies allow them to access said resource, they are granted access; if not, access is denied.
PBAC can be used to control access to any type of resource, including files, databases, applications, and network devices. It is often used in conjunction with other security models, such as Role-based Access Control (RBAC), to provide a more comprehensive security solution.
However, unlike RBAC, PBAC enables organizations to rapidly change privileges based on new regulations or new corporate policies without auditing and changing roles throughout the organization. This ensures assets cannot be compromised and regulations are met.

elements.name: Authentication Services
elements.documentation:
The Authentication Services provide functionality to verify that a claimed identity is genuine and based on valid credentials. Authentication typically leads to a mutually shared level of assurance by the relying parties in the identity. Authentication may occur through a variety of mechanisms including challenge/response, time-based code sequences, biometric comparison, PKI or other techniques.

The Authentication Services provide also functionality required to manage trust relationships between organizations and/or within an organization to enable access to electronic assets across boundaries of entity's governance realms and/or information domains.

elements.name: Digital Identity Services
elements.documentation: The Digital Identity Services comprise the services required to capture and validate information to uniquely identify an individual, determine suitability, and create and manage a digital identity over the life cycle. Digital identity is the representation of identity in a digital environment.

elements.name: Digital Certificate Services
elements.documentation: The Digital Certificate Services provide functionality required to create, manage, distribute, use, store, suspend, resume and revoke digital certificates. It provides a trust framework across organizational, operational, physical, and network boundaries, required to enable the services that rely on digital certificates.

elements.name: Authorization and Access Control Services
elements.documentation:
The Authorization and Access Control Services provide functionality to grant or deny access to information processing services, data and physical facilities. They enforce information security and management policies by ensuring individuals only access those resources they are entitled to use and then only for approved purposes.

The request for access includes the resource and the type of desired access, e.g. reading, writing, opening. The authorization decision is based on the access control rule sets, resulting from privilege management, taking into account the level of assurance of entity's identity determined by the utilized authentication mechanism.

elements.name: NATO Digital Backbone
elements.documentation: The NATO Digital Backbone provides a federated connectivity and physical distribution framework that enables resilient, scalable, and secure digital services across the Alliance. It facilitates seamless data exchange by interconnecting sensors, decision-makers, actors, and effectors across organizational, national, operational, and security boundaries. Realizing logical paths between nodes, it aggregates sub-networks, facilities, and equipment, including cloud and edge services, and supports seamless communication, service delivery, and operational continuity. Adhering to allied governance, security, and interoperability standards, it provides the technical foundation for mission-critical collaboration, scalable C2, data and machine learning sharing ecosystems during peacetime, crisis, and conflict.

Data-Centric Interoperability Concepts and Design Requirements
elements.documentation: This publication provides a comprehensive guide to achieve secure data-centric interoperability by leveraging Data-Centric Security (DCS) alongside Zero Trust and federated identity frameworks. It documents multinational lessons learned to support the transition from legacy network-centric systems to modern data-centric implementations, detailing the required architectures, policies, and technical mechanisms—such as Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC) and a dedicated Access Control Framework (ACF)—for ensuring data confidentiality, integrity, and availability. The document introduces the Zero Trust Data Format (ZTDF) for secure object encoding and outlines a structured approach to enabling interoperability across various operational tiers and national systems, while also anticipating updates to integrate emerging technologies and standards for Multi-Level Security (MLS) in Multi-Domain Operations (MDO).

elements.name: Attribute-Based Access Control Policy Expression Language
Data Product
elements.documentation:
A standardized, self-contained unit of automated data sharing that bundles datasets and/or data services with metadata, usage policies, and delivery mechanisms, enabling it to be discovered, accessed, and consumed within a data ecosystem. A Data Product may also be produced by aggregating two or more existing Data Products. 
Governed by a dedicated Data Product Team, it operates autonomously, ensuring data sovereignty, compliance, and usability for consumers via self-service interfaces such as APIs or portals. Designed to provide actionable insights, a data product facilitates trusted transactions and interoperability, delivering value through informed decision-making and process optimization.

elements.name: Subject
elements.documentation:
The subject is most often assumed to be a person. A non-person entity (NPE), such as an autonomous 
service or application could also fill the role of the subject. In general, every operation performed by a 
computer must be done on behalf of some person or organization (in the case of an NPE) with the authority
to perform the operation. The term subject is used to denote a human or NPE requesting access to a resource.

elements.name: Policy Decision Point Services
elements.documentation: The Policy Decision Point (PDP) Services provide authorization decisions by evaluating Digital Policies (DP) and and Meta-Policies (MP) against the attributes of an authorization request. The request can contain attributes about the subject of the request (the service consumer), the object of the request (the resource that is being accessed), the action that is being performed and other attributes not related to the subject or resource (the "environment"). A decision is returned to the requesting entity, which can contain further obligations about how the request is to be treated. The PDP can be collocated with a Policy Enforcement Point (PEP) to improve performance. One of the main functions of the PDP is to mediate or deconflict DPs according to MPs.

elements.name: Policy Information Point Services
elements.documentation:
Policy Information Point (PIP) Services are collecting, aggregating, and providing relevant attribute and contextual data required for making policy decisions. The PIP service acts as an intermediary, connecting to multiple data sources to retrieve attributes about users, devices, resources, and environmental conditions. These attributes support fine-grained access control decisions based on up-to-date and context-aware information, allowing the PDP to enforce Zero Trust principles accurately. The PIP service can pull information in real time, cache it for performance optimization, and deliver it to the PDP upon request. Examples of attributes might include user roles, device compliance status, threat intelligence, and location data, among others. 
The Policy Information Point (PIP) Services play a vital role in ensuring that policy decisions are made based on accurate, up-to-date, and context-aware information. It aligns with the Zero Trust model by supplying the PDP with necessary attributes for enforcing fine-grained and dynamic access controls. Given the increasing demand for real-time and context-driven authorization, the PIP service is a crucial component for ensuring comprehensive and reliable access control.

Key Features:
* '''Attribute Collection and Aggregation''': Collects attributes from diverse sources, such as identity management systems, device management platforms, threat intelligence feeds, and environmental data providers. Aggregates and normalizes data to create a standardized, unified view of attributes, which simplifies access by the PDP.
* '''Real-Time Awareness''': Provides real-time or near-real-time access to dynamic attributes, allowing policy decisions to adapt to the current security context (e.g., recent threat intelligence updates, current device state). Enhances decision accuracy by ensuring PDP has access to the most current information, which is essential for adapting to a changing security landscape.
* '''Performance Optimization''': Implements caching mechanisms for frequently accessed attributes or low-variance data to reduce latency and improve response times to PDP requests. Configures retention policies to maintain data freshness while balancing system performance.
* '''Attribute Verification and Data Integrity''': Ensures data accuracy and integrity by applying verification checks, especially for high-assurance attributes like user identity and device compliance. Maintains security controls over attribute data to prevent tampering and unauthorized access, thereby supporting a reliable decision-making process.
* '''Data Source Integration''': Integrates with a variety of data sources using standardized protocols and APIs, ensuring compatibility with commonly used identity providers, device management platforms, and security intelligence feeds. Supports interoperability across cloud, on-premises, and hybrid environments, ensuring that the PIP can operate seamlessly within a wide range of IT infrastructures.
* '''Dynamic Attribute Filtering''': Provides filtering options to supply only relevant attributes for each policy decision request, avoiding unnecessary data transfer and optimizing PDP processing. Enables customized data handling to align with different access policies, supporting complex, context-sensitive access control models.
* '''Access Auditing and Logging''': Maintains logs of attribute requests and data transfers to provide an audit trail for policy evaluation and compliance. Supports anomaly detection and forensic investigation by tracking access patterns and data usage.
* '''Scalability and High Availability''': Designed to support high availability and redundancy, ensuring continuous access to attributes even in distributed or cloud-based environments. Must scale with the growth of the organization, maintaining performance and reliability as the volume of attributes and decision requests increases.

In a Zero Trust environment, the Policy Information Point (PIP) interacts with other Zero Trust components as follows:
* Policy Decision Point (PDP): The PIP is queried by the PDP whenever a policy decision requires specific attributes, providing context that the PDP uses to evaluate authorization rules.
* Policy Enforcement Point (PEP): The PEP enforces decisions made by the PDP, which are based on the context provided by the PIP.
* Data Sources: The PIP retrieves data from various sources, such as identity management systems, security intelligence services, and environmental monitors, ensuring that PDP decisions are fully informed.

elements.name: Policy Administration Point Services
elements.documentation: The Policy Administration Point (PAP) Services provide functionality required to compose, modify, manage, and control access control policies in a standard policy exchange format, enabling the policy enforcement through the Policy Enforcement (PEP) and Policy Decision Point (PDP) components.

elements.name: Identity, Credentials and Access Management Services
elements.documentation: Identity, Credentials and Access Management Services

elements.name: Multi-Factor Authentication Services
elements.documentation:
Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) Services enables organizations to add multiple verification layers, requiring users to authenticate through a combination of credentials. Typically, these include something the user knows (password), something the user has (OTP, smartphone), and something the user is (biometric data). MFA services manage these factors via an MFA server or gateway that coordinates verification across connected systems. By layering authentication, MFA significantly reduces the risk of unauthorized access and is commonly used in corporate networks, financial services, and applications requiring strong security. These services also allow for adaptive policies, integrating easily with existing IAM platforms to enforce MFA selectively based on user or device context.

Key Features:
* Supports various MFA factors, including SMS, OTP, app-based codes, and biometrics.
* Centralized management of MFA policies and factor preferences.
* Adaptive enforcement, allowing increased security based on context and risk level.
* Compatibility with multiple Identity and Access Management solutions for integration into enterprise environments.
* Real-time monitoring and logging of authentication events for security compliance.

elements.name: Certificate-Based Authentication Services
elements.documentation:
Certificate-Based Authentication Services leverage digital certificates as a secure and verified credential for authenticating users, devices, or applications. By using certificates issued by a Certificate Authority (CA) within a Public Key Infrastructure (PKI), this service ensures that only verified entities can access sensitive resources. Digital certificates contain a public key and a digital signature from the CA, and when a user attempts to log in, the service verifies the certificate's authenticity. Widely used in enterprise environments and B2B communications, this service enables secure, certificate-based access without the need for passwords.

Key Features:
* Integrates with PKI to verify identities with cryptographic keys.
* Supports device-based authentication for IoT and remote access.
* Enforces strong cryptographic standards for secure communications.
* Suitable for high-assurance access to sensitive applications and networks.

elements.name: Digital Certificate Issuance Services
elements.documentation:
Digital Certificate Issuance Services provide the capability to create and distribute digital certificates, which are electronic credentials that bind an entity's public key to its identity. These certificates are issued by a trusted Certificate Authority (CA) after verifying the identity of the requesting party. The issuance process follows a strict workflow, ensuring compliance with defined policies and standards, such as X.509 or other applicable PKI standards.

Key Features:
*Identity Verification: Verifies the identity of the certificate requester (e.g., organization, individual, or device).
*Certificate Generation: Issues X.509 digital certificates, binding public keys with verified identities.
*Policy Compliance: Ensures certificates comply with security and operational policies.
*Secure Delivery: Distributes certificates securely to requesters, minimizing risk of interception.

elements.name: Digital Certificate Validation Services
elements.documentation:
Digital Certificate Validation Service ensure the validity and authenticity of certificates used in secure communications or transactions. Digital Certificate Validation Services provide mechanisms to confirm the authenticity and validity of a digital certificate in real-time. These services ensure that the certificate has been issued by a trusted Certification Authority (CA) and has not been revoked or expired. Validation services commonly use formats and protocols like ITU-T Recommendation X.509, X.509 Internet PKI Online Certificate Status Protocol (OCSP) or Internet X.509 PKI Certificate and Certificate Revocation List (CRL) Profile for this purpose.

Key Features
* Real-Time Validation: Verifies certificates through OCSP or CRL checks.
* Trust Chain Verification: Ensures the certificate is issued by a trusted CA and complies with the chain of trust.
* Revocation Status: Confirms that the certificate is not revoked.
* Integration with Applications: Provides APIs or plugins for seamless integration with email, web browsers, or other secure applications.

elements.name: Digital Certificate Revocation Services
elements.documentation:
Digital Certificate Validation Service ensure the validity and authenticity of certificates used in secure communications or transactions. Digital Certificate Validation Services provide mechanisms to confirm the authenticity and validity of a digital certificate in real-time. These services ensure that the certificate has been issued by a trusted Certification Authority (CA) and has not been revoked or expired. Validation services commonly use formats and protocols like ITU-T Recommendation X.509, X.509 Internet PKI Online Certificate Status Protocol (OCSP) or Internet X.509 PKI Certificate and Certificate Revocation List (CRL) Profile for this purpose.

Key Features
* Real-Time Validation: Verifies certificates through OCSP or CRL checks.
* Trust Chain Verification: Ensures the certificate is issued by a trusted CA and complies with the chain of trust.
* Revocation Status: Confirms that the certificate is not revoked.
* Integration with Applications: Provides APIs or plugins for seamless integration with email, web browsers, or other secure applications.

elements.name: Identity and Access Management Applications
elements.documentation: The Identity and Access Management Applications enable users to manage identities, credentials, and access control privileges.

elements.name: Messaging PEP Service
elements.documentation:
The Messaging-PEP provides access and release control for:
• User applications requesting information from a PPS, and
• PPS pushing information to a user application or middleware.
The Messaging-PEP authorizes release and receipt based on the users (sender and receiver) privileges and current user specified policy. On receipt, the Messaging-PEP verifies that the local PPS and data-store(s) are authorized to access, process and store the contents of the Message.  On Release, the Messaging-PEP ensures that each recipient to the message is authorized to receive the contents of the message, and that the specific communication mechanism (e.g., topic, queue, communication channel) is authorized to transport the contents of the message.
[Source: https://www.omg.org/spec/IEF-RA/1.0/]

elements.name: E-mail PEP Service
elements.documentation:
The E-mail PEP operates between the e-mail client and the Mail server. The PEP intercepts each request, determines if the user is authorized to perform the requested action given sensitivities of the information assets (email body and each attachment) involved. 
[Source: https://www.omg.org/spec/IEF-RA/1.0/]

elements.name: File PEP Service
elements.documentation:
The File-PEP operates between a user application and the file server.  The PEP intercepts each request, determines if the user is authorized to perform the requested action given sensitivities of each file.
[Source: https://www.omg.org/spec/IEF-RA/1.0/]

elements.name: IM PEP Service
elements.documentation:
The IM-PEP provides features that intercept all interactions between  the users’ Instant or Text Massaging client and the Instant Messaging (IM) server. The PEP intercepts each interaction between an IM client and server to determine if the user (sender and receiver) has the appropriate privileges (/authorizations) to perform the requested action.
[Source: https://www.omg.org/spec/IEF-RA/1.0/]

elements.name: Technology Interaction
Attribute data
Secure data exploitation at speed and scale
elements.documentation: Securely exploit data at speed and scale

elements.name: Linked data across NATO
elements.documentation: The ability of NATO to connect and securely exploit data at speed and scale, institutionalizing and facilitating the sharing2 of data and models to support Allied efforts, including, but not limited to, developing AI applications and AI-enabled capabilities.

elements.name: Persistently connected Alliance
elements.documentation: The ability of NATO to connect and securely exploit data at speed and scale, institutionalizing and facilitating the sharing2 of data and models to support Allied efforts, including, but not limited to, developing AI applications and AI-enabled capabilities.

elements.name: Institutionalized data sharing
elements.documentation: The ability of NATO to connect and securely exploit data at speed and scale, institutionalizing and facilitating the sharing2 of data and models to support Allied efforts, including, but not limited to, developing AI applications and AI-enabled capabilities.

elements.name: Institutionalized AI/ML model sharing
elements.documentation: The ability of NATO to connect and securely exploit data at speed and scale, institutionalizing and facilitating the sharing2 of data and models to support Allied efforts, including, but not limited to, developing AI applications and AI-enabled capabilities.

elements.name: Data Sovereignty
elements.documentation: Ensures that organizations retain control over their data.

elements.name: Policy based data sharing
elements.documentation: Defines rules and policies for data sharing.

elements.name: Establish an Alliance Data Sharing Ecosystem
elements.documentation: Data sharing is an imperative for the Alliance that should be pursued through systematic efforts. Sharing interoperable data to achieve Allied objectives shall become the norm and a shared responsibility for Allies and the NATO Enterprise alike. Initiatives to share data with Allied Industry and research partners will be encouraged and strengthened, as appropriate. ...  An Alliance Data Sharing Ecosystem will be established, where data from Allies, the NATO Enterprise, and Industry and research partners, as appropriate, is shared, labelled, connected and exploited.

elements.name: Provide a NATO Model and Data Sharing Hub
elements.documentation: A Model and Data Sharing Hub is a centralized platform for managing and sharing data and AI/ML models within a collaborative data and services environment. It serves as a repository for cataloging, managing, and accessing datasets and models. The hub provides data analytics and AI development services while linking different data spaces through the digital backbone. It promotes interoperability by implementing metadata standards, harmonizing community-specific standards, and supporting cross-security domain information sharing. Oversight and implementation involve collaboration among all participating organizations.

elements.name: Enable (federated) Data Mesh implementations within the Alliance
elements.documentation:
The Alliance is composed of missions and enterprises with many data types. These include both structured and unstructured data, such as relational tables in databases, Extensible Markup Language (XML), spreadsheets, images, videos, audios, documents, and more. To advance Alliance data interoperability, a modernized data management architectural design is required—one that relies on a set of technical capabilities based on clear data standards and governance to enable secure Alliance-wide discovery and exploitation of shareable data. Leveraging the data mesh approach does not require wholesale disruption to legacy architectures.
