Data mesh has many advantages for enabling a decentralized architecture that, combined with Zero Trust Architecture (ZTA) and NATO's Digital Backbone, will allow Alliance elements to maintain secure control of their data while sharing without duplication.

elements.name: Enable Data Space implementations with external entities
elements.documentation: Users and applications will migrate from maintaining private data (e.g., data kept within system specific storage) to making data available in shared data spaces. These shared spaces will provide repositories where users and applications can submit, or post, data assets to the communities of in space. The shared spaces will provide storage and management services. Data spaces of the ADSE will be maintained, secured, and staged as necessary to support NATO’s missions. Data that is posted to shared spaces will be advertised via its associated metadata and will be discoverable across the Alliance.

elements.name: Data Space
elements.documentation:
A distributed system defined by a governance framework that enables secure and trustworthy data transactions between participants that don't have existing trust relationships while supporting data sovereignty. A data space is implemented by a set of technical services that facilitate the sharing of interoperable Data Products.

A NATO Dataspace is a data space leveraging existing NATO policies, standards and technologies, as well as governance models to facilitate secure and standardized data exchange and data linkage within a trusted Alliance Data Sharing Ecosystem (ADSE). It thereby provides a basis for creating smart-service scenarios and facilitating innovative inter-organizational / multinational business processes, while at the same time guaranteeing data sovereignty for data owners. 
Sharing data between autonomous entities requires the provision of metadata to facilitate the transfer of Data Products by making use of a data transfer (or application layer) protocol.  Data sovereignty is a central aspect of NATO Data Spaces so that Information/Data owners can determine the handling rules on how their data and models might be accessed and used by other entities, e.g. within or across data spaces.

elements.name: Data Mesh
elements.documentation:
A NATO Data Mesh is a federatable data management architecture that enables secure and interoperable data sharing across the Alliance’s diverse enterprises, operational domains, and functional areas. By decentralizing data ownership, it empowers NATO entities to manage their data as products while adhering to shared governance and standards for interoperability. It integrates structured and unstructured data types—including databases, XML datasets, multimedia, and documents—through metadata-driven discoverability and semantic alignment using NATO agreed standards. This ensures that data can be securely discovered, accessed, and used across the Alliance.

The architecture minimizes disruption to legacy systems by integrating them through adapters and connectors aligned with data mesh principles, maintaining compatibility while modernizing capabilities. Federation is supported through distributed data catalogs, standardized policies, and a set of standardized APIs. By combining decentralized management, shared governance, and interoperability, this capability advances the Alliance's operational readiness and ability for exploiting data for decsion making.

elements.name: Data Labelling
elements.documentation:
Data labelling capabilities/facilities are specialized systems or environments integral to the AI and ML model development lifecycle. These capabilities/facilities are designed to generate high-quality labeled datasets, which are crucial for training, validating, and testing machine learning algorithms. They span diverse workflows, integrating human expertise and automated technologies to ensure efficiency, accuracy, and scalability. Supporting data across various modalities such as text, images, videos, audio, and sensor data, these facilities apply metadata or other annotations to curate datasets necessary for supervised and semi-supervised learning. Depending on the security classification of the data, labeling operations can occur in secure physical labs or cloud-based environments, employing advanced encryption, anonymization, and access control mechanisms to protect sensitive information.
The architecture of data labelling facilities integrates automation, human labor, and quality control to maintain accuracy and consistency. Automation through pre-labeling algorithms and active learning accelerates the process, while human involvement remains critical for tasks requiring contextual understanding or subjective judgment. In-house teams often ensure higher security and compliance for sensitive projects. These capabilities/facilities also seamlessly connect with broader AI/ML pipelines, leveraging feedback from training models to refine labeling priorities and enhance data quality iteratively. By combining robust infrastructure, advanced technologies, and skilled human intervention, data labeling capabilites/facilities enable organizations to develop high-quality datasets essential for building and refining cutting-edge AI and ML solutions.

elements.name: Data can be protected across a federation
Data available for exploitation outside the originating domain
Data can be shared securely between the Alliance and external entities
elements.documentation: Enable secure and sovereign data exchange between entities that do not have pre-established trust or federated identity management by creating a decentralized ecosystem, ensuring data sovereignty while fostering interoperability and compliance with shared rules and standards.

elements.name: Annotated Data is available for AI/ML training
elements.documentation: high-quality, annotated datasets tailored for training, validating, and testing machine learning (ML) models. These datasets serve as the foundation for supervised learning, semi-supervised learning, and in some cases, for fine-tuning pre-trained models.

elements.name: Data Architecting
elements.documentation:
The Data Architecture Management Capability provides the necessary framework, tools, and processes to design, implement, and maintain the organization's data architecture. It aligns the data architecture with organizational objectives, ensuring consistency, scalability, and adaptability to changing business needs. This capability ensures data is structured, secure, consistent, and accessible across the organization while aligning with regulatory and compliance requirements.

Key Objectives
* Ensure data architecture aligns with enterprise strategy and business goals.
* Establish and maintain reference models for data and information flow.
* Enable interoperability and integration of data across systems and platforms.
* Govern the design, storage, and usage of data assets to maintain quality and compliance.

Outcomes
* Coherent, standardized, and interoperable data models.
* Optimized data flows and integration across the organization.
* Increased agility in responding to business and regulatory changes.
* A data architecture that supports innovation and data-driven decision-making.

elements.name: Architecture Enablement
elements.documentation:
Enablement Processes ensure that the supporting capabilities needed to perform the architecture activities in the other architecture processes are available when and where necessary. Architecture enablement processes include the following activities:
* Prepare for and plan the architecture enablement effort
* Monitor, assess and control the architecture enablement activities
* Manage the architecture process enablers
* Acquire, develop and establish enabling capabilities, services and resources
* Deploy enabling capabilities, services and resources
* Improve architecture enablement capabilities, services and resources

elements.name: Data Quality Architecture Support Service
elements.documentation: The Data Quality Architecture Support Service ensures that data quality principles are embedded into the organization's data architecture to support Data Quality Management (DQM) processes. This service evaluates and enhances architectural components to align with quality standards, facilitates the integration of data quality tools and automation, and provides design guidance for quality-centric workflows. It promotes interoperability, governance, and continuous improvement, enabling scalable and sustainable DQM practices. By aligning technical architecture with business objectives, this service mitigates risks, enhances data reliability, and supports data-driven decision-making.

elements.name: Data Architecture Conceptualization
elements.documentation: The Data Architecture Conceptualization process involves systematically exploring and defining architectural options within the data domain. It aims to establish a comprehensive understanding of the problem and solution space, formulate candidate architectures, and provide a conceptual framework to guide implementation. The process ensures that the resulting data architecture aligns with business goals, integrates effectively with other architectures, and addresses stakeholder needs.

elements.name: Business Analyst
elements.documentation: Gather business requirements and ensure alignment with data architecture. Business Analysts translate organizational objectives and business requirements into data needs.

elements.name: Data Architecture Description
elements.documentation: The Data Architecture Description Process involves systematically defining, documenting, and communicating the data architecture to ensure it aligns with stakeholder needs, business goals, and technical requirements. This process produces detailed models, views, and documentation that articulate the structure, relationships, and rationale of the data architecture, serving as a bridge to downstream design and implementation activities.

elements.name: Data Architecture Management
elements.documentation:
The Data Architecture Management Process ensures the effective planning, execution, monitoring, and governance of data architectures throughout their lifecycle, maintaining alignment with enterprise strategies while adhering to governance standards and stakeholder expectations. It begins by establishing the organizational capability to oversee data architecture management, defining the processes, tools, and roles necessary for sustained oversight. This capability forms the foundation for developing comprehensive management plans that align with enterprise governance frameworks and address critical objectives, such as ensuring data scalability, security, and compliance.

Guidance and direction are then provided to architectural activities, promoting adherence to foundational principles like treating data as a strategic asset and ensuring its interoperability and reliability. Continuous oversight is maintained by monitoring progress and assessing data architecture initiatives against key performance indicators, governance directives, and organizational goals. As issues or deviations are identified, corrective measures are implemented to address gaps or inefficiencies, with the architecture iteratively refined to remain responsive to evolving business needs.

By integrating strategy alignment, governance adherence, and a focus on continuous improvement, the process ensures data architectures remain relevant and effective, supporting the organization’s ability to manage and leverage its data assets effectively throughout their lifecycle.

elements.name: Data Architect
elements.documentation: Someone who is responsible for the overall data functional construct of an organization; its data architecture and data models, and the design of the data storage and data integration solutions that support the organization.

elements.name: Business Modelling
elements.documentation:
A business model describes the rationale of how an organization creates, delivers, and captures value in economic, social, cultural or other contexts. The business model is an outcome of creating new organizational structures or changing existing structures to pursue a new opportunity. The business models of an organization are critical for its business success. New services and products must fit the existing business model, unless the organization is expressly willing to adapt its model to support the new product or service. Evaluating fit with the business model early and often throughout the development process helps prevent costly mistakes. The processes of developing, maintaining and evolving business models form a part of business strategy.

Business model development generally refers to the activity of designing an organization's business model. It is part of the business strategy process and involves design methods. There is a difference between crafting a new business model when none is in place, and maintaining or reconfiguring an existing business model. The differences between the two processes are profound (for example, lack of resource in the former case and inertia and conflicts with existing configurations and organizational structures in the latter). The two sub-process are not mutually exclusive, meaning reconfiguration may involve steps which parallel those of designing a business model.

elements.name: Optimized data sharing and integration
elements.documentation: The capability enables efficient and streamlined movement and integration of data across the organization, enhancing overall operational efficiency and reducing redundancies.

elements.name: Coherent, standardized, and interoperable data products
elements.documentation: A unified approach to data modeling ensures consistency and compatibility across various systems, facilitating seamless data integration and communication within the organization.

elements.name: Support for Innovation and Data-Driven Decision-Making
elements.documentation: A well-managed data architecture provides a strong foundation for innovation, enabling the organization to leverage data for better strategic decisions, insights, and competitive advantage.

elements.name: Conceptual Data Model
elements.documentation:
Data model that represents an abstract view of the real world

Note: A conceptual model represents the human understanding of a system, which can be anywhere from a paper-based system to a complex database in an IT system.

elements.name: Logical Data Model
elements.documentation:
Data model that is a detailed, diagrammatic representation of an organization's data within a specific problem domain, expressed independently of any particular database management product or storage technology. 

Note: A logical data model employs data structures such as relational tables, columns, object-oriented classes, or XML tags to represent data entities and their relationships. The logical data model captures the abstract structure of information, ensuring that data is effectively organized and utilized for business processes.

elements.name: Controlled Vocabulary
elements.documentation: A predefined and curated set of terms, concepts, codes, or headings used to standardize the representation of concepts in a specific context. It is designed to constrain permissible values, provide consistent labels for concepts, or offer suggestions for applicable values, ensuring uniformity and clarity in classification, indexing, and search.

elements.name: Architecture Management Applications
elements.documentation: The Architecture Management Application enables users to develop, maintain, and implement enterprise architectures covering business, applications, data and technology aspects as well as baseline, transition and target architectures.

elements.name: Data Modelling Applications
elements.documentation:
Data Modelling Applications are specialized software tools designed to create, manage, and visualize data models that represent the structure, relationships, and constraints of data within an organization. These applications are essential for developing conceptual, logical, and physical data models that align with business objectives and technical requirements. Coherent Data Models are critical for ensuring data quality, interoperability, and consistency within an organization’s data architecture. Data Modeling Applications support database design, data integration, and governance efforts, ensuring consistent and accurate data representation across systems and domains.
Key Features 
1.	Integrated Data Modeling: Support for conceptual, logical, and physical modeling to address varying levels of data abstraction.
2.	Visualization: Visualization and diagramming capabilities to create entity-relationship diagrams and other representations of data structures.
3.	Reverse/Forward Engineering: Reverse engineering and forward engineering functionality to analyze existing databases or generate schemas from models.
4.	Schema Management: Schema generation and management tools for creating and maintaining database schemas based on physical models.
5.	Collaboration: Collaboration and version control features to enable team-based model development and change management.
6.	Integration: Integration with databases, ETL tools, and data catalogs to streamline workflows and support various database types.
7.	Validation Tools: Validation and quality assurance tools to ensure models are consistent, complete, and compliant with organizational standards.

elements.name: Data Quality Monitoring Services
elements.documentation: Data Quality Monitoring Services (DQMS) provide continuous, non-intrusive, verification of the quality of data assets against established standards and quality metrics across various Data Quality Dimensions (e.g., accuracy, completeness, consistency, timeliness) by utilizing real-time monitoring tools that proactively detect, report, and help remediate issues. Data verification may be followed by corrective actions, such as data editing or data imputation.

elements.name: Computational Governance Services
elements.documentation:
Computational Governance Services provide real-time monitoring of compliance with governance complinace requirements across business processes and their enableing ICT systems., e.g. laws, policies, regulations and standards. These services use rule engines, audit logs, and analytics tools to identify deviations from governance policies and generates reports or alerts for stakeholders, ensuring transparency and accountability.

Computational Governance refers to the automated enforcement and monitoring of governance policies and regulations using software, algorithms, and platform-level controls. This approach operationalizes governance by embedding policies andbusiness rules directly into IT systems, ensuring they are consistently applied at scale across a decentralized and federated environments. Computational Governance leverages rules engines, metadata-driven workflows, and AI/ML tools to automate policy compliance, monitor adherence, and flag deviations in real-time. It minimizes manual oversight while increasing the precision, transparency, and efficiency of governance activities.

elements.name: Data Archiving
elements.documentation: In this Data Archiving reference model there is a particular focus on digital information. As strictly a conceptual framework, the model does not require the use of any particular computing platform, system environment, system design paradigm, system development methodology, database management system, database design paradigm, data definition language, command language, system interface, user interface, technology, or media for an archive to be compliant. Its aim is to set the standard for the activities and services that are involved in preserving a digital archive rather than the method for carrying out those activities.

elements.name: Archival access API
elements.documentation: The Archival Access API allows users to retrieve data from the archive. The information requested by the user is received as a set of broadcast information (DIP), generated from the AIP stored in the archive center.

elements.name: Archival Information Package
elements.documentation: Archiving Management Formats: SIPs are processed in ingestion, validation and structuring modules to enable the ability to manage persistence within an archival system. That is, the AIPs have a vocation of managing the evolutions of the archived contents and must be sufficiently general to be able to generate targeted formats on demand for the export.

elements.name: Archival Content Management
elements.documentation: This feature supports searching and retrieving archived content using descriptive metadata.

elements.name: Preservation Planning
elements.documentation: Preservation Planning supports all tasks to keep the archive material accessible and understandable over long terms even if the original computing system becomes obsolete, e.g. development of detailed preservation/migration plans, technology watch, evaluation and risk analysis of content and recommendation of update and migration. Archives must have a continuous digital preservation strategy (regularly updated) and be monitored regularly to detect the risks inherent in this type of activity.

elements.name: Data Archivist
elements.documentation: A professional responsible for ensuring the long-term preservation, organization, and accessibility of data by safeguarding its integrity, creating metadata for efficient retrieval, and adhering to legal and organizational standards. Data Archivists play a key role in maintaining data's value and usability within broader data management and governance frameworks.

elements.name: Archival Storage
elements.documentation: AIPs are stored, maintained and retrievable from the archive storage. Archive storage includes persistent storage, regular checking of bit stream integrity, and disaster recovery.

elements.name: Long-term Archiving
elements.documentation: The ability to stores products for long-term preservation of data products and data permanence, and support to basic archive functionality and automated interfaces.

elements.name: Operational Archiving
elements.documentation:
The ability to stores data products and higher-level products, possibly including metadata. Supports retrieval of data products through possibly complex methods, and processing. No support for permanence. Stores products for short term (e.g., typically less than 1 year), and allows retrieval of products. Data products stored in this type of archival capability will be updated frequently and versioned.
(Based on CCSDS 312.0-G-1)

elements.name: Archives Adminstration
elements.documentation:
Archives Adminstration handles customer service and manages the day-to-day operations and maintenance of archives and coordination with other functions: archiving, user assistance, implementation and maintenance of policies and processes, etc.
This function attains submission agreements from information producers, performs system engineering, audits SIPs to ensure compliance with submission agreements, develops policies and standards.

elements.name: Archival Ingest
elements.documentation: The process of conformation of objects intended for a digital archive. The Submission Information Package (SIP) is the digital object to be archived with ancillary metadata. The Archival Information Package (AIP) is generated from the SIP as the final step in the ingestion process. The AIP contains all descriptive metadata, technical, project information, access and usage rights, processing (antivirus scans, extraction mode, ....).The ingest transfers the data of the producer (SIP) to the Archivist / Archives (AIP).

elements.name: Submission Information Package
elements.documentation: A Submission Information Packages (SIP) are the most complete formats that applications can generate and where objects are defined independently. These SIPs are provided by a 'producer' for import into an archival system/service.

elements.name: Data Disposition Services
elements.documentation:
Data Disposition Services are a specialized subset of Data Management Services that focus on the lifecycle management of data, particularly the processes of archiving, retention, deletion, and destruction of data in compliance with regulatory, legal, and business requirements. These services ensure that data is securely and appropriately managed, archived, and eventually disposed of, while maintaining compliance with policies and regulations. Data Disposition Services are deeply tied to regulatory compliance, ensuring that data is managed according to specific retention schedules, privacy mandates, as well as government and industry regulations. Data Disposition Services must tightly integrate with archival systems (such as those based on the Open Archival Information System (OAIS) model) to ensure that data is properly preserved and deleted according to both operational needs and regulatory requirements.
Data Disposition services need to ensure the "data integrity" of Data Objects submitted as part of a Submission Information Package  (SIP) so that data is recorded exactly as originally recorded.

elements.name: Information Disposition
elements.documentation: The disposition process deals with the application of appraisal decisions made in the Information Planning stage to either transfer information of permanent value to an Organization's Archives or destroy information that had only temporary value and is no longer required.

elements.name: Dissemination Information Package
elements.documentation: Dissemination Information Packages (DIP) are targeted export formats: These representations are called "exogenous". These are targeted formats for a particular 'designated community', with a defined overall purpose.

elements.name: Data Consumer
elements.documentation: A Participant Agent that requests access to an offered Data Resource.

elements.name: Disposition of Data Product
elements.documentation: Archive or decommission outdated or irrelevant products. Notify stakeholders and update catalog registries.

elements.name: NATO Data Vision
elements.documentation: By 2030 , the Alliance Data Sharing Ecosystem - seamlessly connected through the NATO Digital Backbone - together with a data-literate workforce and optimized data management processes unleash the potential for curated, high-quality data to flow unencumbered and resilient between Allies, the NATO Enterprise, and their respective Communities of Interest. Efficient and effective data management is fostering data driven decision-making that propels our Alliance towards gaining decision superiority.

elements.name: Capitalizing on data as a strategic asset.
elements.documentation: The Alliance must recognize data as a driver of strategic advantage in the same manner as its people, weapons, and equipment. Internally and externally generated data must be leveraged to achieve collective deterrence and defence, crisis prevention and management, and cooperative security. Advancing data as a strategic asset will enable the Alliance to discover efficiencies, drive innovation, and generate new value from its capabilities.

elements.name: Maximising the value of data to transform decision-making.
elements.documentation: The Alliance must set the necessary conditions to make data available, of high quality, and usable so it may drive reliable insights for operational and business decision-making at every level, across every organisation. Secure, integrated digital environments, sustained by modern, harmonized and shared standards for data management and sharing, will enable data to be directed to niche and pan-military requirements, thereby revolutionising the battlespace and securing the Alliance warfare advantage.

elements.name: Investing in the skills and leadership capable of addressing the data needs of tomorrow.
elements.documentation: Developing and maintaining the capabilities within Allies and the NATO Enterprise that fulfil NATO's Data Vision requires a workforce of skills and potential to understand and leverage opportunities for data exploitation as they arise. The Alliance must also promote leadership able to champion data literacy and lead by example in cultivating a data culture across the entire Alliance.

elements.name: Establishing a trusted and consistent approach to data-centric action for the Alliance.
elements.documentation: Capitalizing on data as a transformational asset hinges on the collective ability of Allies and the NATO Enterprise to implement decisions and execute processes towards an agreed direction, thus ensuring advantage can be effected for warfare and peacetime operations. This requires commitment to implementation and delivering data-related initiatives through adequate funding, detailed planning, and measurable targets for the Alliance.

elements.name: Data is treated as a strategic asset.
elements.documentation: Data is the foundation of decision-making within the Alliance. Data has real, measurable value and is considered as a strategic resource alongside people, weapons and equipment. Data is managed to ensure its location is known, its quality is assured, and it can be accessed when and where it is needed.

elements.name: Data is of sufficient quality and available for exploitation by humans and machines.
elements.documentation: NATO has a common approach to all aspects of data, complying with the Principles of the NATO IM Policy and conforming to NATO agreed standards. Authoritative and secure data is available to those who need it, ready for interrogation and exploitation, driving better decisions.

elements.name: NATO Workforce is skilled to manage data to provide quality data that can be exploited to drive advantage.
elements.documentation: NATO workforce has a range of digital skills and is data-literate so it can curate and manage quality data that can be (automatically) exploited.

elements.name: The Alliance Data Sharing Ecosystem enables NATO to share and exploit data at scale.
elements.documentation: The imperative for the Alliance is to systematically pursue data sharing efforts, making interoperable data sharing a norm and shared responsibility among Allies and the NATO Enterprise, fostering cooperation across operational domains, and adhering to NATO's Principles of Responsible Use of AI and Data.

elements.name: The Alliance is the global leader in data-centric defence organizations.
elements.documentation: Allies and the NATO Enterprise work and collaborate on data with partners, industry and academia to secure operational data access and drive innovation. Business, commercial and partner relationships are benefiting the Alliance.

elements.name: Discoverable Data
elements.documentation: Data is registered, searchable, and its existence visible to authorized individuals or systems, with interoperable metadata.

elements.name: Accessible Data
elements.documentation: Data is available for use by authorized individuals, entities and systems through appropriate mechanisms. This includes the ability to search through, retrieve, utilize and manage data using appropriate devices.

elements.name: Trusted Data
elements.documentation: Users and systems need to trust the source of data, data systems, data security, data processes, and the use and exploitation of the data assets. To that end, users and systems need to be able to determine and assess the integrity of data through provenance, history, lineage (transformations), and access control levels for all data assets

elements.name: Shared Data
elements.documentation: Data is distributed, disseminated, or transferred to authorized individuals, systems, and organizations using appropriate secured and protected mechanisms, in a manner conducive to collaboration and cooperation. Data sharing must transcend operational silos and build on the willingness to share of established communities.

elements.name: Secured Data
elements.documentation: Data is protected from unauthorized access, accurate, and availability when needed. Data is secured and controlled along the whole data life cycle, subject to requirements, legal restrictions, and data owner specifications. Data is protected with respect to confidentiality, integrity, availability, authentication and non-repudiation, while at rest, in transit, and in use.

elements.name: Regulated Data
elements.documentation: While Allies maintain autonomy and authority over their own data, including the right to determine how data is collected, stored, processed, and shared, NATO data is governed and managed according to international agreements and approved NATO policies and standards. Thus, NATO retains control and ownership over the data generated, managed, or exchanged within the Alliance without undue influence or interference from external entities. For non-classified NATO data, the execution of control may be delegated to external entities as long as these external entities are subject to the jurisdiction of a NATO Nation and can ensure that NATO Unclassified information can only be used for official purposes and only individuals, bodies or organisations that require it for official NATO purposes have access to it. Protective measures to be applied by external entities shall be set out in service level agreements, contracts, or their attachments.

elements.name: Interoperable & Curated Data
elements.documentation: As a minimum, data collection, storage, and use architecture should ensure interoperability among Allies and within the NATO Enterprise. This requires data assets to cohere with the NATO Digital Interoperability Framework, whereby data will meet requirements at the level of Policy, Organization, Semantics, Syntax and Technical interoperability.

elements.name: Quality managed Data
elements.documentation: The quality of data is properly assessed, catalogued/ recorded, and communicated to users and applications in order to ensure that it is fit for their intended purpose and use.

elements.name: NATO Data Principles
elements.documentation: NATO’s Key Data Principles are the cornerstone of NATOs data governance framework. They are statements of intent with executive level support intended to shape the framework for all data-related initiatives such as policies, standards and processes. These principles set a clear standard for NATO data to promote trust among diverse stakeholders within the Alliance. Programmes that employ, generate, or create data are required to adhere to the Key Data Principles. This enables NATO to move in the same direction and encodes NATO values and culture into data-related decisions.  (A complete guide is available at Appendix 2 of the Data Strategy for the Alliance.)

elements.name: Data Strategy Outcomes
elements.documentation:
A core tenet of the Data Strategy is recognizing data as an essential contributor to the fulfilment of the Alliance core tasks. Given its ubiquity across the spectrum of Alliance capabilities, data is no longer solely an IT asset but one that must be leveraged to create advantage across all domains of operations and the tactical, operational, strategic, and political levels of decision-making. In turn, the progress of Allies and the NATO Enterprise in delivering the agreed data vision must be checked against measurable targets. 
To that end, the strategic outcomes of the Data Strategy provide clarity to the value and quantifiable results the Alliance strives to achieve when delivering against the required areas of action.

elements.name: Setting Scope and Priorities
elements.documentation:
By clearly defining the scope and priorities of the Data Governance Operating Model, organizations can streamline efforts, allocate resources effectively, and establish a robust foundation for managing data assets in a federated and collaborative environment. Setting Scope and Priorities involves defining the boundaries and focus areas of federated data governance initiatives. Drawing inspiration from industry best practices on Data Governance Operating Models, this process entails establishing a comprehensive framework that addresses the complexities of managing data assets across multiple nations, data spaces, and domains of operations and functional areas.
A critical aspect is delineating the scope of federated data governance to encompass data assets that transcend organizational boundaries. This encompasses identifying critical data assets that are shared or used across different nations, functional areas, or operational domains, ensuring that governance mechanisms are in place to manage them effectively. 
Furthermore, implementing an Alliance wide Data Management Directive is crucial for prioritizing initiatives within the defined scope. By starting with a narrow focus on data catalogues, master and reference data, organizations can identify critical data governance challenges such as data consistency and compliance. This targeted approach allows for focused efforts to improve data management practices. This includes making decisions on identification schemes, defining data hierarchies, and establishing standard definitions to ensure consistency and interoperability across the Alliance.
In the context of the Alliance-wide federated Data Governance, the scope extends to encompassing all aspects related to harmonization of legacy data standards, new data standardization, and data quality metrics that are shared across multiple nations or functional areas.

elements.name: Establishing Data Governance Roles
elements.documentation:
Establishing Governance and Management Roles involves two key steps: Identification of Key Roles and Definitions of Roles and Responsibilities. 
Federated data governance involves roles such as the federated data governance body (i.e. the Digital Policy Committee), data-centric governance boards, data/process owners, stewards, custodians and data management offices (DMO). These roles aim to establish and ensure the implementation of central principles and policies, with individuals representing the corporate value of data and addressing specific needs and IT systems. 
The Digital Policy Committee (DPC), responsible for policy setting, principles determination, and investment prioritization, ensures alignment with operational needs by collaborating with data owners, stewards, and custodians at the domain level. This collaboration prevents central governance committee failures and maintains quality standards. 
Identifying common roles, establishing the federated data governance body (i.e. DPC/NATO Information Management Authority) and creating the NATO Chief Data Officer (CDO) role are critical to drive the delivery of data-centricity requirements and management policies, required for fostering a data-centric organization. Responsibilities include implementing data management policies, ensuring coherent data architectures, and leading critical data management efforts such as establishing a federated NATO Data and API Catalogue. Defining the detailed organizational structure(s) and responsibilities for the NATO CDO function ensures alignment with overarching data governance objectives and facilitating effective data management and exploitation across the Alliance.
The NIMA in Alliance format acts as the federated data-centric governance board for the Alliance which is composed of a designated group of allied key data leaders (e.g. CDOs) who are overseeing the implementation and alignment of national data governance practices with NATO’s strategic objectives, driving enhancements in federated data management, policy compliance, quality, and security, while fostering a culture of continuous improvement to adapt to changing operational needs, technological advancements and policy requirements. NDS would act as the supporting DMO. Similarly, each NATO Nation and the NATO Enterprise are encouraged to establish data-centric governance boards at their respective level (e.g. the NIMA in NATO Enterprise format could act as the data-centric governance board for the NATO Enterprise).
The DPC and NIMA provide critical venues for collaborating with national and NATO Enterprise’s CDOs who will be responsible for directing and overseeing the data governance effort. Additionally, assigning data sponsors for data spaces—senior executives versed in data's strategic impact—fosters collaboration and champions domain establishment. These sponsors play a vital role in steering the data governance initiative. 
Within operational domains or data spaces, roles like Data Sponsors and Data Stewards are established to represent data needs, enforce policies, and address sharing challenges. Stewards engage in quick wins, influence decision-makers, and share best practices, empowering each functional area to complete data stewardship activities effectively. Interested stakeholders from operational communities collaborate with data stewards and provide input into prioritization and decision-making processes.
Agreed definitions and responsibilities for data related roles (e.g. data owner, data steward, data custodian, data management office, data architects, data engineer …) are needed to ensure clear accountability and ownership of critical data governance and management efforts across the Alliance. In addition, dedicated support groups are required for providing Master Data Services such as creating and operating Master Data Management (MDM) tools, and an Information/Data Architecting Group that analyses Information Exchange Requirements and maintains architecture models, taxonomies, ontologies, and data dictionaries.
In addition to establishing key roles within the data governance framework, several additional considerations are vital for its success. Clear communication channels and collaborative mechanisms among all stakeholders involved in data governance must be ensured, fostering an environment conducive to effective coordination and cooperation. Furthermore, the implementation of training programs and resources is essential to equip personnel with the necessary skills required for their roles within the data governance structure, ensuring proficiency and competence in managing data assets.
It is imperative to regularly review and update roles and responsibilities to adapt to evolving data governance needs and challenges, ensuring alignment with organizational goals and objectives to maximize effectiveness and value delivery. By adhering to such a structured approach, organizations can establish robust governance and management roles, effectively managing their data assets and driving strategic initiatives forward with precision and efficacy.

elements.name: Establish core data management functions
elements.documentation:
The Data Strategy outlines a comprehensive framework for implementing core data management functions essential for optimizing data utilization within the Alliance. Initially, the strategy emphasizes transitioning from maintaining data within dedicated applications to the utilization of shared data spaces and data platform services, ensuring that users and applications have the necessary infrastructure for data submission while guaranteeing storage, management services, and security provisions to support NATO missions effectively. 
Furthermore, metadata plays a crucial role in facilitating data discovery and access, with users required to adhere to standardized specifications such as the NATO Core Metadata Specification (NCMS). Alongside metadata association, the creation and maintenance of data catalogues are paramount, serving as repositories of information about shared data assets. Ensuring compliance with NATO agreed standards enables federated searches across multiple catalogues, enhancing data discoverability and utilization. Additionally, efforts are directed towards providing comprehensive descriptions of data structures to enable developers and architects to leverage data assets effectively across the organization. 
Beyond these core functions, the strategy emphasizes the importance of adequate funding and detailed planning, highlighting data as a strategic asset requiring dedicated investment. For example, investments are required for implementing data governance tools and platforms to automate and streamline data management processes, monitoring compliance with data policies, procedures and standards. 
Integration with the broader Digital Transformation Strategy ensures alignment with related initiatives such as the establishment of the Alliance Data Sharing Ecosystem and NATO’s Digital Backbone.

elements.name: Associate Metadata with Data Assets
elements.documentation: To facilitate discovery of data assets, users and applications will provide metadata for all data posted to the shared data spaces, in accordance with the NATO Core Metadata Specification (NCMS) which is standardized through STANAG 5636. The NCMS provides a common set of structured attributes that support discovery and effective use of data assets using search tools. Information producers will determine the desired level of discovery for a data asset. The NCMS incorporates best practices in metadata development from DCMI, International Standards Organization, other military and civilian metadata standards. Security-related metadata will be provided for each data asset in accordance with STANAG 4774 to enable controlled access to assets where binding metadata to data is specified in STANAG 4778.

elements.name: Create and maintain Data Catalogues
elements.documentation:
Data catalogues will advertise the existence of shared data and will contain information about all data assets contained in the associated shared data space (including databases, system output files, web pages, documents, and access services). At a minimum, the mandatory metadata elements in the Discovery Services must be represented within data catalogues for any data asset posted to a shared space. 
NATO Communities of Interest (COIs) should establish and maintain catalogues in their respective interest areas. Each catalogue may be organized according to a community-defined ontology (the classes, properties of and relationships among items of interest to a community). 
An Alliance wide data catalogue will have to be established that links to catalogues of communities and data spaces, effectively creating a “catalogue of catalogues.” The Alliance wide catalogue will also contain metadata for data posted by users and applications without a specific functional area service affiliation. 
Catalogues will be searchable by humans through web-based user interfaces and for machines through application programming interfaces. All metadata catalogues will adhere to NATO agreed standards to allow federated searches across multiple catalogues.

elements.name: Identify Authoritative Data Sources
elements.documentation: Data Spaces and COIs will have to identify authoritative sources for key data assets in their domain. The communities will publicize their identified authoritative sources to on the data sharing hubs of the ADSE, thus allowing users and applications to evaluate and understand the community-implied authority of data sources. COIs may have to resolve potentially conflicting sources and, where appropriate, coordinate with the DPC/NIMA to identify authoritative source(s).

elements.name: Provide Data Products
elements.documentation:
While data spaces provide the infrastructure for data collaboration and exchange, Data Products are the tangible outcomes that serve specific user needs within a data space. Data Products will be self-describing and contain metadata related to data structures, models, dictionaries, and schemas. The task will involve extracting and publishing code lists from existing data structures and standards. This effort aims to standardize and streamline the encoding of data by ensuring that code lists, essential for data interpretation, are readily accessible and aligned with established standards. By extracting and publishing code lists, developers and architects gain improved visibility and clarity into the composition and encoding of data, fostering greater interoperability and consistency across the organization's data ecosystem.
Furthermore, the task will entail the establishment of Application Programming Interfaces (APIs) on legacy Command, Control, Communications, Computers, Intelligence, Surveillance, and Reconnaissance (C4ISR) systems. These APIs will facilitate the seamless integration of these legacy systems and services into the organization's evolving Data Mesh architecture. By providing standardized APIs, Allies and the NATO Enterprise enable efficient and secure access to data products, promoting agility and adaptability in data usage and sharing practices across operational domains and functional areas.

elements.name: Establish Data Access Services
elements.documentation:
Users and applications post data to a “shared space.” Posting data implies that 
(i) descriptive information about the asset (metadata) has been provided to a catalogue that is visible to the Alliance and 
(ii) the data is stored such that users and applications that are connected through NATO’s Digital Backbone can access it. Data assets are made available as Data Products. 
Communities of Interest will create shared Data Spaces. In addition, data access services will be created to help facilitate access to database stores, business logic processes, and system data. Data access services are any mechanisms that help expose data that is not otherwise available to users and applications. For example, a data access service may be a registered, accessible software interface that allows users and applications to extract information from an inventory database.

elements.name: Manage Information Exchange Requirement
elements.documentation:
One crucial implementation task for the Data Strategy is the development and standardization of operational Information Exchange Requirements (IER) and Data Sharing Requirements (DSR) between Nations or Data Spaces. This task involves creating a systematic process for defining, documenting, and managing the requirements for exchanging operational information across different Communities of Interest within the Alliance.
To accomplish this, the Information Architects would begin by conducting a comprehensive analysis of existing data exchange practices, protocols, and needs across various operational domains and functional areas within NATO. This analysis would help identify commonalities, gaps, and specific requirements for information exchange and data sharing. 
The Information Architects will have to collaborate with stakeholders from different nations, operational domains, and data spaces to establish standardized templates, formats, and common tools for documenting IER/DSRs (i.e., the semantic Mediawiki platform used by the FMN initiative). These standardized templates would include clear definitions of data elements, and identification of communication protocols, security requirements, and any other relevant specifications necessary for effective information and data exchange. Additionally, the Information Architects would ensure alignment with existing data standards to promote seamless integration and compatibility across systems and platforms. 
In addition, this task serves as a vital mechanism for identifying standardization gaps within NATO's data management landscape. By systematically documenting and analyzing the requirements for information exchange and data sharing in the Alliance Digital Interoperability Architecture, the Information Architects can pinpoint areas where standards are missing or where existing standards may be lacking or inadequate.
Once these standardization gaps are identified, the DMO can then prioritize them based on their impact on interoperability, data integrity, and mission-critical operations. These prioritized gaps can then be escalated to the NATO Digital Policy Committee (DPC) for further review and action. By proactively addressing standardization gaps identified through the process of defining IER/DSRs, NATO can ensure that its data management infrastructure remains robust, agile, and capable of supporting the Alliance's evolving operational needs. This iterative approach to standardization ensures that NATO maintains its competitive edge in an increasingly complex and data-driven security environment.

elements.name: Define common Semantic References
elements.documentation:
Data Spaces and Communities of Interest (COIs) are encouraged to develop representations that reflect their understanding of data. These representations should be shared across communities to facilitate common interpretation within and across domains. Controlled Semantic Reference Models combine vocabularies, thesauri, taxonomies, and ontologies to achieve this goal. 
Controlled vocabularies define terms used within a community to describe data assets, typically ordered by name or relevant criteria. Thesauri group related terms, aiding translation services by clustering semantically equivalent terms. Taxonomies establish parent-child relationships, creating a hierarchical order between terms for enhanced data discovery. Ontologies, the most comprehensive, define relationships between terms and include attributes and rules (axioms) for expressing complex relationships. The use of ontologies is essential for automated semantic processing. Any community establishing these semantic resources should publish them centrally and align with the NATO Common Cross Community of Interest Semantic Reference Model (CXCSRM) to promote understanding and reuse.
The establishment of CXCSRM is crucial for improving interoperability by providing a common core vocabulary for information exchange across domains and functional areas. While domain-specific data models persist, CXCSRM ensures semantic consistency, fostering cross-domain information exchange. Its implementation is foundational to the Data Strategy, requiring governance, standardization, and organizational changes.
To realize the CXCSRM vision, actions include establishing joint governance with the Multilateral Interoperability Programme (MIP), resource management, and inter-organizational coordination. Continuous collaboration is essential for the success of the CXCSRM initiative, vital for enhancing cross-domain information accessibility and sharing within NATO.

elements.name: Continuous improvement in Data Governance
elements.documentation:
Continuous improvement in data governance is essential for fostering data interoperability and ensuring data quality across the organization. This involves a comprehensive approach that goes beyond mere policy alignment to address the complexities of data management and utilization.
Firstly, continuous improvement requires conducting thorough assessments to identify areas where data interoperability and quality can be enhanced. This entails analysing data exchange processes, assessing the compatibility of data formats and structures, and evaluating the accuracy, completeness, and consistency of data.
Subsequently, initiatives should focus on implementing changes that promote interoperability and improve data quality. This may include establishing data standards and protocols for data exchange, harmonizing data schemas and vocabularies, and implementing data validation mechanisms to ensure data quality.
Collaboration among different stakeholders is crucial for the success of these initiatives. Domain-specific groups should work together to define common data management practices, facilitate data sharing and integration, and address interoperability challenges across data spaces, operational domains and functional areas.
Additionally, change management efforts are necessary to embed data interoperability and quality principles into the organizational culture. This involves raising awareness about the importance of data governance, providing training and support to staff, and fostering a culture of data stewardship and accountability.
Continuous monitoring and evaluation mechanisms should be established to track progress and measure the impact of data governance initiatives on interoperability and data quality.

elements.name: Architectures
elements.documentation: Reference Architectures that set the standards, design principles, patterns and technology recommendations for program compliance must be scalable to accommodate the increasing data needs of the Alliance and must be designed to facilitate data exploitation and interoperability.

elements.name: Data Exploitation Framework Policy
Data are a Shared, Strategic Asset
elements.documentation:
To advance common objectives, NATO Allies and the NATO Enterprise commit to share data to the greatest extent possible to address a set of priority Alliance use cases, which will be assessed and prioritized as part of a Data Exploitation Framework Strategic Plan.
NATO Enterprise will maximize data sharing and rights for data use to ensure NATO data are corporate9 , shared, strategic assets.
Data will be made available for use through appropriate secured and protected mechanisms

elements.name: Adherence to Applicable Legal and Regulatory Frameworks
elements.documentation:
Exploitation of NATO data will be governed under the aegis of the NATO Data Exploitation Framework Policy, and will respect Council-approved policies on 
 * Information Management (Reference G), 
 * Records Management (Reference H), 
 * C3 Framework (Reference I), including Data Management (Reference J), 
 * Security (Reference K, Reference L), 
 * Management of Non-Classified Information (N), and 
 * Public Disclosure (Reference M), 
 * Retention and Disposition of NATO information (Reference O, Reference P), in addition to other relevant policies approved in the future. 
 * Category-specific Data Use policies (e.g. Intelligence (Reference Q), Cyber (Reference R), Geospatial (Reference S), Biometrics Framework Policy (Reference T), Battlefield Evidence Policy (Reference U), Open Source Intelligence (Reference V), etc.) will apply, where relevant, in addition to relevant internal policies and rules applicable to the NATO Enterprise.
Data are owned by the entity (NATO nation, NATO entity, or third party) which creates, produces, or collects the data and maintains content, defines access rules, negotiates and agrees to release constraints, establishes disposition instructions, and is the authority for the life-cycle of information.
The collection, storage, sharing, and exploitation of data will be subject to requirements and legal restrictions specified by the information owner and will be handled in accordance to the extant policies, and their updates, as specified in principle 2.i. All action taken on the basis of shared data will be in accordance with applicable legal and regulatory frameworks. Collected data will be managed in accordance with the applicable policies and rules, and retention and disposition will be undertaken in consultation with the NATO Archivist and the Archives Committee. iv) The lifecycle of the data and the lifecycle of the information products derived or created from that data, such as intelligence products, assessment reports, etc., may take separate pathways, with potentially different category-specific Data Use policies applied, as applicable. 
Personal Data: 
  (1) Personal data should be accurate and kept only for as long as necessary and appropriate. 
  (2) Personal data should be protected and exploited securely; fairly and in accordance with applicable national and international law; and only for stated and limited purposes.

elements.name: Enabled Data Use and Custodianship
elements.documentation:
NATO nations or NATO bodies with whom data are shared, act as custodians of the data, and shall comply with the requirements and legal restrictions of the owner for the sharing and use of the data. Data stewards will support the custodians in enabling and ensuring compliance to stated requirements.
Unless otherwise specified by the information owner, data shared with NATO in NATO-led operations and exercises will be protected and handled in accordance with NATO agreed security, legal, and regulatory frameworks.
Data can be categorized as either national or NATO data, as indicated in the information owner and originator metadata that accompanies the data. The information owner and originator will facilitate proper handling by providing the appropriate originator, ownership, and confidentiality security policy metadata values, as well as any special handling rules. iv) In order to facilitate the digital management of data, the information owner will ensure that data is accompanied with complete and high quality metadata, as specified in the Data Management Policy (Reference J), Metadata Management Principles, and supporting directives or implementation guidance, to enable protection, handling, and management of the data throughout its full lifecycle. To ensure traceability and auditability, the following additional guidance should be followed: 
  (1) Certain original metadata of the data shall be preserved throughout its lifecycle. Specifically, the Originator metadata should be specified and should become protected fields that do not change throughout the data/information lifecycle. 
  (2) Ownership should be specified, and could change, but only in accordance with provided transfer of ownership rules (Reference G). 
  (3) Confidentiality metadata values could change, but only with the permission of the information owner or in accordance to the specified handling rules. When these values do change, alternative metadata fields should be used if the original confidentiality metadata values have also to be maintained, to enable proper processing, such as to enable protected sharing through technical firewalls or information exchange gateways. 
  (4) The confidentiality “security policy” metadata should not be used in place of the originator and ownership metadata. 
  (5) If applicable, transfer of ownership rules and metadata changes should be stored electronically with the data/information.

elements.name: Governed Analytics
Interoperable by Design
NATO Data Exploitation Vision
elements.documentation: The vision is to achieve information superiority and data-driven decision making at all levels across the Alliance by fully leveraging the value of NATO generated, national, and publicly available data. The approach to data exploitation will be underpinned by core Alliance values and by principles of responsible development and use reflecting our shared values and consistent with applicable international law. Our efforts will be built in secure, trusted, and reliable environments. These data exploitation efforts will be driven by a strong, collaborative culture which encourages sharing of data. Data exploitation expertise and tools will be made easily accessible to all

elements.name: Information superiority across all areas of the Alliance
Greater awareness, usability, and availability of quality data
Increased sharing of NATO, Alliance, and Coalition data and capabilities
Trusted, sound data, and its coherent governance to ensure data are treated as a valuable strategic asset
Greater Alliance value from data, with increased evidence-informed decision- making within the political and military spheres;
Better reporting on data exploitation and analysis results to decision-makers
Improvements in programs, policies, and capabilities development
Increased numbers of data science practictioners participating in a culture of innovation, learning, and experimentation to ease access to data exploitation
Data exploitation efforts aligned with core Alliance values, including the protection of personally identifiable information and privacy
Objective 1.1: Leadership driven integration of data exploitation
elements.documentation: Leadership driven integration of data exploitation into decision-making processes, in a form whereby decision makers and analysts are able to understand the context, caveats, and limitations of data-driven outputs so that they can be considered and evaluated for use.

elements.name: Objective 1.2: Broadened data literacy
elements.documentation: Broadened data literacy, by providing managed, easy access to data, exploitation expertise, and tools, as well as to the policies, processes, and training to use them appropriately.

elements.name: Objective 1.3: Established specialized data science competencies and increasing numbers of data science practitioners
elements.documentation: Established specialized data science competencies and increasing numbers of data science practitioners, working together to build a culture of innovation, learning, and experimentation through shared collaborative spaces, ideas incubators, managed self-service data analytics, and continuous learning opportunities.

elements.name: Objective 1.4: Commitment to staff development in data exploitation and use
elements.documentation: Commitment to staff development in data exploitation and use, streamlining and improving recruitment, retention and upskilling of staff.

elements.name: Strategic Goal #1 (People)
elements.documentation: Empower our people to derive maximum value from data through a user-focused and leader driven approach.

elements.name: Strategic Goal #2 (Processes)
elements.documentation: Establish a framework of coherent policies, processes, and simplified implementation guidance for leveraging Alliance, Enterprise, Coalition and public initiatives to enable data exploitation.

elements.name: Strategic Goal #3 (Technology)
elements.documentation: Establish a single logical environment for the Alliance, “secure and governed by design”.

elements.name: Data Exploitation Principles
elements.documentation: To ensure that all data exploitation work is guided by core Alliance values, principles of responsible use, and that the critical components of Council-approved policies concerning the sharing of data remain in place, a set of Data Exploitation Principles have been drawn up which will apply to all NATO’s Data Exploitation efforts. These principles collectively provide the rules of conduct that should be followed towards effective data exploitation across its full lifecycle.

elements.name: Data is secure
elements.documentation: Data is secured and controlled along the whole data life cycle, subject to requirements, legal restrictions, and data owner  specifications.

elements.name: Data is trusted
elements.documentation: Users and systems are able to determine and assess the integrity of data, data-related systems and processes.

elements.name: Data is interoperable & curated
elements.documentation: Data, data-related systems and processes ensure interoperability among Allies and the NATO Enterprise, at the level of Policy, Organization, Semantics, Syntax, and Technical interoperability .

elements.name: Data is discoverable
elements.documentation: Data is registered, searchable, and its existence visible to authorized individuals or systems, with interoperable metadata.

elements.name: Data is quality managed
elements.documentation: The quality of data is properly assessed, catalogued/ recorded, and communicated to users and applications in order to ensure that it is fit for their intended purpose and use.

elements.name: Data is shared
elements.documentation: Data is shared to authorized individuals, systems, and organizations using appropriate secured and protected mechanisms, in a manner conducive to collaboration and cooperation.

elements.name: Data is regulated
elements.documentation: NATO data is governed and managed according to international agreements and approved NATO policies and standards, respecting the autonomy and authority of Allies over their own data.

elements.name: Data is accessible
elements.documentation: Data is available for use by authorized individuals, entities and systems through appropriate mechanisms.

elements.name: Standardized Information
elements.documentation:
Information shall have standardised structures and consistent representations to enable interoperability, cooperation and more effective and efficient processes. 
[Source: C-M(2007)0118, The NATO Information Management Policy (NIMP)]

elements.name: Information is a Corporate Resource
elements.documentation:
Information is a corporate resource and shall be managed as such to support NATO’s missions, consultation, decision making processes, and operational requirements by organising and controlling information throughout its life-cycle regardless of the medium and format in which the information is held.
[Source: C-M(2007)0118, The NATO Information Management Policy (NIMP)]

elements.name: Life-cycle managed information
elements.documentation:
Information shall have an originator, and clearly defined ownership and custodianship assigned throughout its life-cycle. 
[Source: C-M(2007)0118, The NATO Information Management Policy (NIMP)]

elements.name: Governed Information
elements.documentation:
Leadership and Organisational Structure: Management of information is a fundamental responsibility, which shall require executive leadership, top level involvement and the creation and maintenance of an effective organisational structure.
[Source: C-M(2007)0118, The NATO Information Management Policy (NIMP)]

elements.name: Needs driven information generation
elements.documentation:
Information needs shall be determined as part of the planning and architecture processes to meet intended activities and effects. The architecture processes encompass activities of designing and maintaining a representation (i.e. blueprint) of components of a business (i.e. organisation, processes, information, technology) and their relationships in order to understand where, when and why information is required.
[Source: C-M(2007)0118, The NATO Information Management Policy (NIMP)]

elements.name: Protected Information
elements.documentation:
Information shall be managed with an emphasis on the ‘responsibility-to-share’ balanced by the security principle of ‘need-to-know’, and managed to facilitate access, optimise information sharing and re-use, and reduce duplication, all in accordance with security, legal and privacy obligations. 
[Source: C-M(2007)0118, The NATO Information Management Policy (NIMP)]

elements.name: Shared Information
elements.documentation:
Information shall be managed with an emphasis on the ‘responsibility-to-share’ balanced by the security principle of ‘need-to-know’, and managed to facilitate access, optimise information sharing and re-use, and reduce duplication, all in accordance with security, legal and privacy obligations. 
[Source: C-M(2007)0118, The NATO Information Management Policy (NIMP)]

elements.name: Establish a data governance operating model for the Alliance
Establish a data centric reference architecture for the Alliance
NATO Information Management Mission
elements.documentation: To support NATO in the conduct of its mission by efficient and effective information management, enabling decision-making by the sharing of information within and between NATO, the Nations and their respective Communities of Interest.

elements.name: Data are a shared, strategic asset
Data Quality Management
elements.documentation: The Data Quality Management (DQM) Capability ensures organizational data is accurate, consistent, and fit for its intended purposes, supporting decision-making and operations. It integrates people, processes, and technology to establish quality standards, define metrics, measure and monitor data quality, and implement improvements. DQM addresses issues like inconsistencies and gaps across data lifecycle stages, promoting governance, compliance, and efficient data integration. By fostering a culture of quality, it minimizes risks, reduces costs, and maximizes data value, ensuring alignment with organizational goals and consumer requirements.

elements.name: Data Quality Team
elements.documentation: The Data Quality Team is responsible for ensuring the accuracy, consistency, and integrity of organizational data, supporting enterprise-wide data quality activities and initiatives. This team typically includes roles such as Chief Data Officer, Data Stewards, Data Quality Analysts, and Data Users, integrating both technical and business expertise. By managing data inputs, quality processes, and outputs, the team aligns data quality efforts with organizational needs. They play a pivotal role in data governance, ensuring data compliance, usability, and value creation across systems and departments. The team's structure adapts to the organization's size and data complexity.

elements.name: Data Quality Improvement
elements.documentation: Deliver sustainable data quality improvements.

elements.name: Data Quality Planning
elements.documentation: Development and agreement of the overall requirements, objectives and plan for delivering the desired maturity of data quality management to the organisation

elements.name: Data Quality Control
elements.documentation: Processes to ensure that data arising from activities meets requirements.

elements.name: Data Quality Assurance
elements.documentation: Identify data quality issues and assess the data against business requirements. Prioritize problems based on impact and risk. Evaluate the need for resources (e.g., time, technology, skills) to investigate and address data quality issues effectively.

elements.name: Data Profiling Services
elements.documentation:
Data Profiling Services provide a foundational analysis of datasets to understand their structure, relationships, and content, enabling organizations to evaluate data quality and fitness for use. Data profiling is a form of data analysis used to inspect data and assess quality that utilizes statistical techniques to discover the true structure, content, and quality of a collection of data.
These services identify patterns, anomalies, and key metrics that drive quality assessments and remediation efforts.

Key Features:
* Data Structure Analysis: Examine data attributes, formats, distributions, and relationships to uncover inconsistencies and deviations from expected patterns.
* Key Metrics Identification: Measure dimensions such as uniqueness, completeness, and validity, providing a quantitative assessment of data quality.
* Anomaly Detection: Highlight outliers, duplicates, and other irregularities that may indicate data quality issues.
* Dependency Analysis: Identify relationships between data elements to ensure referential integrity and structural soundness.
* Profiling Across Sources: Conduct profiling across databases, files, or data lakes to ensure consistency and uniformity in data representation.
* Workflow Integration: Facilitate data quality improvement by generating metadata and recommendations for cleansing, enrichment, and validation workflows.

elements.name: Data Quality Reporting Services
elements.documentation:
Data Quality Reporting Services deliver comprehensive insights into the quality of organizational data by aggregating, visualizing, and disseminating data quality metrics and trends. These services provide interfaces for business and technical stakeholders to monitor the state of data quality across systems, identify critical gaps, and evaluate compliance with established data quality standards.

Key Features:
* Scorecards and Dashboards: Offer graphical representations of key metrics, such as completeness, accuracy, timeliness, and consistency, tailored to business requirements.
* Customizable Reports: Enable stakeholders to generate detailed, ad-hoc, or scheduled reports focusing on specific datasets, dimensions, or quality issues.
* Trend Analysis: Highlight data quality trends over time to support predictive analysis and proactive management.
* Managed Access: Ensure that reports are accessible only to authorized personnel based on their roles in data governance.
* Automated Alerts: Notify relevant parties about deviations from quality thresholds or emerging patterns of nonconformance.

elements.name: Data Validation and Remediation Services
elements.documentation: Data Validation and Remediation Services provide essential support for Data Quality Managment by ensuring the accuracy, consistency, and integrity of enterprise data. These services include automated validation checks to detect and flag data anomalies, comprehensive functions for correcting errors, standardizing and normalizing data formats, and resolving inconsistencies. By leveraging advanced algorithms and business rules, these services help maintain high-quality data across all systems, enabling accurate analytics, informed decision-making, and regulatory compliance. Continuous feedback loops further enhance data quality, supporting a robust data governance framework.

elements.name: Data Catalogue Services
elements.documentation:
Data Catalogue Services are a specialized type of the Data Discovery Services catagory that enables users to discover, browse, and retrieve metadata about available datasets and data products across various data sources. The Data Catalogue Service enhances data discoverability and access, offering a cohesive experience while preserving the autonomy of individual catalogues.

Key Features:
* Creating a unified view on available data to improve data management to increase operational efficiency and productivity.
* Users can search and explore datasets or data products based on various criteria such as keywords, categories, or data formats. The service consolidates metadata into a single, easily navigable interface.
* Detailed metadata for each dataset is available, including descriptions, structure, access conditions, and usage rights.
* Enables multiple organizations to share datasets while maintaining control over their own catalogues, allowing for easy scaling across organizations and sectors while ensuring interoperability within a federated data-sharing environment.

Data catalogues (curated metadata) are collections of metadata to create, curate and maintain an inventory of data assets and data products. It enables data consumers to find and understand relevant datasets or data products to extract business value from data. Data in a data catalogue can contain any type of data in all conditions, either trusted/untrusted or governed/ungoverned.

elements.name: Criticality
elements.documentation: Data Quality Management should focus on the data most critical to the enterprise and its customers. Priorities for improvement should be based on the criticality of the data and on the level of risk if data is not correct.

elements.name: Prevention
elements.documentation: The focus of a Data Quality Management should be on preventing data errors and conditions that reduce the usability of data; it should not be focused on simply correcting records.

elements.name: Root cause remediation
elements.documentation: Improving the quality of data goes beyond correcting errors. Problems with the quality of data should be understood and addressed at their root causes, rather than just their symptoms. Because these causes are often related to process or system design, improving data quality often requires changes to processes and the systems that support them.

elements.name: Standards-driven
elements.documentation: All stakeholders in the data lifecycle have data quality requirements. To the degreepossible, these requirements should be defined in the form of measurable standards and expectationsagainst which the quality of data can be measured.
