elements.name: Objective measurement
elements.documentation: Data quality levels need to be measured objectively andconsistently. Measurements and measurement methodology should be shared with stakeholders sincethey are the arbiters of quality.

elements.name: Business processes driven
elements.documentation: Business process owners are responsible for the quality of data produced through their processes. They must enforce data quality standards in their processes.

elements.name: Systematically enforced
elements.documentation: System owners must systematically enforce data quality requirements

elements.name: Connected to service levels
elements.documentation: Data quality reporting and issues management should be incorporated into Service Level Agreements (SLA).

elements.name: Lifecycle management
elements.documentation: The quality of data should be managed across the data lifecycle, from creation or procurement through disposal. This includes managing data as it moves within and betweensystems (i.e., each link in the data chain should ensure data output is of high quality).

elements.name: Develop a governed approach to make data fit for purpose based on data consumers’ requirements.
elements.documentation: Develop a governed approach to make data fit for purpose based on data consumers’ requirements.

elements.name: Define standards, requirements, and specifications for data quality controls as part of the data lifecycle.
elements.documentation: Define standards, requirements, and specifications for data quality controls as part of the data lifecycle.

elements.name: Define and implement processes to measure, monitor, and report on data quality levels.
elements.documentation: Define and implement processes to measure, monitor, and report on data quality levels.

elements.name: Identify and advocate for opportunities to improve the quality of data, through process and system improvements.
elements.documentation: Identify and advocate for opportunities to improve the quality of data, through process and system improvements.

elements.name: Shared Stewardship
elements.documentation: Everyone in the organization has some data quality management responsibility, some more directly than others. Two important stakeholders are the user of the data who determine data quality goals and top management who must not only oversee the process and set the vision, but must also provide the resources that enable other to accomplish their tasks.

elements.name: Data Quality Service Level Agreement
elements.documentation: A Data Quality Service Level Agreement (SLA) is a formal contract that outlines an organization's expectations for managing and resolving data quality issues across systems. It specifies the data elements covered, business impacts of data flaws, and the quality dimensions that apply. The SLA includes agreed-upon quality expectations, acceptable thresholds, methods for measurement, and timelines for resolution. It also defines roles, responsibilities, escalation processes, and penalties for unmet service levels. Regular monitoring, inspection, and trend analysis ensure that data quality is maintained, helping to minimize business impact through timely remediation of issues.

elements.name: Data Quality Management Procedures
elements.documentation: Data Quality Management Procedures are specific guidelines, routines, and mechanisms designed to implement, rectify, and maintain stringent data quality management protocols. These procedures encompass activities such as profiling, measuring, monitoring, cleansing, and validating data against established quality metrics. By addressing dimensions like accuracy, completeness, and timeliness, they ensure data aligns with organizational goals, supports decision-making, and fosters continuous improvement through consistent and reliable data practices.

elements.name: Data Quality Standards/Indicators
elements.documentation: Documented agreements that define the required attributes of data within an organization, ensuring that it is accurate, consistent, reliable, and fit for use. These standards encompass key dimensions such as accuracy, consistency, completeness, timeliness, and uniqueness. They guide data governance by establishing clear criteria for data entry, format, and representation. Enforced through tools like data quality software, these standards are essential for compliance with regulations and policies, reducing errors, and minimizing costs by ensuring data validity. Standardized schemas for recording quality test results further support interoperability.

elements.name: Data Quality Rules
elements.documentation: Rules that define how data should be structured, validated, and maintained to ensure it is useful, accurate, and consistent within an organization. These rules are aligned with data quality dimensions and can be enforced through systems, software, or templates. Examples include defining value formats, verifying completeness, ensuring data falls within predefined ranges, maintaining consistency between attributes, and confirming data accuracy. Rules can also involve checks for uniqueness, timeliness, and mapping data between different systems or standards to ensure alignment and quality across processes.

elements.name: Master- and Reference Data Management
elements.documentation:
Master- and Reference Data Management (MDM/RDM) is a foundational capability ensuring critical data entities and domain values are consistently accurate, accessible, and governed across an organization. It supports data quality, reduces integration complexity, and enables confident decision-making by providing a unified view of data critical to operations, analytics, and compliance. By managing the lifecycle of these data entities securely and aligning with governance and regulatory requirements, MDM/RDM fosters simplification, reduces risks, and ensures seamless data sharing across diverse organizational functions.

Note: The Data Mesh and Data-as-a-Product approaches represent a paradigm shift that also affects MDM. By focusing on semantic linking, embracing diverse perspectives, empower data owners, and rethinking technological solutions organizations can create more integrated and effective MDM solutions. Therefore a new approach should not only addresses the complexities of current systems, but also empowers domains with the autonomy to manage their data and master data of their domain, ultimately leading to better Data Management practises and more tangible and valuable outcomes.

elements.name: Reduced fragmentation of the data entities
elements.documentation: Reduced fragmentation of the data entities (copies of master data stored at multiple locations while keeping validity and consistency).

elements.name: Trusted, central vocabulary of master- and reference data
elements.documentation: A source of master data that can be trusted to be correct and authentic by systems and people within an organisation.

elements.name: Authoritative MD/RD
elements.documentation:
Ensuring the organization has complete, unique, consistent, current, authoritative master- and reference data across organizational processes

Note: A system of reference may be required to enable sharing of Master Data across an organization.

elements.name: Shared MD/RD
elements.documentation:
Reference and Master Data must be managed so that they are shareable across theorganization.

Note: Enabling Master and Reference Data to be shared across organizational functions and applications by decoupling master and reference data from enterprise applications and ensuring they can accessed in a performant manner.

elements.name: Meeting organizational data requirements
elements.documentation: Multiple areas within an organization need access to the same data sets, with the confidence that the data sets are complete, current, and consistent. Master Data or Reference Data often form the basis of these data sets (e.g., determining whether an analysis includes all customers depends on having a consistently applied definition of a customer).

elements.name: Managing data quality
elements.documentation: Data inconsistencies, quality issues, and gaps, lead to incorrect decisions orlost opportunities; Master Data Management and Reference Data Management reduce these risks by enabling a consistentrepresentation of the entities critical to the organization.

elements.name: Managing the costs of data integration:
elements.documentation: The cost of integrating new data sources into an already complex environment are higher in the absence of Master Data and Reference Data, which reduces variation in how criticalentities are defined and identified.

elements.name: Reducing risk
elements.documentation: Master Data and Reference Data can enable simplification of data sharing architecture to reduce costs andrisk associated with a complex environment.

elements.name: Corporate MD/RD
elements.documentation: Ownership: Reference and Master Data belong to the organization, not to a particular application or department. Because they are widely shared, they require a high level of stewardship.

elements.name: Managed MD/RD lifecycle
elements.documentation:
Master- and Reference Data entities must be securely managed and quality controlled by an accountable Data Steward across the complete lifecycle. 

Controlled Change:  At any given point of time, Master Data values should represent the organization’s best understanding of what is accurate and current. Matching rules that change values should beapplied with caution and oversight. Any identifier merged or split should be reversible. Changes to Reference Data values should follow a defined process; changes should be approved and communicated before they are implemented.

Stewardship: Business Data Stewards are accountable for controlling and ensuring the quality of Reference Data.

elements.name: Standardized MD/RD
elements.documentation: Lowering the cost and reducing the complexity of data usage and integration through standards, common data models, and integration patterns

elements.name: Master Data Management
elements.documentation: Master Data Management (MDM) is a form of data integration, it entails control over Master Data values and identifiers that enable consistent use, across systems, of the most accurate and timely data about essential business entities.The goals of MDM include ensuring availability of accurate, current values while reducing risks associated with ambiguous identifiers (those identified with more than one instance of an entity andthose that refer to more than one entity).

elements.name: Reference Data Management
elements.documentation:
Reference Data Management (RDM) is a form of data integration, it entails control and maintenance of defined domain values, definitions, and the relationships within and across domain values. The goal of Reference Data Management is to ensure values are consistent and current across different functions and that the data is accessible to the organization. Like other data, Reference Data requires Metadata. An important Metadata attribute for Reference Data includes its source. For example, the governing body for industry standard Reference Data.

Reference Data Management (RDM) is the discipline of managing relatively stable, standardized data sets—such as classifications, hierarchies, and coded values—used to classify and characterize other data within an organization. RDM provides centralized governance, processes, and technologies to ensure the accuracy, consistency, and controlled dissemination of reference data across enterprise systems, applications, and analytical platforms.

elements.name: Data Steward
elements.documentation: A professional responsible for ensuring the quality, integrity, security, and effective management of data assets within an organization or community of interest (Data Domain). Acting as a custodian of data, the Data Steward oversees its lifecycle, ensuring compliance with legal and policy requirements and adherence to governance and security standards. They are accountable for defining and maintaining metadata, improving data quality, resolving data-related issues, and ensuring that datasets are governed and utilized effectively while enabling secure and compliant data sharing.

elements.name: Reference Data Manager
elements.documentation: A specialized role focused on managing reference data sets across an entire organization.

elements.name: Master Data Manager
elements.documentation: A senior role responsible for overseeing master data management (MDM) practices, focusing on the consistency, accuracy, and governance of core business data entities like customers, products, suppliers, and locations.

elements.name: Reference Data
elements.documentation: Reference Data is a specialized subset of data used to categorize, define, and standardize other data, providing critical context for interpreting master and transactional data. It includes controlled vocabularies, classification schemes, code lists, taxonomies, ontologies, and cross-reference datasets that support consistent and accurate data usage across systems and processes. Reference Data is typically stable over time and governed to ensure accuracy, relevancy, and adherence to organizational and industry standards. Examples include country codes, currency codes, product classifications, and mappings between equivalent values, all of which facilitate interoperability, data integrity, and efficient communication within and between organizations.

elements.name: Master Data
elements.documentation: Master Data refers to the consistent, accurate, and authoritative information about key entities that are essential for an organization’s operations, analytics, and decision-making. These entities—such as people, organizations, locations, assets, systems, customers, suppliers, and products—provide the context and reference for transactions and activities across various domains. Relatively stable over time, Master Data ensures uniformity and serves as a single source of truth, supporting data integration, efficient processes, regulatory compliance, and reliable reporting while enabling better decision-making and operational excellence.

elements.name: Master Data Management Services
elements.documentation:
Master Data Management (MDM) Services provide a centralized framework for managing an organization’s critical business entities and associated data, including customers, products, employees, and suppliers. These services ensure that core data assets are accurate, consistent, and accessible, creating a single, reliable source of truth across the organization. MDM Services support governance, data quality, and harmonization processes, helping organizations maintain operational and analytical consistency. By unifying data from disparate sources, MDM improves decision-making, reduces redundancies, and fosters a cohesive, data-driven business environment.

Key Features:
* Centralized Entity Management: Centralizes critical data entities, creating unified records that standardize names, attributes, and hierarchies across departments and systems.
* Data Quality Assurance: Applies data validation, deduplication, and cleansing to ensure data completeness, consistency, and accuracy across all records.
* Data Governance & Stewardship: Enforces policies, roles, and responsibilities for managing master data, including version control and traceability, supporting data stewardship practices.
* Integration & Interoperability: Facilitates seamless data integration and real-time synchronization with various applications and data sources, ensuring master data consistency organization-wide.
* Hierarchy & Relationship Management: Maintains complex relationships and hierarchies among master data entities, supporting scenarios like customer segmentation, product categorization, and supply chain mapping.
* Security & Compliance Controls: Provides robust access control and data encryption features to protect sensitive master data and comply with industry regulations and data privacy laws.

elements.name: Reference Data Management Services
elements.documentation:
Reference Data Management (RDM) Services provide a structured approach to managing standardized data sets, such as airport or currenecy codes, Community of Interest and industry classifications, and organizational hierarchies, used across applications and business processes. These services support data standardization, helping organizations ensure that reference data remains accurate, consistent, and easily accessible. RDM Services play a crucial role in data interoperability, ensuring that all systems in an organization adhere to standardized values and definitions, reducing data discrepancies, and enhancing data integrity for both operational and analytical purposes. Both MDM and RDM Services form the backbone of organizational data management strategies, supporting data quality, governance, and consistency across the organization and ensuring that critical data is reliable and accessible for all stakeholders.

Key Features:
* Standardization & Version Control: Establishes and enforces consistent definitions and classifications across reference data sets, with version control to manage changes and updates.
* Controlled Vocabulary & Taxonomy Management: Supports the creation and maintenance of controlled vocabularies, taxonomies, and code lists that ensure consistent use of terms and categories across the organization.
* Cross-System Consistency & Integration: Enables integration of reference data with multiple applications, ensuring alignment and reducing inconsistencies across systems and processes.
* Audit Trail & Data Lineage: Tracks changes and provides a history of updates, supporting traceability and helping users understand the evolution of reference data over time.
* Data Governance & Access Control: Implements governance policies for managing reference data, ensuring appropriate access controls, and supporting data stewardship for reference data maintenance.
* Validation & Quality Control: Applies validation checks and quality measures to ensure that reference data adheres to standards, reducing errors and improving data reliability across systems.

elements.name: Database Services
elements.documentation: The Database Services provide access to shared, structured virtual storage components for data and information persistence as part of the platform environment.

elements.name: Enterprise Data Hub Applications
elements.documentation:
Enterprise Data Hub Applications allow specialist data management roles to manage, integrate, and govern an organization’s critical data assets, including Reference Data and Master Data. It provides a unified environment for ensuring the accuracy, consistency, and accessibility of data across all systems and domains within the enterprise. By serving as a single source of truth, the Enterprise Data Hub supports both operational and analytical use cases, facilitating seamless data sharing, enhanced decision-making, and compliance with governance policies and regulations. Enterprise Data Hub Applications are used for harmonizing disparate datasets and aligning them with organizational objectives. They empowers data stewards, business analysts, and IT teams to collaboratively govern and manage critical enterprise data assets.

Key Features
* Unified Data Management: Centralizes the management of both Master Data (e.g., customers, products, suppliers) and Reference Data (e.g., code lists, taxonomies, classifications) to ensure consistency across the organization.
* Data Quality and Validation: Provides tools for cleansing, deduplication, and validation to maintain the accuracy and completeness of data, reducing errors and inconsistencies.
* Relationship Management: Maintains complex relationships and hierarchies among data entities, such as organizational structures, product categorizations, and customer segments.
* Data Governance Support: Enforces policies, workflows, and role-based permissions to ensure compliance with data standards, privacy regulations, and governance frameworks.
* Versioning and Change Management: Tracks changes to data over time, supporting version control and maintaining a comprehensive audit trail for traceability and accountability.
* Data Publishing: Facilitates the secure distribution of reference and master data to consuming systems and stakeholders, ensuring consistency and accessibility across the enterprise.

elements.name: Data Science Services
COI-Specific Services
elements.documentation: The Community of Interest (COI)-Specific Services provide functionality as required by user communities in support of operations, exercises and routine activities. These COI-Specific Services may have been previously referred to as "functional services" or "functional area services".

elements.name: NCDF Data Product API
elements.documentation: The NATO Core Data Framework (NCDF) is an emerging standard (STANAG 5653) for guiding the development and enhancement of data exchange specifications to enable multi-domain interoperability.  NCDF aims to improve data exploitation for decision-making by using common metadata and establishing common semantic references together with a common syntax within a framework that cuts across multiple COIs and their data stovepipes.

elements.name: Data Repository Services
elements.documentation:
Data Repository Services provide comprehensive storage and management solutions for data across diverse formats and sources within a unified, scalable infrastructure. These services support structured, semi-structured, and unstructured data, facilitating both analytical and operational use cases within an organization. Data Repository Services accommodate various storage models, including Data Warehouses, Data Lakes, Lakehouses, Data Marts, and specialized Data Stores, creating a cohesive environment for storing, retrieving, and managing data. These services are integral to modern data architectures, such as Data Mesh and Data Fabric, by enabling seamless access, interoperability, and governance, ensuring data is accessible and reliable for business insights, decision-making, and compliance requirements.

Key Features:
# Unified Data Repository: Centralized repository for diverse data types (structured, semi-structured, unstructured), enabling easy access and integration from multiple sources.
# Scalable Architecture: Dynamically scales to accommodate data growth and performance needs, supporting both high-volume storage and rapid data processing.
# Multi-Model Data Support: Flexibility to persist and manage raw, curated, or processed data, catering to both analytical and operational use cases.
# Governance and Security: Supports data governance, privacy, access controls, and encryption, ensuring compliance with policies and regulations across maintained data assets.
# Interoperability: Enables seamless data sharing and access across distributed systems, supporting a Data Mesh or Data Fabric architecture for seamless cross-platform interoperability..
# Optimized for Workloads: Tuned for both analytical (BI, reporting) and operational workloads, with performance enhancements like indexing and caching.
# High Availability & Recovery: Ensures reliability through redundancy and disaster recovery features, maintaining business continuity and data accessibility.

elements.name: Data Ingestion Services
elements.documentation:
The Data Ingestion Services support the automated attainment and import of unstructured and structured data from various sources for immediate use or storage in a database. In other words, these services extract data from the source where it was created or originally stored, and load it into a destination or staging area such as long-term storage in a data warehouse or data lake.

Ingested data can be streamed in real time or processed in batches. In real-time data ingestion, each data item is imported as the source emits it. When data is ingested in batches, data items are imported in discrete chunks at periodic intervals of time. The first step in an effective data ingestion process is to prioritize the data sources. Individual data object must be validated and data objects routed to the correct destinations.

A simple data ingestion pipeline might apply one or more light transformations enriching or filtering data before writing it to some set of destinations, a data store or a message queue. More complex transformations such as joins, aggregates, and sorts for specific analytics, applications and reporting systems may be done with additional Data Processing pipelines.

elements.name: Policy Access Control
elements.documentation: Tools for ensuring proper access restrictions and identity verification for all consumers and producers in the data mesh.

elements.name: Operational Data Plane
elements.documentation: The Operational Data Plane is dedicated to supporting real-time data processing and low-latency access to transactional and critical operational data across an organization. This plane integrates diverse data sources such as relational and non-relational database management services, as well as Line-of-Business (LOB) or Community-of-Interest (COI) systems—specialized applications crucial for running core enterprise functions. LOB/COI systems typically serve specific business needs or customer-facing transactions, often featuring tightly integrated capabilities connected to databases and managed through database management systems (DBMS). Examples include ERP systems, CRM platforms, and supply chain management solutions that require rapid data retrieval and update capabilities.

elements.name: Data Management Plane
elements.documentation: The Data Management Plane is a central architectural layer for managing, governing, and ensuring the quality of data across an organization. It integrates core services such as data governance, metadata management, Master Data Management (MDM), Reference Data Management (RDM), and ontology management to deliver consistent and compliant data frameworks. Supporting both real-time and batch ingestion, it enables seamless acquisition of data from diverse sources while embedding governance and metadata to ensure accessibility and trust. Through Data Repository Services, it offers scalable storage solutions—spanning Data Warehouses, Data Lakes, Lakehouses, and Marts—that unify structured, semi-structured, and unstructured data for analytical and operational needs. This plane supports modern data architectures like Data Mesh and Data Fabric, creating an interoperable environment for discovery, sharing, and analytics at scale.

elements.name: Data Store Services
elements.documentation:
Data Store Services provide specialized, efficient, and scalable storage solutions designed for specific types of data and use cases. Data Store Services focus on the storage and management of specific data assets that may require low-latency access, high throughput, or specialized storage formats. These services support the storage of operational data, transactional data, time-series data, or other domain-specific data, enabling efficient retrieval and fast query responses for real-time or operational needs. Data Store Services are typically optimized for particular workloads such as caching, key-value stores, document stores, or graph data stores, and are integral to delivering high-performance capabilities in applications requiring quick, frequent access to specific data sets.  Depending on the specific data store technology (e.g., eventual consistency for distributed stores or strong consistency for transactional stores), these services guarantee that data remains accurate and reliable for the use case at hand.

Key Features:
* Specialized Storage Models: Data Store Services support different storage models, such as key-value stores, document stores, column-family stores, or graph databases, depending on the specific requirements of the workload (e.g., fast reads, high availability, or complex relationships).
* High-Performance and Low-Latency Access: These services are optimized for real-time or operational use cases where low-latency access to data is critical, ensuring fast response times for queries and transactions.
* Integration with Other Data Services: Data Store Services are often tightly integrated with other components of the data architecture, enabling seamless flow of data between operational systems, analytics platforms, and data lakes or warehouses.
* Flexible Querying and Indexing: Depending on the data store model, these services provide fast and flexible querying capabilities with optimized indexing to allow for quick retrieval and analysis of data in real-time or near-real-time contexts.

elements.name: Data Lake Services
elements.documentation:
Data Lake Services facilitate the efficient ingestion, storage, and management of large datasets within a centralized repository. These services are designed to store a diverse range of data, including raw, unstructured, semi-structured, and structured data, in its native format without requiring pre-structuring or transformation. The flexibility of Data Lake Services enables the organization to store data as-is, supporting both batch and real-time data flows, making it an ideal solution for big data applications and data-driven analytics.

Key Features:
* Flexible Data Storage: Data is stored in its raw form, allowing for schema-on-read, meaning the data can be ingested without the need for upfront schema definition or transformation.
* Scalability: The underlying infrstructure storage services are typically designed to scale dynamically to accommodate large volumes of data,  ensuring it can support growing data needs while maintaining high performance for analytical and operational workloads.
* Data Governance and Security: Despite the flexibility in data storage, modern Data Lake Services include strong governance mechanisms, such as access control, data encryption, audit trails, and data lineage, to ensure compliance with privacy laws, security regulations and organizational policies.
* Data Cataloging and Search: Data Lakes incorporate tools for data discovery, such as data catalogs and metadata management, which help users locate and understand the data stored within the lake, enhancing data accessibility and usability.
* Versioning: The ability to track and manage versions of data as it evolves over time, ensuring that historical data is preserved and retrievable for audit purposes or to understand data changes.

elements.name: Data Warehouse Services
elements.documentation:
Data Warehouse Services provide a centralized, highly structured repository designed to store, manage, and process large volumes of structured data, optimized for high-performance querying, reporting, and business intelligence (BI) tasks. These services focus on transforming raw data into cleansed, organized, and integrated datasets, enabling fast access and efficient decision-making for analytical workloads. By storing historical data in a consistent, relational format, Data Warehouse Services ensure that data is readily available for complex queries, BI reporting, and strategic analysis. The data is typically organized in predefined schemas and optimized for high-speed access, making it an essential component for enterprise analytics. Additionally, OLAP Cubes can be used within the data warehouse to provide multidimensional querying capabilities, enhancing analytical insights.

Key Features:
* Structured Data Storage: Data Warehouse Services utilize a relational model with predefined schemas (schema-on-write), ensuring data is stored in an organized format that is optimized for fast querying, reporting, and analytical processing.
* High Performance and Scalability: These services are engineered for high-throughput, low-latency querying, enabling the efficient execution of complex analytical queries even as data volumes scale. They ensure performance is maintained while handling vast datasets.
* BI and Analytics Support: Designed for seamless integration with business intelligence tools, Data Warehouse Services offer fast, structured access to data for reporting, dashboards, data visualization, and advanced analytics.
* Data Governance and Security: Robust governance features such as access control, data encryption, user authentication, and audit logging are included to ensure compliance with privacy laws, security regulations, and organizational policies.
* Data Consistency and Integrity: By enforcing strict data models and integrity constraints, Data Warehouse Services guarantee data consistency and accuracy, ensuring the data remains reliable and trustworthy for critical business decision-making.

elements.name: Stream Processing Services
elements.documentation:
Stream Processing Services enable the real-time processing, analysis, and management of continuous data streams, supporting the ingestion, transformation, and enrichment of data as it flows through a data centric architecture. These services are designed to handle high-throughput data from diverse sources such as IoT devices, sensors, logs, and event-driven services, processing it in real-time or near-real-time to provide instant insights, triggers, or actions. Stream Processing Services are essential for use cases requiring real-time analytics, monitoring, and decision-making, such as fraud detection, predictive analytics, and operational monitoring. By processing data on-the-fly, these services eliminate the need for batch processing, enabling organizations to react to new information as it arrives, ensuring that valuable data is always up-to-date and accessible for critical applications.

Key Features:
* Real-Time Data Processing: Stream Processing Services are optimized for low-latency, continuous processing of data streams, allowing data to be ingested, analyzed, and acted upon in real time.
* Scalability: These services are designed to handle high-volume, high-velocity data streams, scaling horizontally to accommodate large amounts of data while maintaining processing speed and performance.
* Event-Driven Architecture: Stream Processing Services work in an event-driven architecture, processing data based on events or triggers, enabling actions to be taken immediately in response to specific conditions or patterns.
* Data Enrichment: These services support the enrichment of incoming data streams, allowing raw data to be processed and made ready for analysis or use in downstream services and applications.
* Fault Tolerance: Stream Processing Services are built with fault tolerance mechanisms to ensure continuous processing even in the event of failures, maintaining data integrity and system reliability.
* Integration with Data Repositories: These services are often integrated with data repositories such as Data Lakes, enabling the seamless flow of processed data for storage, analysis, or further downstream processing.
* Analytics and Monitoring: Stream Processing Services provide real-time monitoring and analytics capabilities to identify data trends, detect anomalies, and gain insights as the data flows through the system, empowering quick decision-making.

elements.name: Data Discovery Services
elements.documentation:
Data Discovery Services enable searching, identifying and registering datasets or data assets together with their metadata to allow data consumers - such as data scientists, analysts, and data engineers - to find datasets of their interest and to gain real-time visibility into the datasets’ current state, in addition to their “catalogued” or ideal state. Data discovery enables security teams to identify sensitive or regulated information, including confidential or proprietary data as well as protected data to protect it and ensure its confidentiality, integrity, and availability.

Data catalogues, data dictionaries and business glossaries are commonly used for data management. 
* Data catalogues (curated metadata) are  collections of metadata to create, curate and maintain an inventory of data assets through the discovery, description and analysis of datasets. It enables data consumers to find and understand relevant datasets to extract business value from data. Data in a data catalog can contain any type of data in all conditions, either trusted/untrusted or governed/ungoverned. The business glossary and data dictionary are considered part of the data catalog. 
* Data dictionaries (technical metadata) are collections of names, definitions and attributes of data elements that are being used or captured in a database or information system. They describes the meanings and attributes of data elements within a specific context and provide guidance on interpretation, accepted meanings and representation. It also provides metadata about data elements. 
* Business glossaries (business metadata) are a collection of business terms and definitions across business domains, providing common understanding over data elements for all business operations. It facilitates governance policy analysis and development and supports taxonomies and ontologies to address semantic variations. This expands from business glossaries into identifying relationships between data elements, synonyms and (preferably) support ontologies and semantic relationships. A business glossary is primarily used, maintained and governed by business users, since these are the words that they use every day in their business conversations. A business glossary is a form of data model that defines data in business terms. It is also a metadata management capability which is the foundation for all things "semantic", which contains the different meanings of words and terms. 

Data Discovery Services manage Data Catalogs to:
* Gain a unified view to minimize the effort of searching for the right data.
* Support enriching data with technical and business metadata (tagging).
* Improve data management to increase operational efficiency and productivity.

elements.name: Ontology Management Services
elements.documentation:
Ontology Management Services enable the creation, management, and sharing of complex, hierarchical structures that define relationships between data entities across domains. These services provide tools for developing and maintaining ontologies, which help create a shared understanding of data by defining domain-specific concepts, classes, and relationships. Ontology Management Services are crucial for semantic consistency and data interoperability across distributed systems, enabling the automation of complex analytics and decision-making processes by providing context-rich data interpretation.

Key Features:
* Ontology Creation & Editing: Tools for defining and customizing ontological structures, including concepts, classes, properties, and relationships.
* Semantic Alignment & Standardization: Enforces standardized vocabularies, helping align data across departments or domains to prevent inconsistencies.
* Interoperability with Knowledge Graphs: Connects with Knowledge Graphs to build meaningful context and supports semantic queries and reasoning.
* Data Governance Integration: Ensures that ontologies comply with governance rules, helping to standardize data definitions across the organization.

elements.name: Knowledge Graph Services
elements.documentation:
Knowledge Graph Services support the creation, maintenance, and querying of knowledge graphs—data structures that capture relationships and dependencies among data points. These graphs represent organizational knowledge in a way that supports complex queries, inference, and context-driven insights, which is especially useful in a Data Fabric or Data Mesh architecture. Knowledge Graph Services bring powerful relationship mapping and contextual awareness to enterprise data ecosystems, enhancing decision-making by enabling advanced queries and data exploration.

Key Features:
* Entity Definition: Allows for the definition of entities, attributes, and relationships, making it easy to build a network of connected data points.
* Semantic Search & Reasoning: Enables users to run sophisticated semantic queries, perform inferences, and discover patterns based on relationships defined in the graph.
* Real-Time Analytics Support: Offers dynamic data relationships for near real-time analytics and operational decision-making.
* Integration with Ontology and Metadata Management: Works in conjunction with ontology and metadata management services, enriching data with contextual meaning and supporting AI and ML applications.

elements.name: Data-centric governance APIs
elements.documentation:
* Master/Reference Data Management
* Data Catalog Management
* Identiy and Access Attribute Management
* Access Managment Policies

elements.name: NCDF Data Lake API
elements.documentation: The NCDF Data Lake API is a standardized interface for querying and accessing data in a semantically understandable form a data storage & delivery service (e.g. Data Lake) that acts as a logical repository of C4ISR data composed of varied sources of operational domain and functional area data.

elements.name: Message-Oriented Middleware Services
elements.documentation:
The Message-Oriented Middleware Services provide functionality to support the exchange of messages (data structures) between data producer and consumer services, independent of the message format (XML, binary, etc.) and content.

Message-Oriented Middleware Services support different models of message exchange (direct, brokered, queues), exchange patterns (request/response, publish/subscribe, solicit response (polling for response), and for fire and forget), topologies (one-to-one, one-to-many) and modes of delivery (synchronous, asynchronous, long running). They also provide the support for routing, addressing, and caching.

elements.name: Data Platform Monitoring Services
elements.documentation: Data Platform Monitoring Services are specialized functions within a broader platform monitoring framework, designed to ensure the reliability, performance, and quality of data-centric ecosystems. These services provide continuous oversight of data assets, pipelines, and interactions, enabling proactive detection, analysis, and resolution of issues to minimize impact on users. They extend traditional monitoring capabilities by focusing on data-specific metrics such as quality, lineage, freshness, and usage patterns, supporting computational governance to enforce automated compliance with standards. By integrating performance analytics and real-time observability tools, Data Platform Monitoring Services deliver actionable insights that optimize the utilization and value of data products while ensuring the seamless operation of data platforms.

elements.name: Unique Identifier Services
elements.documentation:
Unique Identifier (UID) Services provide a decentralized, scalable system for assigning unique identifiers for resources within a federated architecture. UID Services ensure decentralization, control, and privacy by enabling Data Domains and Product Teams to independently generate and manage identifiers, avoiding reliance on central authorities. By incorporating the Linked Data principles of using Uniform Resource Identifiers, HTTP, and semantic standards, the UID Service enhances resource identification, linking, and interoperability within federated data-centric ecosystems.

Key Features:
* Decentralization: Allows each domain or team to independently manage identifiers, eliminating single points of failure and fostering autonomy by assigning universally unique, persistent Uniform Resource Identifiers (URI, RFC 3986)  using URN with the UUID namespace (e.g., urn:uuid:f81d4fae-7dec-11d0-a765-00a0c91e6bf6), ensuring collision-free uniqueness across federated environments.
* Discoverability: Promotes the interlinking of URIs to build a connected knowledge graph, aiding resource discovery.
* Privacy: Offers selective disclosure for metadata associated with identifiers, safeguarding sensitive information.
* Interoperability: Adheres to Linked Data principles, enabling seamless integration with RDF, SPARQL, and other Semantic Web standards.
* Portability: Ensures system-independent usage of identifiers across platforms.
* Simplicity: Focuses on intuitive management for broad adoption and ease of implementation.

elements.name: Web Platform Services
elements.documentation: The Web Platform Services provide a suite of functionalities that can be used to support the deployment of services onto a common web-based application platform.

elements.name: Analytical Data Plane
elements.documentation: The Analytical Data Plane is dedicated to supporting data analysis, and insights. It manages the data required for business intelligence, machine learning, and advanced analytics. This plane is typically organized with a focus on enabling self-service analytics, where users can access data products in formats that support various analytical workloads. It integrates data from both the Data Management Plane and external sources, transforming it to suit analytical and reporting needs. The Analytical Data Plane utilizes a combination of services from the Data Management Plane to deliver high performance and scalability analytical services.

elements.name: Machine Learning Services
elements.documentation:
The Machine Learning Services simplify and accelerate the building, training, and deployment of machine learning models. Machine learning facilitates the continuous advancement of computing through exposure to new scenarios, testing and adaptation, while employing pattern and trend detection for improved decisions. Machine Learning Services support multiple stat methods that address categorization (binary and multi-class), clustering, anomaly detection, regression, recommendation, and cognitive analysis such as:
* Vision: Image-processing algorithms to smartly identify, caption, index, and moderate pictures and videos.
* Knowledge: Mapping complex information and data in order to conduct semantic searches.
* Language: Processing natural language and evaluating sentiment.
* Speech: Converting spoken audio into text. Recognizing voices of individual speakers.

elements.name: Statistical Analysis Services
elements.documentation: The Statistical Analysis Services are part of a wider data analytics toolset. The Statistical Analysis Services support data exploration, with the help of visualization and description techniques, and the analysis of data sets with descriptive and inferential statistical methods.

elements.name: Analytics Services
elements.documentation:
The Analytics Services provide analytical services to support the decision-making needs of the enterprise, using information produced by gathering, consolidating, cross-referencing and enhancing information from various sources. Different types of analytics can be applied:
Descriptive analytics looks at past performance and understands that performance by mining historical data to look for the reasons behind past success or failure.
Predictive analytics is an area of data mining that deals with extracting information from data and using it to predict trends and behaviour patterns. It is trying to answer the question what will happen.

The Analytics Services enable the development, management, generation and dissemination of reports from identified information sources in a format most readily understood by the target reader and possibly based on specified templates.

elements.name: Reporting Services
elements.documentation: The Reporting Services transform data from a various sources source such as a database, XML stream or a spreadsheet, and use it to produce a document in a format which satisfies a particular human readership. The information provided by this service shall be timely, accurate and relevant. Report generation targets the final requirement by making sure that the information delivered is presented in the way most readily understood by the target reader

elements.name: Data Visualisation Services
elements.documentation:
Data Visualisation Services transform raw or processed data into visual formats such as charts, graphs, and dashboards, enabling users to easily interpret and gain insights. These services integrate seamlessly with various data sources, process and render visual outputs, and support interactive features like filtering, drill-downs, and dynamic exploration. They enable real-time updates, scalability, and customization, ensuring compatibility with diverse tools while facilitating collaboration and decision-making for both technical and non-technical users.

==Key Features==
* '''Interactive Visualisations:''' Support for dynamic features like filtering, drill-downs, and real-time exploration to enable users to engage deeply with the data.
* '''Customizable Dashboards:''' Allow users to create tailored visual layouts and representations based on their specific data and business needs.
* '''Integration with Data Sources:''' Seamless connection to various data sources, including databases, APIs, and other data platforms, for accurate and real-time data visualisation.
* '''Advanced Analytics:''' Incorporating advanced features such as predictive modeling, trend analysis, and anomaly detection within the visualisations.
* '''User-Friendly Interfaces:''' Intuitive and easy-to-use interfaces for both technical and non-technical users to create and interpret visual insights.

elements.name: Geospatial Terrain Analysis Services
elements.documentation:
The Geospatial Terrain Analysis Services support planning and predictive decision tools by providing information and knowledge products that capture integrated terrain and weather effects.

Terrain and weather effects represent a fundamental, enabling piece of battlefield information supporting situation awareness and the decision-making processes within Command, Control, Communications, Computer, Intelligence, Surveillance and Reconnaissance (C4ISR). These effects can both enhance or constrain force tactics and behaviors, platform performance (ground and air), system performance (e.g. sensors) and the soldier.

elements.name: Infrastructure Storage Services
elements.documentation: The Infrastructure Storage Services provide access to shared physical and/or virtual storage components for data persistence. They offer data retention at different levels of complexity, ranging from simple block level access to sophisticated big data object storage.

elements.name: Infrastructure CIS Security Services
elements.documentation: The Infrastructure CIS Security Services provide the necessary means to implement and enforce CIS Security measures at the infrastructure level.

elements.name: Infrastructure Networking Services
elements.documentation: The Infrastructure Networking Services provide access to high-level protocols and methods that fall into the realm of process-to-process communications across an Internet Protocol (IP) network. They are akin to components in the Open Systems Interconnection's (OSI) application layer but are limited to those services required for the infrastructure layer in that taxonomy. OSI application layer protocols such as those for e-mail and directory services are covered by other Core Enterprise Services.

elements.name: Infrastructure SMC Services
elements.documentation: The Infrastructure Service Management and Control (SMC) Services provide the means to implement and enforce SMC policies at the Infrastructure level. The services coordinate and communicate with other technical services (Communications Services, Platform Services, etc.) to fulfill the requirements of service delivery. The requirements are translated into Infrastructure specific parameters and distributed to other Infrastructure Services.

elements.name: Infrastructure Processing Services
elements.documentation: The Infrastructure Processing Services provide shared access to physical and/or virtual computing resources. They primarily provide Operating System (OS) capabilities to time-share computing resources (e.g. CPU, memory and input/output busses) between various tasks, threads or programs based on stated policies and algorithms.

elements.name: Direct Messaging Services
elements.documentation: The Direct Messaging Services are any services that can communicate by exchanging messages in a direct communication with another services. The exchange of messages can be implemented using any Message Exchange Pattern (e.g. request/response, publish/subscribe, fire and forget, solicit response). The Direct Messaging Services can be implemented to use synchronous (i.e. blocking) and asynchronous (i.e. non-blocking) communication modes. For example in a Publish-Subscribe scenario a notification producer would be one Direct Messaging Service sending one-way messages to a notification consumer that would be another Direct Messaging Service receiving the message.

elements.name: Infrastructure Services
elements.documentation:
The Infrastructure Services provide the foundation to host infrastructure services in a distributed and/or federated environment in support of operations and exercises. They include computing, storage and high-level networking services that can be used as the basis for data centre or cloud computing implementations.

Infrastructure Services in this taxonomy are aligned with "Infrastructure as a Service" (IaaS) concepts that are used and promoted by industry today as part of their Cloud Computing developments.

elements.name: Storage Manager
elements.documentation: A dedicated role or automated service responsible for allocating data to the appropriate storage tier based on classification, access policies, and operational needs. It ensures efficient storage utilization, cost optimization, and compliance with security and governance requirements.The Storage Manager serves as the integration layer that orchestrates and unifies the on-premises and cloud storage environments. It facilitates smooth data movement, provides unified management, and ensures consistent access across the hybrid infrastructure. The Storage Manager supports a broad range of data retention needs—from simple block-level storage to sophisticated big data object storage—ensuring that data is available, secure, and efficiently managed across the entire organization.

elements.name: Cloud Storage Services
elements.documentation: A scalable, on-demand, cloud-based storage model - aka Storage-as-a-Service (STaaS) - that enables organizations to dynamically allocate storage resources, automate data placement, and optimize costs while ensuring data portability across multiple cloud providers.

elements.name: On-premise Storage Services
elements.documentation: Traditional on-premises storage infrastructure designed for high-performance, structured workloads. It provides secure, centralized data management for mission-critical operations, supporting databases, file systems, and archival solutions within private data centers.

elements.name: raw data
elements.documentation: Data in its originally acquired, direct form from its source before subsequent processing.

elements.name: data set
elements.documentation: Identifiable collection of data available for access or download in one or more formats

elements.name: registered data
elements.documentation: Data that has gone through a registration process and as part of this has an identifier and usually metadata to aid in its search and retrieval

elements.name: processed data
elements.documentation: Data which have been transformed from raw data or from an earlier data stage into a more refined stage by data cleaning, sorting, linking, verifying and similar operations.

elements.name: data product
elements.documentation: Collection of one or more data objects that are packaged for or by a specific application

elements.name: Data Owner
elements.documentation: A Data Owner is a national, a legal entity or an individual responsible for governing and managing data assets throughout their life cycle. This includes creating and maintaining data, authorizing data release, defining policies, access rules, and retention requirements, and ensuring governance, compliance, and quality. They allocate resources for data maintenance, approve significant changes, and delegate tasks to custodians and stewards, ensuring data aligns with business goals and supports effective decision-making.

elements.name: Data Sponsor
elements.documentation: An executive-level role responsible for championing the strategic importance of data within an organization or a data space, setting data-related goals aligned with business objectives, advocating for data-driven decision-making, and ensuring that data investments align with the organization's overall strategy.

elements.name: Data Quality Specialist
elements.documentation: A responsible individual who implements data quality controls using automated tools to evaluate and monitor data quality, ensuring corrective actions are taken to maintain data fitness for purpose.

elements.name: Data Engineer
elements.documentation: Person who is skilled in designing, building, and managing robust data systems. This includes creating data pipelines, transforming and annotating data, maintaining datastores, ensuring data accuracy and security.

elements.name: Data Custodian
elements.documentation: Data Custodians receive data assets from the Data Owners and are responsible to the Data Owners for providing the data under their custodianship in accordance with the rules established by the Data Owners such as the agreed level of availablility and safe-keeping of data.

elements.name: Data Space Management Authority
elements.documentation: Entity that manages one or more Dataspaces. This will include Dataspace Participant registration and may entail mandating business and/or technical requirements.

elements.name: data set
elements.documentation: An identifiable collection of logically grouped data, organized for access, analysis, or utilization in one or more formats.

elements.name: Data Product Team
elements.documentation: The primary delivery team which has end-to-end accountability for delivery of services (ingestion, consumption, discovery, observability, quality etc.) required by the data product. The data product team has a clear scope and boundaries (management for an identified database, set of tables, of files etc.), an accountable owner, and a skilled team to deliver necessary capabilities. Building data products involves understanding business requirements, managing data, developing solutions, and continuously iterating for improvements. The Data Product Team interacts with other types of service delivery teams required for the successful delivery of the Data Product.

elements.name: Chief Data Officer
elements.documentation:
A Chief Data Officer (CDO) is a data management role responsible for managing the enterprise’s data assets.

Responsibilities are:
* Establishing an organizational data (management) strategy.
* Aligning data-centric requirements with available IT and business resources.
* Establishing data governance standards, policies and procedures.
* Providing advice (and perhaps services) to the business for data-dependent initiatives, such as business analytics, Big Data, data quality, and data technologies.
* Evangelizing the importance of good information management principles to internal and external business stakeholders.
* Oversight of data usage in analytics and Business Intelligence.

elements.name: Data Quality Analyst
elements.documentation:
A data quality analyst is a data management role responsible for ensuring that data meets the data quality requirements.

Notes
* This role involves analyzing data to identify any inconsistencies or errors and developing strategies to correct them.
* The data quality analyst works with other members of the organization to understand the data requirements and business needs. They develop data quality standards, policies, and procedures, and implement them across the organization. The analyst is also responsible for monitoring data quality over time, and for reporting any issues or concerns to management.
* In addition to technical skills, a data quality analyst must have strong analytical, communication, and problem-solving skills. They must be able to work with large datasets, understand data modeling and analysis techniques, and have experience with data management tools and systems.
* Overall, the goal of a data quality analyst is to ensure that the organization's data is of high quality, which can lead to improved decision-making, increased efficiency, and better business outcomes.
* Responsabilities are:
*# Monitoring and reviewing the quality (accuracy, integrity) of data that users enter into company systems, data that are extracted, transformed and loaded into a data warehouse
*# Identifying the root cause of data issues and solving them.
*# Measuring and reporting to management on data quality assessment results and ongoing data quality improvement.
*# Establishing and oversight of service level agreements, communication protocols with data suppliers, and data quality assurance policies and procedures.
*# Documenting the ROI of data quality activities.

elements.name: Data Security Officer
elements.documentation: A data protection officer is data management role that oversees the application of and compliance with the physical and digital security measures to protect sensitive datasets within an organisation.

elements.name: Data Supplier
elements.documentation:
Enity that induces data into a Data Space ecosystem. 
Depending on the individual business and technical operation model, the business role Data Supplier typically assumes the basic roles Data Creator, Data Owner, and/or Data Provider.

elements.name: Data Consumer
elements.documentation: Core Participant in Dataspaces and Data Meshes requesting data and data products provided by a Data Provider. Data Consumer utilize data products for analytics, decision-making, or operational processes and provide feedback to improve data quality and relevance.

elements.name: Data Analyst
elements.documentation:
A data analyst is a data management role identifying data requirements, defining data, and developing and maintaining data models.

NOTE: Data analysts translate data into insights to improve and refine the (business) processes. In addition, business decisions will depend on their analyses.

Responsibilities of a data analyst are:
* Collecting data from different sources, either by surveying people, or gathering already captured data.
* Cleaning and preparing data depending on the requirements of the analysis to ensure result accuracy.
* Analysing data and interpreting results – this involves identifying trends or patterns in data that can help in making informed decisions.
* Communicating analysed results to teams through visualizations or written reports.

elements.name: Data Quality Implementation Planning
elements.documentation: Development of a plan that defines roles, responsibilities, sequencing, funding and technology enablers to perform all other data quality management related activities;

elements.name: Requirements Management
elements.documentation: Identification, definition and prioritisation of the delivery of different data related requirements for the organisation

elements.name: Data Quality Regulations Management
elements.documentation: Development of policies, standards and procedures that support the data quality strategy;

elements.name: Data Quality Strategy Management
elements.documentation: Establish, evaluate and improve the organisations Data Quality Strategy.

elements.name: Plan Data Remediation
elements.documentation: Identify root causes of data quality issues and develop a data quality improvement plan to prevent re-occurrence. The plan includes prioritization of issues, scope, and required resources. Allocate personnel, budget, and technology to ensure sufficient support for improvement projects. Align resources with business objectives to maximize impact.

elements.name: Data Remediation and Cleansing
elements.documentation: Correction of data quality issues using automated tools and/or human intervention and implement the data quality improvement plan, by dedicating resources to address root causes of data quality issues. Manage projects efficiently, ensuring that the right resources are assigned and utilized to resolve the issues on time and within budget.

elements.name: Improve Data Quality Management
elements.documentation:
Implementation of solutions to prevent re-occurrence of data and process non-conformities.
Address emerging data quality issues and improvement opportunities by implementing corrective actions based on evaluations and feedback. It tackles root causes, documents actions, and fosters continuous improvement across teams. The process also includes updating data quality policies, standards, and plans to reflect improvements and changes in data management practices. Regular monitoring ensures the improvement cycle restarts when thresholds are breached, new data sets are introduced, or requirements evolve, while reassessing resource allocation to support future improvements.

elements.name: Review Data Remediation
elements.documentation: Monitor the effectiveness of the data quality improvements. Assess if allocated resources were sufficient and if the improvements meet the strategic objectives and defined standards and requirements.

elements.name: Review of Data Quality Issues
elements.documentation: Assess reported data quality issues to understand their nature and extent.

elements.name: Provision of Measurement Criteria
elements.documentation: Development of measurement metrics and methods to support data quality measurement.

elements.name: Measurement of Data Quality and Process Performance
elements.documentation: Engagement of resources to measure data quality levels and assessing the measurement process;

elements.name: Evaluation of Measurement Results
elements.documentation: Analyse outputs of data quality measurement and assess the impacts of poor data quality and the measurement process.

elements.name: Provision of Data Specifications and Work Instructions
elements.documentation: Ensuring data requirements arising from processes are specified, as are the process steps;

elements.name: Data Quality Monitoring and Control
elements.documentation: Identify and respond to instances where data quality assessment identified non-conformance issues.

elements.name: Data Processing
elements.documentation: Checking that data arising from processes meets data requirements;

elements.name: Data Quality Workforce Development Service
elements.documentation: The Data Quality Workforce Development Service ensures that the organization’s workforce is equipped with the knowledge, skills, and practices necessary to maintain and improve data quality. This service focuses on identifying skill gaps, designing and delivering training programs, and fostering a culture of continuous learning in data quality management. It facilitates the sharing of best practices across teams, develops data literacy initiatives, and aligns training objectives with organizational data quality goals. By collaborating with Human Resource Management, this service ensures that the workforce remains capable of addressing evolving data quality challenges, ultimately enhancing the organization's ability to maintain reliable, high-quality data.

elements.name: Master Data Modelling
elements.documentation: This process is a key part of Master Data Management (MDM) focused on creating, maintaining, and harmonizing data models to ensure consistent, accurate, and interoperable master data across the organization or within a data domain. It aligns master data with enterprise-wide objectives and uses Semantic Data Models (ontologies, taxonomies, and controlled vocabularies) to define meaning and context. This process reconciles discrepancies across systems, harmonizes attributes, and creates mappings between source and enterprise or domain models to support seamless integration and enterprise-wide understanding.

elements.name: Master Data Acquisition
elements.documentation: This process involves the planning, evaluation, and integration of new data sources into a enterprise MDM solution through a reliable and repeatable approach. This process includes responding to acquisition requests, performing data quality assessments, evaluating the complexity of data integration, piloting the impact of new data on match rules, defining data quality metrics, and assigning responsibility for ongoing monitoring of data quality. Additionally, it ensures the seamless integration of new data sources into the data management environment, ensuring alignment with organizational data quality standards, data architecture, and compliance requirements. The process ensures that new data sources support the organization’s goals and maintain high data quality and consistency across different data domains and data sources.

elements.name: Data Refinement
elements.documentation:
The Data Refinement process ensures that input data is accurate, consistent, and ready for effective entity resolution. This process involves three key activities: validation, alignment, and enrichment. Validation identifies and corrects erroneous or potentially incorrect data, such as removing clearly fake or default values (e.g., invalid email addresses). Alignment ensures that data conforms to predefined reference values, formats, or fields, ensuring that previously inconsistent or varied formats (e.g., phone numbers) are now standardized and compatible for matching. Enrichment involves enhancing the data by adding further attributes that improve entity resolution. By making data as consistent as possible, this process reduces errors in associating records and improves the overall accuracy of entity resolution.

Note: DMBoK2 section 1.3.3.4.3 refers to this process as: "Data Validation, Standardization, and Enrichment"

elements.name: Entity Resolution
elements.documentation: The Entity Resolution process is crucial for identifying, matching, and consolidating multiple references to real-world entities across various systems, ensuring that all references to the same entity are unified. It determines whether two or more data records refer to the same real-world object or represent different entities. By identifying duplicates, merging records, and assigning unique global identifiers, entity resolution helps maintain an accurate, consistent Master Data set. Utilizing advanced algorithms, data matching techniques, and business rules, this process ensures that entities are correctly identified and represented across systems, enabling the linking of disparate data sources, removal of redundancies, and ongoing Master Data quality.

elements.name: Entity Matching
elements.documentation: The Entity Matching process involves identifying how different data records correspond to a single real-world entity by comparing data records and assessing their similarity. This process minimizes false positives and false negatives using similarity analysis, which can include deterministic algorithms based on predefined rules and patterns, as well as probabilistic algorithms that calculate the likelihood of records representing the same entity, allowing for continuous improvement as more data is analyzed.

elements.name: Identiity Resolution
elements.documentation: The Identity Resolution process, focuses on accurately linking and consolidating user-specific data across multiple sources to create a unified view of an individual. This process resolves conflicts between different records, such as variations in names, addresses, and other identifiers, by assessing the likelihood that they refer to the same entity. Identity resolution uses matching algorithms and confidence scoring to determine if records represent the same person, accounting for discrepancies in data like address changes or name variations. This process enables businesses to create a single, comprehensive identity profile for each customer, enhancing customer experience, improving personalized marketing, and ensuring regulatory compliance.

elements.name: Entity Relationship Managment
elements.documentation: The Entity Relationship Management process, focuses on maintains complex relationships and hierarchies among master data entities, supporting scenarios like customer segmentation, product categorization, and supply chain mapping.

elements.name: Reference Data Modelling
elements.documentation: The Reference Data Modelling process is a critical component of Reference Data Management (RDM) that focuses on designing a data model for structuring, and harmonizing reference data to ensure consistent, accurate, and interoperable representation across the organization or within specific data domains. It involves defining entities, attributes, relationships, hierarchies, and mappings, guided by international standards, canonical models, and semantic data frameworks (i.e. controlled vocabularies) to establish meaning, context, and relationships. Reference Data models support version control to maintain data quality and traceability.

elements.name: Hierarchy and Relationship Management
elements.documentation: The Hierarchy and Relationship Management process involves defining, organizing, and maintaining the hierarchical structures and relationships inherent within reference data to reflect dependencies, groupings, and associations accurately. This process supports the creation of taxonomies, parent-child hierarchies, and complex networks of relationships between entities, enabling a coherent representation of data across domains. It incorporates robust governance practices to ensure the alignment of hierarchies with business rules, international and  industry standards.

elements.name: Reference Data Distribution
elements.documentation: The Reference Data Distribution process focuses on ensuring that approved reference data is made available to relevant stakeholders and systems across the organization. This process includes the preparation of reference data for distribution, ensuring that it aligns with organizational data standards and governance policies. It involves identifying the appropriate recipients, defining the frequency and method of distribution, and ensuring that all systems and users access the most current and accurate data. Effective communication and collaboration with business users and departments are key to ensure seamless integration and usage of the reference data. Regular monitoring and feedback loops are established to assess the effectiveness of the distribution process and make necessary adjustments to maintain data accuracy and consistency across the enterprise.

elements.name: Reference Data Change Management
elements.documentation: The Reference Data Change Management process tracks changes to reference data, providing an audit trail of modifications, additions, and deletions. It ensures that any changes to reference data are properly documented, approved, and transparent to stakeholders. Change management practices ensure that reference data remains aligned with business needs and regulations, minimizing errors and maintaining consistency.

elements.name: Reference Data Integration
elements.documentation: The Reference Data Integration process addresses how reference data is integrated across various enterprise applications, data warehouses, and external systems. It ensures that reference data can be consumed by different systems and that any transformations, mappings, or adjustments needed to synchronize data across systems are effectively handled.

elements.name: Data Custodian
elements.documentation: An IC element that, on behalf of the Originating Element, may perform mission and business data-related tasks such as collecting, tagging, and processing data, and granting individual users access to additional information beyond that of general systems, applications, and file permissions to perform such functions, where appropriate.

elements.name: Data Scientist
elements.documentation: Someone who creates repeatable means to draw key insights and signals from data.

elements.name: Data Architect
elements.documentation: Someone who is responsible for the overall data functional construct of an organization; its data architecture and data models, and the design of the databases and data integration solutions that support the organization.

elements.name: DM CaT
elements.documentation: The purpose of the Data Management Capability Team (DM CaT) is to advise and support the Communications and Information Services (CIS) Capability Panel (CaP) on all matters pertaining to Data Management topics in support of Consultation, Command & Control (C3), and decision-making within the Alliance and the NATO Enterprise. The DM CaT serves under the NATO Consultation, Command and Control Board (C3B) and the CIS CaP to work on publishing the STANAGs 5644 (Web Services Messaging Profile, WSMP), STANAG 5653 (NATO Core Data Framework, NCDF), STANAG 4733 (NATO Vector Graphics, NVG), STANAG 5643 (Multilateral Interoperability Programme Information Model, MIM), and providing technical responses to the data related inquiries.

elements.name: Information Owner
elements.documentation: The nation or organisation which creates and maintains content, defines access rules, negotiates and agrees to release constraints, establishes disposition instructions, and is the authority for the life-cycle of information.

elements.name: Information custodian
elements.documentation: The nation or organisation which receives information and makes it visible and is responsible to the information owner for the agreed level of safe-keeping and availability of information.

elements.name: Originator
elements.documentation: The nation or international organisation under whose authority the information has been produced or introduced into NATO.

elements.name: Data Analyst
elements.documentation: Someone who produces reports, briefings, and actionable insights that are informed by data.

elements.name: Data Manager
elements.documentation: Person who establishes plans for data quality improvement in an organization, grants data administrators the authority to trace and correct data over the information systems or organization, and maintains data consistency in individual information systems through the organization-wide data architecture

elements.name: Data Administrator
elements.documentation: Person who controls and coordinates the work of data technicians by defining criteria needed to maintain data quality, by designing data schemata, and by analysing the causes of errors to prevent their recurrence

elements.name: Data Technician
elements.documentation: Person who creates, reads, modifies, and deletes data in accordance with the guidelines for data quality management, and measures data quality and corrects data errors found as a result of measuring data quality

elements.name: Data Governance Authority
elements.documentation: Authority responsible for data governance within an organization.

elements.name: Data Subject
elements.documentation: Individual about whom personal data are recorded

elements.name: Data Holder
elements.documentation: Party that has legal control over data to authorize data processing of the data by other parties

elements.name: Data Handler
elements.documentation: Person authorized to operate on certain types of data

elements.name: Data Originator
