elements.documentation: Party that created the data and that can have rights

elements.name: Data User
elements.documentation: Party that is authorized to perform processing of data under the legal control of a data holder

elements.name: Data Broker
elements.documentation: Party that collects data from one or more sources and sells the data to one or more data users

elements.name: Data Recipient
elements.documentation: Entity that receives data via data sharing or data exchange

elements.name: Data Product Custodian
elements.documentation: Someone responsible for the maintenance of each assigned Data Product within the Data Domain.

elements.name: Data Librarian
elements.documentation: Someone engaging in collecting raw data

elements.name: Data User
elements.documentation: Legal entity that has the legal right to use the data of a Data Owner as specified by the usage policy.

elements.name: Data Generation
elements.documentation: Data is created or acquired from various sources.

elements.name: Data Collection
elements.documentation: Data is gathered from different sources and prepared for processing.

elements.name: Data Processing
elements.documentation: Raw data is processed and manipulated to be usable and consistent.

elements.name: Data Storage
elements.documentation: Processed data is securely stored.

elements.name: Data Management
elements.documentation: Data is organized, maintained, and governed to ensure quality and accessibility.

elements.name: Data Analysis
elements.documentation: Data is examined to extract insights and patterns.

elements.name: Data Visualization
elements.documentation: Insights are presented in graphical or visual formats for easier interpretation.

elements.name: Data Interpretation
elements.documentation: Results are interpreted to inform decision-making and drive actions.

elements.name: Data registration
elements.documentation: Process following data acquisition by which data is identified as a unit for subsequent access and processing.

elements.name: Data publishing
elements.documentation: Process whereby data are subjected to an assessment process to determine whether they should be acquired by a repository; followed by a rigorous acquisition and ingest process that results in products being publicly made available and supported for the long-term by that repository.

elements.name: Data curation
elements.documentation: Process, throughout the data lifecycle, by which data/data collections are cleansed, documented, standardized, formatted and interrelated

elements.name: Data deposit
elements.documentation: Process by which data is stored in a data archive

elements.name: Data integration
elements.documentation: Systematic combining of data from different independent and potentially heterogeneous sources, to create a more compatible, unified view of these data for research purposes.

elements.name: Data cleaning
elements.documentation: Process used to improve data quality by detecting and correcting (or removing) defects and errors in data

elements.name: Data entry
elements.documentation: Process of putting data onto a machine-readable medium.

elements.name: Data transformation
elements.documentation: Conversion from one form of data representation to another

elements.name: Data migration
elements.documentation: Process of transferring electronic information from one software/hardware environment or storage medium to another environment or storage medium with little or no alteration of structure and no alteration in content and context.

elements.name: Data access
elements.documentation: Process that enables users to retrieve or read published data.

elements.name: Data archiving
elements.documentation: Digital preservation process that is moving data into a managed form of storage for long-term retention.

elements.name: Data quality management
elements.documentation: Coordinated activities to direct and control an organization with regard to data quality.

elements.name: Data analysis
elements.documentation: Data lifecycle stage that involves the techniques used to satisfy analyst goals of producing informative knowledge from organized data

elements.name: Data translation
elements.documentation: Conversion of a data value from one code set to another

elements.name: Ontology
elements.documentation: Ontologies are formal, hierarchical frameworks that represent concepts, their attributes, and relationships within a specific domain. They enable semantic richness, advanced reasoning, knowledge discovery, and machine interpretability by grouping instances or concepts based on shared properties and relationships.

elements.name: Thesaurus
elements.documentation: Thesauri are structured vocabularies with hierarchical relationships between terms, such as broader terms, narrower terms, and related terms. They also include synonyms and preferred labels, often across multiple languages, to facilitate indexing, retrieval, and contextual understanding.

elements.name: Taxonomy
elements.documentation: Taxonomies organize terms into a hierarchical structure with parent-child relationships, classifying concepts logically and systematically. They support categorization, navigation, and data organization, focusing on broader and narrower term relationships.

elements.name: Alignment
elements.documentation: Alignments define relationships or correspondences between concepts from different vocabularies, enabling cross-domain integration and interoperability. They are crucial for linking data across systems with varying structures and standards.

elements.name: Code List
elements.documentation: Code lists are predefined enumerations of standardized codes representing specific values or types within a category. They ensure data consistency, reduce errors, and enable reliable information exchange and integration between systems.

elements.name: Domain Dictionary
elements.documentation: A structured dataset that provide definitions, word origins, and grammatical details for terms specific to a field, such as medicine or law. They clarify term meanings and usage, ensuring consistent understanding and communication within a domain.

elements.name: controlled vocabulary [ISO:24622:-2:2019]
elements.documentation: A set of values that can be used either to constrain the set of permissible values or to provide suggestions for applicable values in a given context.

elements.name: controlled vocabulary [ISO 25964-1:2011]
elements.documentation:
A prescribed list of terms, headings or codes, each representing a concept.

Note: Controlled vocabularies are designed for applications in which it is useful to identify each concept with one consistent label, for example when classifying documents, indexing them and/or searching them. Thesauri, subject heading schemes and name authority lists are examples of controlled vocabularies.

elements.name: DS::Catalog Protocol
elements.documentation: A set of allowable Message Types that are used to request a Catalog from a Catalog Service.

elements.name: DS::Data Catalog
elements.documentation: A collection of entries representing Datasets and their Offers that is advertised by a Provider Participant.

elements.name: Federated Data Catalogue
elements.documentation: A system that aggregates and organizes metadata from decentralized data sources, balancing local autonomy with centralized data discovery. Federation can be achieved through different mechanisms, such as employing crawlers to periodically retrieve and consolidate participant Data Catalogues into a locally cached repository or leveraging Federated Search to query multiple Data Catalogues in real time and present unified results. Both approaches ensure enterprise-wide data visibility, governance, and collaboration while maintaining robust and responsive functionality.

elements.name: EDC::Federated Data Catalog
elements.documentation:
The Federated Data Catalog (FDC) represents the aggregated catalogs of multiple participants in a dataspace. To achieve that, the FDC employs a set of crawlers, that periodically scrape the dataspace requesting the catalog from each participant in a list of participants and consolidates them in a local cache.

Keeping a locally cached version of every participant's catalog makes catalog queries more responsive and robust, and it can cause a reduction in network load.

elements.name: Data Catalogue
elements.documentation: An inventory of metadata that organizes and describes an organization's data assets, enabling entities to discover, understand, and access data efficiently through features like search capabilities, metadata management, and governance tools.

elements.name: DS::Catalog Service
Upper Ontology
elements.documentation: A formal, highly abstract framework that represents the most general concepts and categories universally applicable across all domains. It provides a foundational vocabulary—such as “entity,” “process,” or “event”—that other, more detailed ontologies can build upon.

elements.name: Mid-level Ontology
elements.documentation: A formal framework that extends the upper ontology by introducing more concrete yet still domain-agnostic concepts. It bridges the gap between the abstract upper ontology and detailed domain ontologies by organizing general concepts like “organization,” “location,” or “asset,” which are relevant across multiple fields.

elements.name: Domain Upper Ontology
elements.documentation: A formal, hierarchical framework specific to a broad domain (e.g., defence, healthcare, finance, logistics). This ontology defines the general concepts, relationships, and vocabulary of that domain—for instance, in the military domain, terms such as “operation,” “unit,” “battlespace,” “target,” and “weapon system” might be included.

elements.name: Domain Specific Ontology
elements.documentation: A refined and detailed formal representation tailored to a particular subdomain within a broader domain. It captures specialized concepts and relationships that address the unique aspects of that subdomain (e.g., specific ontologies for Air C2, Land C2, Maritime C2, Cyberspace, etc.).

elements.name: Use-Case Ontology
elements.documentation: The most detailed and specific ontology, designed to meet the information needs of a particular application or use case. It integrates and organizes the necessary terms and relationships from one or more domains to precisely address the requirements of a specific scenario.

elements.name: Data Centric Analysis
elements.documentation: The Data Centric Analysis Process focuses on deriving value by treating data as a primary asset throughout the decision-making lifecycle. It begins with understanding the business context and identifying key questions or objectives, followed by discovering, collecting, and assessing the quality and relevance of available data. Analysts then apply techniques such as descriptive statistics, visualization, and exploratory data analysis (EDA) to uncover patterns, trends, and anomalies. The process emphasizes semantic consistency, data governance, and metadata management to ensure data is trustworthy, interoperable, and reusable across domains. Insights generated are interpreted in context and communicated effectively to stakeholders to inform strategy, operations, or further analytical modelling.

elements.name: Define Problem
elements.documentation: The Define Problem process focuses on a systematic approach to identify the Problem for which the Stakeholders seek analytics or analysis support.

elements.name: Analyze Data
elements.documentation: Once collected, data must be analysed so that valid conclusions can be drawn about the metrics. Change in these metrics must then be analysed in aggregate to determine progress towards individual effects or directly towards objectives.

elements.name: Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)
elements.documentation: Exploratory Data Analysis is a step in data analysis where analysts investigate datasets to uncover underlying patterns, spot anomalies, and test assumptions. It’s an open-ended process that encourages looking at the data from various angles without a predefined hypothesis.

elements.name: Confirmatory Data Analysis (CDA)
elements.documentation: Confirmatory Data Analysis is a targeted, hypothesis-driven approach that follows EDA. It focuses on formally testing assumptions or hypotheses derived from the exploratory phase using statistical models and techniques. CDA aims to validate the findings with rigorous evidence.

elements.name: Present Findings
elements.documentation:
An important part of developing an assessment is reporting and presenting its key aspects.

Depending on the complexity of the findings, the analysis process may identify additional requirements for analysis instead of providing a solution or outcome. In this case, these requirements feed back into the initiation phase of Defining a Problem process.

elements.name: Develop ML Model
elements.documentation: A structured workflow that transforms data extracted from data-sources into a production-ready machine learning asset and registers it for sharing.

elements.name: ML Model
elements.documentation: Reusable and versioned ML models, pipelines, or algorithms for continuous analytical tasks, accompanied by detailed metadata that describes its architecture, training parameters, and performance.

elements.name: Acquire Data
elements.documentation:
The "Aquire Data" Process focuses on obtaining the data needed to support the problem. Given the nature of data, the details of this activity depend heavily on who you are and what you do. Following are some recommendations to ensure maximum return on your investment.

* Look inside first -- What data do you have current access to that you are not using?  This is in large part the data being left behind by the filtering process, and may be incredibly valuable.
* Adopt an ABC (Adopt/Buy/Create) policy -- Use existing data (internal, open and free or purchased)  before data is created from scratch.
* Remove the format constraints -- Stop limiting your data acquisition mindset to the realm of structured databases. Instead, think about unstructured and semi-structured data as viable sources.
* Figure out what’s missing -- Ask yourself what data would make a big difference to your processes if you had access to it.  Then go find it!
* Embrace diversity -- Try to engage and connect to publicly available sources of data that may have relevance to your domain area.
* Eliminating data silos -- Make data an enterprise commodity.

elements.name: Prepare Data
elements.documentation:
In an operational environment it is likely that data is gathered from a range of sources, and within each source individuals, processes and opinions will change, all of which affects the consistency and quality of the data supplied. Data can be considered of suitable quality if they are fit for their intended uses, whether that is short-term decision-making or longer term planning, or if they correctly represent the real-world situation, which they are intended to describe.

The first step in the analysis is to figure out what data has been collected. Data requires sorting, putting into some type of order; otherwise it will be completely unmanageable. If data is disorganised it cannot possibly be reviewed to identify patterns; trends in the data will be missed and confidence in the in quality will be lost. Organising  data will help with initial analysis and prepare for further analysis.

A good start when organising data is to build an index of data sources and record extra information about each of them that may help to explain what they contain. This will help to retrieve the data easily later. The extra information recorded may provide some explanation of the trends and anomalies  observed between data sources during exploration and analysis.

It is likely that data will start all mixed up within each source and in a variety of formats that are not easy to compare or perform statistical analysis on. In order to see exactly what is in the data, it is handy to put it into a common format. Again, as data is re-formatted, it is useful for later if to mark in the data with which topic they relate to, if they seem to support or disprove a hypotheses and which questions they might help to answer..

elements.name: Organize Data
elements.documentation: Structuring, transforming, and categorizing data to ensure it is ready available for further cleansing and analysis.

elements.name: Clean Data
elements.documentation: Clean data to remove duplicates, correct errors, and handle outliers.

elements.name: Data Scientist
elements.documentation:
A professional who uses statistical, mathematical, and computational techniques to analyze, interpret, and generate key insights and signals from data. 



--------
A Data Scientist is a practitioner who has sufficient knowledge in the overlapping regimes of expertise in business needs, domain knowledge, analytical skills, and programming and systems engineering expertise to manage the end-to-end scientific method process through each stage in the big data lifecycle, till the delivery of an expected scientific and business value to science or industry. [NIST SP1500-1]

NOTE:  Data Scientists are skilled in data wrangling, visualization, machine learning, and domain expertise to solve business problems, make predictions, and support decision-making. Data Scientists work across disciplines, bridging technical and business functions, to transform raw data into actionable knowledge.

elements.name: Visualization Designer
elements.documentation: Visualization Designers transform complex data into clear, engaging visual representations to facilitate decision-making and storytelling. Collaborating with data scientists, analysts, and stakeholders, they create intuitive dashboards and reports tailored to diverse audiences. By applying design principles and focusing on user experience, they ensure visualizations are accessible, aesthetically pleasing, and effectively highlight key trends and insights for informed decision-making.

elements.name: Decision Maker
elements.documentation: A "Decision Maker" in the context of Data Analytics is a key stakeholder who relies on insights derived from data to guide strategic and operational choices. This role involves interpreting analytics outputs, setting priorities, and using data-driven insights to make informed decisions that align with organizational objectives. Decision Makers collaborate with data analysts and visualization designers to ensure the analytics address business needs and translate complex findings into actionable plans. They often prioritize clarity, relevance, and timeliness in the analytics provided.

elements.name: Data-Driven Decision-Making
elements.documentation: Drive fact-based, data-driven decision-making and improve operational performance.

elements.name: clean/structured data
elements.documentation: Data after processing that is of suitable quality for subsequent analysis.

elements.name: Insights
elements.documentation: Valuable, actionable understanding gained from analysing patterns, relationships, and trends within data. These insights go beyond raw data by revealing underlying behaviours, anomalies, predictions, or opportunities that help inform decisions, optimize processes, and achieve specific goals efficiently and effectively.

elements.name: Need for data centric analysis identified
Data Librarian
elements.documentation: A data librarian identifies and vets relevant data sources, collects raw data in a uniform format, and integrates it into data management systems. They apply librarianship principles to data management, including acquisition, organization, cataloguing, metadata management, data citation, and the use of digital object identifiers (DOIs), ensuring ethical standards and efficient data access.

elements.name: Analytical Visualisation
elements.documentation: Graphs, charts, dashboards, and other visual representations of analysis results for intuitive understanding and interpretation.

elements.name: Data Analytics Report
elements.documentation: Structured outputs that summarize and present the results of data analysis, offering insights into business questions, performance metrics, trends, or opportunities. These reports, delivered as static documents, interactive dashboards, or live feeds, use visual elements like charts and graphs to simplify complex information. Tailored to their audience, they support decision-making and may focus on descriptive (what happened), diagnostic (why it happened), predictive (what is likely to happen), or prescriptive (recommended actions) insights.

elements.name: Data Story
elements.documentation: Narratives combining data, visuals, and context to communicate insights effectively to stakeholders

elements.name: ML Engineer
elements.documentation: A person responsible for designing, developing, and deploying machine learning solutions that address business challenges. They prepare and analyze data, select appropriate algorithms, train and optimize models using standard frameworks and tools, and integrate these models into production environments while monitoring performance. In this role, the engineer collaborates with cross-functional teams to troubleshoot issues, enhance model performance, and ensure that solutions are scalable, ethical, and aligned with organizational objectives.

elements.name: Machine Learning
elements.documentation: Machine learning (ML) is an iterative, end-to-end process that begins with defining the business problem and assessing feasibility, followed by data acquisition, preparation, and exploration. Once the data is cleaned and annotated, models are trained, validated, and optimized using appropriate algorithms and evaluation metrics. The best-performing model is then deployed into a production environment where it can generate predictions or insights. Post-deployment, the model is continuously monitored for performance, accuracy, and drift, triggering retraining or updates as needed. Throughout the lifecycle, collaboration among data engineers, data scientists, ML engineers, and business stakeholders ensures alignment with organizational goals and real-world effectiveness.

elements.name: Train ML Model
elements.documentation: Select and configure appropriate machine learning algorithms and leverage standardized frameworks to initiate the training phase, where models learn patterns from historical existing data. During training, hyperparameters are tuned iteratively to optimize model performance, with each run being tracked and documented to capture critical metrics and configurations. The resulting models are then subjected to validation and evaluation using established performance metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, or mean squared error, ensuring they meet predefined quality standards. The output is a refined, production-ready ML model artefacts accompanied by detailed metadata that describes its architecture, training parameters, and performance.

elements.name: Register ML Model
elements.documentation: The ML model and its associated metadata are encapsulated into a standardized resource description enriched with usage policies, making the model discoverable and ready for controlled sharing within the data ecosystem.

elements.name: Annotate Training Dataset (with common Semantics)
elements.documentation: Applying domain-specific ontologies to create enriched training datasets and semantic mappings

elements.name: Acquire ML Training Dataset
elements.documentation: Obtaining the input data needed to develop a ML Model that reduces errors downstream and meets compliance obligations in support of the business problem.

elements.name: Initiate ML Model development
elements.documentation: Develop the mandate for developing a machine learning solution, expanded into scoping documents detailing objectives, success criteria, and constraints. Stakeholders—ranging from leadership to domain experts—iterate on the requirements to confirm feasibility, clarify success metrics, and preempt potential rework.

elements.name: semantically enriched Training Dataset
elements.documentation: Data semantically enriched with domain-specific ontologies and semantic mappings.

elements.name: Training Dataset Delivery Package
elements.documentation: Finalized datasets formatted according to client specifications (e.g., JSON, CSV, or other machine-readable formats) and securely delivered to clients. These packages include labelled datasets and their associated metadata, ready for immediate use in AI/ML workflows.

elements.name: Need for ML Model identified
elements.documentation: This Machine Learning process begins with a senior sponsor or organizational directive that initiates the need for a machine learning solution, expanded into scoping documents detailing business problems, objectives, success criteria, constraints etc..

elements.name: MLOps Specialist
elements.documentation: A person responsible for bridging the gap between model creation and operations by integrating machine learning models into DevOps processes to ensure reliable, scalable, and repeatable model deployment. They build and maintain automated pipelines for model training, testing, versioning, and monitoring, while also addressing performance, security, and compliance considerations throughout the model’s lifecycle.

elements.name: Machine Learning Team
elements.documentation:
The primary ML delivery team which has end-to-end accountability for development and operations (DevOps) of Machine Learning (ML) Models. 
In large enterprises there might one or more people filling distinct roles within a ML Team such as Data Scientists, ML Engineers,  etc. which ensures depth and best practices in each area. In smaller organizations fewer, more generalized roles—sometimes referred to as “Full Stack Data Scientists” who handle analysis, model building, and deployment end-to-end may combine the different roles, which enables easier communication and broader skill exposure; but may leave gaps in deep specialization.

elements.name: IT Architect
elements.documentation: Someone who is responsible for designing and managing IT systems that align with enterprise architecture and business strategies. This role involves defining technology standards, ensuring interoperability, and guiding the integration of IT solutions. The IT Architect ensures solutions are scalable, secure, and aligned with organizational goals.

elements.name: ML Model Custodian
elements.documentation: A ML Model Custodian (aka ML Model Owner) is responsible for the creation, development, and ongoing life-cycle maintenance of their assigned ML Model. With deep functional expertise, they hold long-term accountability for the security, quality, and integrity of the ML Model and associated deployed services. They maintain end-to-end accountability for the delivery, ensuring that the ML Model meets organizational standards and consumer needs.

elements.name: Software Engineer (full-stack)
elements.documentation: A person responsible for designing, developing, and maintaining both client-facing and server-side components of software applications and IT services, ensuring seamless integration between user interfaces, data pipelines, and backend services, including machine learning models. Collaborating with cross-functional teams, they deliver scalable, secure, and robust solutions that align with business goals while enhancing user experience.

elements.name: Data Platform Engineer
elements.documentation: A person responsible for designing, developing, and maintaining integrated data systems across the entire data lifecycle—from ingestion and transformation to storage, processing, and visualization—to empower business analytics and machine learning solutions. Collaborating with cross-functional teams, they ensure seamless integration, robust performance, and secure data pipelines that deliver actionable insights for data-driven decision-making.

elements.name: ML Model Offer
elements.documentation:
Re-usable ML Models have associated ML Model Offers to broker effective discovery and access for users.
ML Model Offers provide ML Model developers with a formal means to express to potential users the architecture, training parameters, performance, format, semantics, and typical terms of use (usage policy) for exchanging ML Models between a ML Model provider and their consumers.

elements.name: ML Model Offer
elements.documentation: A concrete description of the architecture, training parameters, performance, format, semantics, and typical terms of use (usage policy) for exchanging ML Models .  A ML Model Offer is a critical component supporting a ML Model, particularly in federated ML model-sharing environments.

elements.name: ML Model Artefacts
elements.documentation:
ML model artefacts are the outputs and supporting resources generated during model development, often represented in standard formats that support cross-platform portability, allow predictive models to be represented in a vendor-neutral markup, or capture both model and runtime logic. 

For example, a trained neural network—whether a smaller model or a large language model—can be packaged with its architecture, weights, and configuration files (including hyperparameters, training settings, and data transformations) to facilitate portability and reproducibility across diverse tools and environments.

elements.name: Common Metadata
elements.documentation:
Common Metadata provide administrative metadata elements to support discoverability, maintenance, administration 
of a resource in accordance with ADatP-5636.

elements.name: Confidentiality Metadata
elements.documentation: Confidentiality Metadata captures the sensitivity of the information and its associated metadata in accordance with ADatP-4774.

elements.name: Provenance Metadata
elements.documentation: Provenance Metadata enables assessing where the information comes from, how it’s created, and whether it can be used, legally.

elements.name: Amplifying Metadata
elements.documentation: Amplifying Metadata enhance the information resource's usability, interoperability, and computational governance i.e. quality attribute as needed.

elements.name: Conform with NATO Machine Learning Profile
Standardization Task for Exchanging Trained Machine Learning Models
Neural Network Exchange Format
elements.documentation: The Neural Network Exchange Format (NNEF) is an open, standardized data format designed to enable seamless exchange of trained neural network models between deep learning frameworks and hardware acceleration platforms. As neural networks move from research to real-world applications, the diversity of frameworks and specialized hardware has created fragmentation and compatibility challenges. NNEF addresses this by providing a unified format that encapsulates both the network structure and network data, using a human-readable, script-like syntax along with a standardized set of operations. This allows models trained in any supported framework to be exported to NNEF and easily consumed by different execution engines, regardless of the underlying hardware. Ultimately, NNEF simplifies integration across the ML ecosystem, supporting toolchain interoperability and paving the way for hardware-agnostic neural network deployment.

elements.name: Open Neural Network Exchange
elements.documentation: The Open Neural Network Exchange (ONNX) is an open-source ecosystem and model format that enables interoperability between different AI frameworks, tools, and hardware platforms. It supports both deep learning and traditional machine learning models by defining a flexible computation graph, standard data types, and a set of built-in operators. ONNX focuses primarily on model inferencing (scoring), allowing developers to train models in one framework (e.g., PyTorch, TensorFlow) and deploy them across diverse environments without compatibility issues. By standardizing model representation, ONNX accelerates the transition from research to production and fosters innovation by giving AI developers the freedom to choose the best tools for each stage of their project.

elements.name: GPT-Generated Unified Format
elements.documentation: The GPT-Generated Unified Format (GGUF) is a binary file format designed to enable efficient storage, sharing, and deployment of large language models (LLMs). GGUF supports edge computing by enabling efficient, lightweight deployment of large language models (LLMs) on on consumer-grade hardware resource-constrained devices—like laptops, smartphones, or embedded systems—particularly for inference . GGUF encapsulates model parameters, metadata, quantization details, and prompt templates into a single, self-contained file, supporting fast loading, extensibility, and backward compatibility. Building on previous formats such as GGML, GGUF introduces a structured, future-proof approach that simplifies model interoperability across tools, platforms, and environments, making it a foundational format for accessible and scalable AI deployment.

elements.name: Generative Pre-trained Transformer Quantization
elements.documentation: Generative Pre-trained Transformer Quantization (GPTQ) is a highly efficient, one-shot weight quantization method designed to significantly reduce the memory and computational requirements of large language models (LLMs), such as GPT and OPT. By leveraging approximate second-order information, GPTQ compresses models—often with hundreds of billions of parameters—down to 2–4 bits per weight with minimal accuracy loss. This enables large-scale models to run on a single GPU, drastically improving accessibility and inference speed. GPTQ offers compression gains over previous methods while maintaining performance, making it a key technique for deploying powerful generative models efficiently, particularly in resource-constrained or edge environments.

elements.name: Activation-aware Weight Quantization for LLM Compression and Acceleration
elements.documentation: AWQ (Activation-aware Weight Quantization) is a hardware-friendly technique for compressing large language models (LLMs) to enable efficient, low-bit inference on edge devices. Unlike traditional methods that treat all weights equally, AWQ identifies and protects the most important weight channels by analyzing activation distributions, significantly reducing quantization error while avoiding costly mixed-precision computations. Requiring no backpropagation or retraining, AWQ generalizes well across tasks and domains, including instruction-tuned and multi-modal models.

elements.name: Predictive Model Markup Language
elements.documentation: Predictive Model Markup Language (PMML) is an open, XML-based standard that enables the seamless exchange of statistical and data mining models between different software applications and platforms. PMML allows models created in one system to be deployed in another—regardless of the vendor—by encapsulating the complete model specification, including preprocessing steps, model structure, and post-processing, in a standardized XML format. Widely supported, PMML streamlines the deployment of predictive analytics and machine learning models, making it a trusted format for interoperability in data science workflows.

elements.name: Portable Format for Analytics
elements.documentation: Portable Format for Analytics (PFA) is an open standard for representing statistical and machine learning models, along with their associated data transformations, in a portable and executable format. Unlike PMML, which focuses on predefined model structures, PFA offers greater flexibility by expressing models, pre-processing, and post-processing as composable functions using JSON or YAML. This makes PFA better suited for defining complete analytic workflows that can be deployed consistently across different environments, enabling dynamic, scalable, and vendor-independent execution of complex AI and ML pipelines.

elements.name: Data Analytics
elements.documentation:
The Data Analytics Capability serves as a critical enabler for transforming governed and standardized data into actionable insights. It integrates tools and frameworks to support advanced data processing, analysis, and visualization across decentralized data assets. This capability facilitates descriptive and predictive analytics, statistical exploration, and the deployment of machine learning models for tasks such as categorization, clustering, and anomaly detection. The architecture emphasizes interoperability, ensuring seamless integration with diverse systems and scalability to handle growing data volumes and analytics workloads.

Designed for secure and privacy-aware operations, the Data Analytics Capability incorporates role- and attribute based access controls to safeguard insights. It supports real-time and batch data processing, producing consumable outputs via intuitive dashboards and reports. Leveraging modular design, it enables flexible analysis and decision-making while adhering to federated principles, allowing analytics to operate effectively across decentralized data ecosystems without compromising control or autonomy.

elements.name: Data Privacy-Enhancement Services
elements.documentation:
Data Privacy-Enhancement Services protect sensitive data by employing Privacy-Enhancing Technologies (PETs), ensuring secure data use without compromising privacy. These services allow organizations to manage and protect sensitive data and facilitate safe data sharing and collaboration while complying with regulatory standards without compromising its analytical value.

Key Features:

Tokenization: Sensitive data elements (e.g., credit card numbers, PII) are replaced with token values, keeping the original data safe. Tokenized data can be used in applications without exposing actual sensitive values. This feature is widely used in industries like finance and healthcare to reduce data exposure risks while enabling secure transactions.
Anonymization and K-anonymization: These services remove or obfuscate personally identifiable information (PII), making it impossible to trace the data back to individuals. K-anonymization provides an added layer by ensuring that data records are indistinguishable among 'k' individuals, minimizing re-identification risks in large datasets, especially in data-sharing scenarios.
Differential Privacy: Differential privacy introduces noise to the data to mask individual entries while still enabling useful statistical analysis. Global differential privacy is applied at the dataset level, while local differential privacy applies noise to data points before they are collected, offering enhanced security for sensitive data at the collection stage.

elements.name: A Guide to the Data Science Body of Knowledge – Version 2
elements.documentation:
The Data Science Body of Knowledge (DS-BoK) is based on overview and analysis of existing bodies of knowledge that are relevant to intended frameworks for Data Science and required to fulfil identified in CF-DS competences and skills.
The intended EDSION framework comprising of mentioned above components will provide a guidance and a basis for universities to define their Data Science curricula and courses selection, on one hand, and for companies to better define a set of required competences and skills for their specific industry domain in their search for Data Science talents, on the other hand.
The DS-BoK definition is based on the proposed CF-DS that defines the five groups of competences for Data Science that include the commonly recognised groups Data Analytics, Data Science Engineering, Domain Knowledge (as defined in the NIST definition of Data Science) and extends them with the two new groups Data Management and Scientific Methods (or Business Process management for business related occupations) that are recognised to be important for a successful work of Data Scientist but are not explicitly mentioned in existing frameworks.

elements.name: Data Modelling & Design
elements.documentation:
A Data Modelling and Design capability represents an organization's ability to systematically discover, analyse, and define data requirements, and then represent these requirements through precise data models. This capability enables the organization to understand, manage, and utilize its data assets effectively, ensuring alignment with current and future business needs. It creates a foundation for broad-scoped initiatives such as Master Data Management (MDM) and data governance programs, enhances data quality, reduces support costs, and increases the reusability of data assets.

Core Elements:
* People: Business Analysts, Data Modelers, and Data Architects who collaborate to define and design data models.
* Processes: Activities such as planning for data modelling, building and reviewing data models (conceptual, logical, and physical), and managing the lifecycle of these models.
* Technology: Tools and platforms used for data modelling, integration, and management to support the creation, maintenance, and utilization of data models.

elements.name: Data Modelling & Design Roles and Processes
Data Model & Design Quality Management
elements.documentation: This process ensures that data models and database designs meet both short-term and long-term business requirements, balancing the needs of information consumers and data producers. Data analysts and designers act as intermediaries, ensuring that data structures are secure, recoverable, sharable, and reusable while meeting immediate business obligations and project constraints. This process involves continuous evaluation and improvement, incorporating quality control measures and feedback loops to maintain the accuracy, relevance, and usability of data models, thus supporting effective data management and organizational goals.

elements.name: Review the Data Models
elements.documentation: This process involves thorough quality control and continuous improvement practices to ensure the correctness, completeness, and consistency of data models. Using tools like the Data Model Scorecard® and implementing feedback loops for refinement, this process results in high-quality models that accurately reflect business requirements and enhance data governance. Stakeholders such as business analysts, data modelers, and developers are involved in reviewing and approving the models.

elements.name: Maintain the Data Models
elements.documentation: This process keeps data models up-to-date with evolving requirements and business processes, ensuring consistency across conceptual, logical, and physical levels through regular updates and automated tools. This includes reverse engineering the latest physical data models to maintain alignment with their logical counterparts and documenting all changes. This process ensures that data models remain accurate, relevant, and consistent over time, supporting improved data quality and governance.

elements.name: Plan for Data Modelling
elements.documentation: A plan for data modelling contains tasks such as evaluating organizational requirements, adopting or creating standards, and determining data model storage.

elements.name: Build the Data Models
elements.documentation: The foundational activity of organizing and structuring data within a database, ensuring efficient management, storage, and analysis. It involves creating semantic, conceptual, logical, and physical data models that serve as blueprints for structuring data entities, attributes, and their interconnections. This process ensures data interoperability, database performance, accuracy, consistency, and scalability through techniques such as normalization and entity-relationship diagrams. It is iterative and involves continuous refinement and validation of data models.

elements.name: Data Modeler
elements.documentation: A data modeler is a data management role responsible for capturing and modeling data requirements, definitions, business rules, and data quality needs to design and manage logical and physical data models. This role supports data pipeline lifecycles and broader initiatives such as business architecture, master data management, and governance. Collaborating with data architects and database administrators, they ensure data structures are optimized, aligned with organizational objectives, and consistent with enterprise terminology and rules. By leveraging advanced tools and creating visual data representations, Data Modelers facilitate integration, improve data quality, and enable efficient decision-making and customer-focused operations.

elements.name: Data Modelling & Design Information and Data
Semantic Data Model
elements.documentation: A Semantic Data Model is a data model that defines the meaning and relationships of data, utilizing controlled vocabularies, ontologies, and taxonomies. It operates at a high level of abstraction, concentrating on "what data means" universally and provides a conceptual framework for ensuring data interoperability and accurate knowledge representation, enabling consistency in how data is understood and interpreted across diverse organizational contexts.

elements.name: Physical Data Model
elements.documentation: A physical data model is a database-specific model that represents relational data objects (for example, tables, columns, primary and foreign keys) and their relationships. A physical data model can be used to generate Data Definition Language (DDL) statements which can then be deployed to a database server.

elements.name: Data Model Pattern
elements.documentation: Data model patterns are reusable modeling structures that can be applied to a wide class of situations. There are elementary, assembly, and integration data model patterns. Elementary patterns are the ‘nuts and bolts’ of data modeling. They include ways to resolve many-to-many relationships, and to construct self-referencing hierarchies. Assembly patterns are often published and can give the modeler proven, robust, extensible, and implementable designs. Integration patterns provide the framework for linking the assembly patterns in common ways.

elements.name: Data Modelling & Design Applications and Technology
Data Modelling & Design Vision and Principles
Scalability and Reusability
elements.documentation: Design models that can grow with organizational needs and be reused across multiple projects and domains.

elements.name: Traceability
elements.documentation: Ensure that data models are versioned, governed, and traceable to their business and technical origins.

elements.name: Simplicity and Clarity
elements.documentation: Create data models that are clear, understandable, and as simple as possible while meeting requirements.

elements.name: Business-Driven
elements.documentation: Data models should reflect business goals, processes, and semantics, ensuring alignment with enterprise priorities.

elements.name: Technology Neutrality
elements.documentation: Maintain flexibility in data models to support diverse platforms, systems, and evolving technologies.

elements.name: Enhanced Data Quality
elements.documentation: Improved data integrity, consistency, and accuracy through well-defined and governed data models.

elements.name: Streamlined Operations
elements.documentation: Reduce inefficiencies by aligning data structures with business processes, ensuring smooth data integration and management.

elements.name: Data Centric Governance
elements.documentation:
The Data-Centric Governance Capability ensures that governance is a collective and accountable process, embedded at the data domain level within a federated structure. It operates through a cross-functional governance team composed of domain representatives, platform experts, and subject-matter experts (SMEs) from security, compliance, legal, and other relevant areas. This team defines Alliance-wide governance policies, determines how these policies are computationally enforced through the data platform, and oversees their consistent adoption by all data products.
A Data Governance Board orchestrates the implementation of these policies, balancing centralized oversight with domain-specific flexibility. The board ensures compliance monitoring, coordination of data stewardship, and adherence to shared standards for metadata quality, semantic modeling, and interoperability. It also oversees Data Governance Support Services, which provide the tools, frameworks, and guidance necessary for operationalizing governance policies.
In addition to standardizing data management practices, the capability regulates the establishment and operations of Data Spaces, mandating business and technical requirements to ensure secure, interoperable, and compliant data sharing. By integrating governance at every layer—domains, products, and infrastructure—this capability fosters trust, accountability, and agility across a decentralized data ecosystem.

elements.name: Establish a Data Quality Framework for the Alliance
elements.documentation: To implement the Data Quality Framework for the Alliance, Allies and the NATO Enterprise must integrate data quality principles to enhance data usability, reliability, and value, addressing both technical and non-technical challenges. The Data Quality Framework for the Alliance ensures data portability, interoperability, and traceability across the Alliance, promoting effective collaboration and readiness for partnerships. It validates the foundations for digital transformation, establishes data as a fundamental asset, and fosters evidence-based trust among stakeholders. By improving decision-making, operational efficiency, and governance, the framework strengthens NATO’s mission success while ensuring compliance, resource optimization, and sustained data quality improvement.

elements.name: Establish Master- and Reference Data Management
Data Centric Governance Applications and Technology
IT Governance Applications
elements.documentation:
The IT Governance Applications support organizations to align IT architecture and strategy with business goals to ensure that the right technology will be in place to support the organization as it moves forward. IT Governance Applications are part of an organizational IT governance framework, to enable the identification of needs and interests of stakeholders, measure IT results and understand the return on investment that the organization is making in its business architecture.

IT Governance Applications provide a platform:
* To understand the existing IT landscape.
* To align systems with business goals.
* To gain insight into IT and application architecture with a real-time picture of the enterprise architecture landscape, including all applications, the technologies they run on and the business processes they support.
* To gain insight into how changes to the enterprise architecture will impact business and IT.
* To increase consistency and enhance transparency of the enterprise architecture landscape with other repositories for business, IT services and Service Assets.
* To establish a foundation for IT transformation.

elements.name: Data Centric Governance Vision and Principles
Integrated data ecosystems
elements.documentation:
Integrated data ecosystems with capabilities that are managed, governed, and secured to ensure 
consistent data quality and the flexibility to evolve and scale based on the data requirements of the Alliance.

elements.name: Data-savvy workforce
elements.documentation: A data-savvy workforce possess the skills, knowledge, and mindset to effectively use data in their roles. This workforce is capable of understanding, analyzing, and interpreting data to drive decisions, solve problems, and contribute to organizational goals. A data-savvy workforce is not limited to technical roles like data scientists or analysts but extends to all employees who can leverage data insights in their day-to-day work.

elements.name: Data Centric Governance Roles and Processes
Federated Data Governance
elements.documentation:
Establishes Alliance-wide governance policies while allowing domain-specific implementations.
Includes policy creation, compliance monitoring, and stewardship coordination. Ensures consistent adherence to data standards, semantic modelling, and metadata quality.

elements.name: Manage Data Governance Support Services
elements.documentation: Manage Data Governance Support Services

elements.name: Manage Information Exchange Requirements
elements.documentation: The IER process is a structured methodology for defining, documenting, and managing the requirements for exchanging operational information across the organization (i.e. between different business units), business domains, and with external entities. This process enables the elicitation of common requirements for the seamless, secure, and standardized data exchange within the Alliance to support interoperability and mission success.

elements.name: Classification of Data
elements.documentation: The process of arranging data into homogeneous (similar) groups according to their common characteristics.

elements.name: Data Creator
elements.documentation:
The Data Creator creates data, e.g. by generating data such as from a sensor or accessing data in backend IT systems.
Usually, a participant acting as a Data Creator automatically assumes the role of the Data Owner. However, if rights or licenses on data are given to different participant, the same assumes the role of the Data Owner. In this case, Data Owner and Data Creator would be different participants.
Initially, a participant acting as a Data Creator automatically assumes the role of the Data Provider as well. However, there may be cases in which the Data Provider is not the Data Creator, e.g. if the data is technically managed by a different entity than the Data Creator. This can be the case of a company using an external IT service provider for data management, or if data management activities are handed over to a data intermediary as a Data Custodian.

elements.name: Data Centric Governance Information and Data
Data Management Procedures
elements.documentation:
Data Management Procedures represent formalized workflows and guidelines necessary for effective governance and management of data as a strategic asset. 
--------------------------------------
The Data Management Procedures proposed below provide a comprehensive framework for supporting a Data-Centric Governance Capability, ensuring a structured, repeatable, and scalable approach to managing data as a strategic asset. By implementing these procedures, organizations can improve data quality, enhance operational efficiency, and ensure compliance with regulatory and business requirements.

1. Data Governance Procedures: These procedures establish the overarching framework for governing data assets, ensuring accountability, and enforcing compliance across the organization:
* Define roles and responsibilities (e.g., Data Owners, Data Stewards, Governance Committees).
* Establish governance frameworks, policies, and decision-making mechanisms.
* Manage data governance policies related to compliance, risk, and usage.
* Develop and enforce data stewardship practices and accountability measures.

2. Data Quality Management Procedures: Focus on maintaining and improving the quality of data assets by aligning them with organizational requirements:
* Develop processes for data profiling, quality measurement, and root cause analysis.
* Define acceptable data quality thresholds and monitor against them.
* Create procedures for identifying, resolving, and preventing data quality issues.
* Develop data quality reporting mechanisms for stakeholders.

3. Data Lifecycle Management Procedures: Ensure proper management of data throughout its lifecycle, from creation to deletion:
* Define retention polici*es and data archival processes.
* Establish procedures for data versioning and tracking changes over time.
* Develop disposal and secure destruction policies for obsolete data.
* Ensure compliance with legal, regulatory, and business requirements during data lifecycle management.

4. Reference-, Master- and Metadata Management Procedures:  Ensure consistency and reliability of shared, critical data entities across the organization to ensure interoperability, discoverability, traceability, and data understanding:
* Develop processes for defining and managing reference-, meta- and master data.
* Implement procedures for data harmonization, standardization, and integration.
* Define workflows for managing hierarchies, relationships, and mappings within data sets.
* Establish procedures for resolving discrepancies and ensuring data integrity.
* Develop procedures for integrating metadata with tools like data catalogs.
* Monitor metadata quality and its alignment with governance policies.

5. Data Integration and Interoperability Procedures: Enable seamless integration of data across disparate systems and ensure interoperability:
* Define processes for integrating data from diverse sources, ensuring consistency and alignment.
* Develop conflict resolution mechanisms for overlapping or duplicate data.
* Establish data transformation and mapping procedures to align with organizational needs.
* Define integration testing and validation procedures to ensure data integrity.

6. Data Quality Monitoring and Reporting Procedures: Provide continuous oversight of data assets and their alignment with governance frameworks:
* Establish monitoring procedures for data quality, access, and compliance.
* Define processes for creating and distributing dashboards, reports, and metrics.
* Develop escalation workflows for handling deviations and anomalies.
* Monitor the effectiveness of governance policies and update them as necessary.

7. Data Sharing and Usage Procedures: Facilitate appropriate data access and sharing within and outside the organization:
* Define policies and processes for data access requests and approvals.
* Establish procedures for creating and maintaining data-sharing agreements.
* Develop guidelines for tracking data usage and ensuring compliance with sharing policies.

elements.name: Data Risk Management Framework
elements.documentation: A structured approach designed to identify, assess, mitigate, and monitor risks associated with the management, processing, and usage of data within an organization. This framework ensures that data-related risks are systematically addressed, supporting organizational compliance, operational continuity, and informed decision-making. It integrates governance, processes, tools, and responsibilities to proactively manage data risks across the enterprise.

elements.name: Data Training Programme Plan
elements.documentation:
A strategic framework designed to develop a data-savvy workforce within the organization. It aligns with the organization's operational objectives, promoting data literacy, governance, and advanced analytics to support mission-critical decisions and enhance interoperability across the organization.
Objectives:
* Enhance data literacy across all organization's personnel levels.
* Build specialized skills in data management, analytics, and data governance.
* Promote interoperability by standardizing data training across all organizational entities.
* Develop capabilities to manage, analyze, and secure  the organization's critical data assets.

General Audience: All personnel requiring a foundational understanding of data literacy.
Specialized Roles:
* Data Stewards and Data Custodians: Focus on data governance, quality, and lifecycle management.
* Data Analysts: Training on analytical tools, visualization, and insights generation.
* Data Architects: Advanced training on designing and implementing data models and architectures.
* Command Staff and Decision-Makers: Training in interpreting data insights for strategic and operational decisions.
* IT and Cybersecurity Specialists: Training on data protection, privacy, and interoperability standards.

elements.name: Data Classification Framework
elements.documentation:
A formalized set of policies, standards, and procedures that serves as a rulebook for data stewards, analysts, and other stakeholders to ensure data is consistently arranged into homogeneous (similar) groups according to their common characteristics in alignment with business objectives, regulatory requirements, and risk management practices. It includes clear definitions of data categories so that all users understand the scope and implications of each metadata label, while outlining standardized procedures and precise criteria for examining, evaluating, and categorizing data. By incorporating role assignments and continuous improvement protocols, the Data Classification Framework provides a structured approach essential for effective data governance, ensuring data is classified objectively and consistently to support compliance and mitigate risk.

Example: When classifying data, the following criteria may be used:
* Data Domain: Categorize data by the business area or subject matter it represents—such as finance, marketing, operations, or customer relations—ensuring that data from different domains is consistently grouped.
* Data Ownership: Assign classifications based on data stewardship or ownership, which helps in tracking accountability and ensuring that the right stakeholders manage and maintain the data.
* Data Sensitivity: Although more security-focused, sensitivity classification can also be integrated to distinguish between public, unflassified, restricted, confidential, secret and top secret data, which supports proper protection and handling.
* Data Lifecycle Stage: Classify data by its stage in the lifecycle—raw, processed, archived, or purged—to facilitate efficient management and storage strategies.
* Data Quality: Assess and classify data based on quality dimensions like accuracy, completeness, consistency, timeliness, and validity. This helps in identifying datasets that meet required standards for analysis and reporting.
* Data Format and Structure: Distinguish between structured, semi-structured, and unstructured data, or by file types and formats (e.g., relational data, text, images, etc.) to determine the best storage and processing techniques.
* Usage Frequency and Criticality: Group data based on how frequently it is accessed and its importance to business operations, which supports prioritization in both maintenance and performance optimization.
* Geographical or Regional Origin: In cases where location matters (e.g., for regulatory reasons or market analysis), data can be classified by geographic region or the originating business unit.

elements.name: Data Strategy for the Alliance
elements.documentation:
The Data Strategy for the Alliance (DaSA) represents a comprehensive framework aimed at accelerating NATO's transition into a data centric organization, leveraging curated quality data for seamless interoperability and integration across all domains of operation. This strategy replaces the legacy NATO Network Enabled 
Capability (NNEC) Data Strategy and aims to redefine data management within NATO, challenging traditional distinctions between data and information. 
Aligned with NATO's Digital Transformation Implementation Strategy, the Data Exploitation Framework Strategic Plan, and the Data Centric Security Implementation Plan, the Data Strategy outlines clear objectives, scope, and structure, providing guidance for managing NATO data as a strategic asset and operationalizing its use for joint and multi-domain operations. Key components include a comprehensive definition of data, key data principles and supporting data enablers, and an effective data governance operating model.

elements.name: Alliance C3 Policy
elements.documentation: The Alliance Consultation, Command, and Control (C3) Policy, is a fundamental mechanism to ensure the Alliance’s Core Tasks are implemented coherently with the delivery of interoperable and modern C3 Capabilities and Information, and Communication Technology Services.

elements.name: Data Centric Reference Architecture for the Alliance
elements.documentation: The Data Centric Reference Architecture addresses data-centric capabilities developed or upgraded by Allies and the NATO Enterprise in support of NATO’s Digital Transformation. The principles, functions and federation interfaces described in the Data Centric Reference Architecture are applicable to NATO Nations and NATO Enterprise entities that aspire to deploy data intensive systems and services onto NATO’s Digital Backbone.

elements.name: Data Management Policy
elements.documentation: This Policy aims to support NATO in the execution of its tasks by setting the mandatory principles for the effective and efficient management of data and metadata, with a view to enhancing adequate quality, security, integrity, protection, availability, interoperability, understanding and effective use of data in the NATO Enterprise, in the Alliance, and in coalitions with Partners.

elements.name: A Data Quality Framework for the Alliance
elements.documentation:
The Data Quality (DQ) Framework for the Alliance sets the stage for a consistent approach for Allies, the NATO Enterprise, and Partners to assess, managing and improve data quality throughout the data lifecycle. The Framework covers a broad range of data types, acknowledging the blurred line between "data" and "information" in the context of evolving technologies.
It establishes common understanding regarding Data quality dimensions; Data quality metadata; and Data quality management, the combination of which will ensure data that is fit-for-purpose and fit-for-use.
In addition, the Framework provides guidance on practical tools and techniques to assess, communicate, and improve data quality throughout an organization. It stresses the importance of agreed metadata standards for communicating data
quality, continuous improvement, and technology enablers. The suite of tools and techniques will be subject to revision as new policies, directives, and standards continue to promote data quality efforts within the Alliance.
The Framework is intended for all parties involved in the generation, exchange, management or exploitation of data across Allies, the NATO Enterprise, and Partner Nations, including their implementation authorities, standardization bodies, decision makers, and data owners, custodians, and stewards.
The document recommends developing more detailed implementation plans for Allies and the NATO Enterprise, reviewing Data Exploitation and AI Priority Use-Cases, reviewing the Alliance Data-Centric Reference Architecture, and considering
data quality in the context of Machine Learning (ML) and Artificial Intelligence (AI).

elements.name: Data Labelling Roles and Processes
elements.documentation:
The Business Layer of a data labelling facility encompasses the roles, processes, and outputs essential for delivering high-quality labeled datasets for AI/ML applications. At its core, this layer orchestrates the data acquisition and preparation process, transforming raw datasets into structured and preprocessed formats ready for annotation. The data annotation and labeling process is the central operation where annotators, often supported by AI-assisted tools, label data such as text, images, audio, or video according to predefined guidelines. Ensuring accuracy and consistency, the quality assurance and validation process employs manual reviews, automated checks, and consensus scoring to create dependable datasets. Once validated, labeled datasets are organized into metadata catalogs for traceability and delivered to clients in formats compatible with AI/ML pipelines. Supporting these workflows, the compliance management process guarantees adherence to data privacy regulations and security standards, while the continual improvement process ensures ongoing refinement of workflows, tools, and annotation quality.

The roles within the business layer are specialized to support these processes effectively. Annotators perform the critical task of applying labels, while subject matter experts provide domain-specific knowledge for high-complexity or sensitive projects. Quality assurance specialists validate the outputs to ensure compliance with accuracy and consistency requirements, and data engineers handle the preprocessing of datasets and manage data pipelines. Client managers liaise with clients to capture project requirements and provide iterative feedback, while project managers oversee timelines, resource allocation, and overall workflow efficiency. Compliance officers ensure all operations adhere to legal and regulatory standards, particularly for sensitive or classified datasets. Together, these roles and processes produce key products such as labeled and quality-assured datasets, metadata catalogs, annotation guidelines, compliance reports, and workflow optimization outputs, all tailored to meet client requirements and industry standards.

elements.name: Data Security Specialist
elements.documentation: Someone responsible for implementing and monitoring physical and digital security measures to protect sensitive datasets.

elements.name: Subject Matter Expert
elements.documentation:
Subject Matter Experts (SMEs) provide domain-specific expertise for complex or high-stakes data labelling tasks, such as annotating imagery or video material. SMEs are professionals with specialized knowledge and experience in a particular field relevant to the data being labelled. They ensure that annotations are contextually accurate and align with the nuanced requirements of the domain.

Examples of SME Roles in Data Labeling:
* Medical Experts: Radiologists or pathologists annotate medical imagery like X-rays, MRIs, or histopathology slides for applications in diagnostic AI systems.
* Geospatial Analysts: Professionals with expertise in satellite imagery and remote sensing label geographic or environmental features for defense, disaster management, or urban planning.
* Security and Intelligence Specialists: Annotate video surveillance or aerial drone footage for object detection, activity recognition, or threat assessment.
* Linguists and Language Experts: For natural language processing tasks, linguists provide expertise in language-specific nuances, such as sentiment analysis, translation, or speech recognition.

elements.name: Training Data Quality Assurance Specialist
elements.documentation: Review and validate annotations to ensure consistency, accuracy, and adherence to guidelines.

elements.name: AI/ML Training Engineer
elements.documentation: Develops pre-labelling algorithms, active learning systems, or tools to assist with semi-automated labelling.

elements.name: Data Annotator/ Labeller
elements.documentation: Perform manual labelling tasks such as annotating text, tagging objects in images, or transcribing audio. Depending on the required domain-specific expertise, annotators may require specific training (e.g., medical annotators for radiology data).

elements.name: Training Data Acquisition and Preparation
elements.documentation: The Data Acquisition and Preparation Process focuses on collecting raw data from clients or external sources and preparing it for downstream annotation workflows. Data preparation includes activities such as formatting datasets into compatible structures, normalizing values to ensure consistency across inputs, and anonymizing sensitive information to comply with privacy standards. The result of this process is a structured and pre-processed dataset ready for annotation, ensuring that subsequent workflows operate efficiently and accurately.

elements.name: Data Annotation and Labelling
elements.documentation: The data annotation and labelling process is the central operation of the facility, where annotators apply labels to various types of data, including text, images, video, and audio. This process follows task-specific guidelines provided by the client or the facility and is supported by a combination of manual efforts and AI-assisted tools, depending on the complexity of the task. The outcome is a labelled dataset that serves as the foundation for training and validating AI/ML models.

elements.name: Quality Assurance and Validation
elements.documentation: The quality assurance and validation process ensures that labelled datasets meet the required standards of accuracy and consistency before being delivered to the client. Quality assurance involves manual reviews by experts, spot checks of annotations, and the application of automated validation scripts to detect and correct errors. The final output is a quality-assured dataset that aligns with client expectations and the specific requirements of the AI/ML model development pipeline.

elements.name: Training Dataset Dissemination
elements.documentation: Once datasets are labelled and validated, the dissemination process organizes them into a catalogue enriched with metadata, making them traceable and reusable. The labelled datasets are then converted into client-specified formats, or other required structures, and securely delivered to the client. This process ensures that the datasets are ready for immediate integration into AI/ML pipelines, facilitating seamless utilization.

elements.name: Compliance Management
elements.documentation: Compliance management ensures that all data labelling activities adhere to relevant data privacy regulations and security policies. This process includes implementing secure environments for data handling, role-based access controls, encryption, and rigorous audit trails to maintain confidentiality. It ensures that labelled datasets are compliant with regulatory frameworks and that sensitive information is handled securely throughout the labelling lifecycle.

elements.name: Continual Improvement
elements.documentation: The continual improvement process focuses on enhancing the overall efficiency and quality of labeling operations through iterative improvements. Annotators receive ongoing training to align with updated guidelines and project requirements, while client feedback and AI model performance data are analyzed to refine workflows. Active learning and AI-assisted pre-labeling tools may be employed to optimize repetitive tasks, further increasing productivity. The result is a continually improving process with refined guidelines and workflows that enhance accuracy, quality and scalability over time.

elements.name: Data Annotation and Labeling Applications
elements.documentation:
Data Annotation and Labeling Applications are are designed to facilitate the annotation of datasets across various modalities such as images, videos, text, audio, and 3D sensor data (e.g., LiDAR or radar). They provide the necessary user interfaces and workflows to label data accurately and efficiently. This application class bridges the gap between raw data and usable datasets by transforming unstructured data into labeled, high-quality datasets that meet the requirements for training AI/ML models. They are indispensable for domains requiring complex or high-accuracy annotations, particularly in environments with large-scale or multimodal data challenges.
These user applications are typically integrated into broader annotation platforms, offering flexibility, collaboration, and robust workflows tailored to the needs of the data labeling task. 

Some common sub-types of Data Annotation and Labeling Applications include:
* '''Image Annotation Applications:''' These applications enable annotators to label images using techniques such as bounding boxes, segmentation masks, keypoint annotations, and polygon annotations. 
* '''Video Annotation Applications:''' For video data, annotators use platforms that allow frame-by-frame labeling, object tracking, and temporal segmentation. These tools support efficient annotation of moving objects and temporal relationships within video streams.
* '''Text Annotation Applications:'''  Annotators working with text use specialized text annotation applications to perform tasks such as entity recognition, sentiment analysis, intent classification, and part-of-speech tagging. These applications often include features for collaborative annotation and NLP-specific workflows.
* '''Audio Annotation Applications:''' These tools allow annotators to transcribe spoken content, identify acoustic events, and tag specific segments of audio data. Advanced platforms may include features for annotating phonemes, speaker diarization, and background noise classification.
* '''Sensor Data Annotation Applications:''' For annotating data from LiDAR, radar, or other sensors, tools like Scale AI, Annotell, and Pointly provide functionality for point cloud labeling, 3D object detection, and spatial segmentation. These platforms are particularly useful in domains like autonomous driving and robotics.

elements.name: Accuracy and Quality Assurance
elements.documentation:
Ensure the highest level of accuracy, consistency, and quality in data labelling to enable effective AI training and minimize errors.

Implementation: Employ rigorous validation mechanisms, human-in-the-loop systems, and automated quality checks to maintain labelling precision and prevent inaccuracies from propagating through AI models.

elements.name: Bias Mitigation
elements.documentation:
Identify and eliminate potential biases in labelled training datasets to promote fairness and equity in AI outcomes.

Implementation: Regularly audit datasets for representational fairness, incorporate diverse annotator teams, and develop labelling guidelines that explicitly address sources of bias.

elements.name: Traceability and Explainability
elements.documentation:
Maintain traceable workflows and metadata documentation for all labelled datasets, ensuring transparency in the data preparation process.

Implementation: Log all labelling activities, decisions, and changes to datasets, making them accessible for audit trails and explaining the origins of training data.

elements.name: Security and Privacy
elements.documentation:
Protect sensitive information and ensure compliance with relevant security policies, privacy laws and regulations.

Implementation: Use secure environments, encryption, anonymization techniques, and access controls to handle sensitive data safely, especially when labelling classified or personally identifiable data.

elements.name: Responsibility and Accountability
elements.documentation:
Clearly define roles and accountability for annotators, reviewers, and systems to uphold ethical and operational standards.

Implementation: Establish guidelines for human oversight and involve domain experts for high-stakes labelling tasks, ensuring that errors can be traced back and corrected responsibly.

elements.name: Scalability and Interoperability
elements.documentation:
Design the capability to scale across diverse data modalities (e.g., images, videos, text, 3D point clouds) and ensure interoperability with AI/ML systems of customers of the training datasets (i.e. Allies and the NATO Enterprise).

Implementation: Develop modular workflows and adopt standard formats, APIs, and labelling taxonomies compatible with NATO standards and international frameworks.

elements.name: Ethical Alignment
elements.documentation:
Ensure that labelled data supports AI applications in a manner consistent with ethical guidelines and NATO’s Principles of Responsible Use (PRUs).
