Implementation: Regularly evaluate the ethical implications of datasets and AI systems developed using labelled data, ensuring alignment with values such as lawfulness and human oversight.

elements.name: Continuous Improvement
elements.documentation:
Establish feedback loops from AI systems and stakeholders to refine labelling processes and guidelines over time.

Implementation: Incorporate client feedback, error analyses, and active learning models to adapt labelling workflows and improve accuracy and efficiency.

elements.name: Compliance with recognized Standards
elements.documentation:
Adhere to relevant data and AI standards, such as ISO/IEC 23053 for AI training data, to ensure global alignment and credibility.

Implementation: Regularly review compliance with NATO, ISO, and other applicable standards for data labeling, ensuring datasets meet accepted best practices.

elements.name: Provide high-quality labelled datasets—essential for training and refining AI and ML models
Data Labelling Information and Data
elements.documentation: Finalized datasets formatted according to client specifications (e.g., JSON, CSV, or other machine-readable formats) and securely delivered to clients. These packages include labelled datasets and their associated metadata, ready for immediate use in AI/ML workflows.

elements.name: Annotation Guidelines
elements.documentation: Documentation detailing the labelling instructions, rules, and standards used during the annotation process. Annotation guidelines ensure consistency across annotators and projects and may be delivered to clients for transparency or as a reusable asset for future projects.

elements.name: Feedback and Refinement Records
elements.documentation: Iterative insights based on client feedback, model evaluation outcomes, and historical annotation data. These records document improvements made to workflows, guidelines, or datasets and may be shared with clients to demonstrate continuous optimization.

elements.name: Compliance Reports
elements.documentation: Documentation proving adherence to regulatory standards and data privacy laws, including audit trails, security measures, and compliance certifications. These reports provide clients with assurance that their sensitive data was handled securely and in accordance with applicable regulations.

elements.name: Quality-Assured Training Datasets
elements.documentation: High-accuracy labelled datasets that have passed rigorous quality assurance processes, including manual reviews, validation scripts, and error corrections. These datasets meet predefined accuracy and consistency standards, ensuring reliability for AI/ML applications.

elements.name: Labelled Datasets
elements.documentation: The primary product of the annotation process, these datasets are enriched with labels or annotations (e.g., bounding boxes for images, transcriptions for audio, or tags for text). Labelled datasets are the foundation for training, validating, and testing AI/ML models, tailored to the client’s specific use case.

elements.name: Pre-processed Datasets
elements.documentation: Structured and cleaned datasets that have undergone formatting, normalization, and anonymization during the data acquisition and preparation process. These datasets are ready for annotation workflows and ensure consistency, efficiency, and compatibility with labelling tools and client requirements.

elements.name: Data Product Custodian
elements.documentation:
A Data Product Custodian (aka Data Product Owner) is responsible for the creation, development, and ongoing maintenance of their assigned Data Product. With deep functional expertise, they hold long-term accountability for the security, quality, and integrity of the data and associated services. Data Product Custodians are dedicated to the success and longevity of their products, acting as the primary spokesperson and point of contact for the data product team. They maintain end-to-end accountability for the delivery, ensuring that the data product meets organizational standards and consumer needs.

==========

From IC RA:
A Data Product Custodian is responsible for the maintenance of each assigned Data Product within the Data Domain. May perform mission and business data-related tasks such as collecting, tagging, and processing data, and granting individual users access to additional information beyond that of general systems, applications, and file permissions to perform such functions, where appropriate. The Data Custodian does not assume the legal or policy roles of the Originating Element.

elements.name: Data Product Release Manager
elements.documentation: Manages, communicates, and socializes the overall roadmap and evolution of the data product

elements.name: Metadata Manager
elements.documentation: Defines, governs, and manages metadata and manages data product semantics, glossary, and knowledge graph.

elements.name: Data & Security Manager
elements.documentation: Someone responsible for managing, protecting, and governing data in the data product.

elements.name: Data Services Manager
elements.documentation: Someone responsible for building, supporting, and evolving the services needed to collect data required for building the data product, and also for disseminating the Data Product.

elements.name: Data Product
elements.documentation: A Data Product is a self-contained, domain-owned, and fully governed data asset designed to deliver high-quality, discoverable, and reusable data that aligns with organizational and domain data models. It integrates datasets, structured metadata, and potentially code, such as pipelines, APIs, and transformations, supported by infrastructure to enable its creation, deployment, and management. Each data product is semantically rich, interoperable, and adheres to open standards to ensure usability across organizational boundaries. Governed collaboratively by Data Product Teams, Data Stewards, and consumers, its lifecycle is managed to maintain compliance with standardized governance practices while addressing specific domain and organizational needs. By combining discoverability, trustworthiness, and reusability, data products form the cornerstone of scalable, interoperable, and collaborative data ecosystems.

elements.name: Data Domain
elements.documentation: A Data Domain, also known as a subject area, is a logical grouping of related data aligned with a specific business area or function. Managed by experts with specialized functional expertise, it provides a framework for organizing, governing, and producing data products that ensure consistency, clarity, and compliance with regulatory requirements and standards while enabling effective information sharing.

elements.name: Allied Data Domains
elements.documentation: To support interoperability and effective data governance in a joint or multinational context, data domains are closely aligned with joint function and staff frameworks defined by Allied Joint Doctrine that assists commanders to integrate actions through the five operational domains (Air, Land, Maritime, Cyberspace and Space). These allied data domains facilitate efficient data management, governance, and integration to support decision-making and operational effectiveness.

elements.name: Command and Control (C2)
elements.documentation: All data required for supporting the processes and systems used for situational awareness, decision-making, mission planning, and operational coordination.

elements.name: Cyberspace Operations
elements.documentation: Handles data related to cyber operations, including cyber defense and offense.

elements.name: Space Operations
elements.documentation: Addresses data associated with space-based operations, including satellite coordination, space situational awareness, and space control activities.

elements.name: Air Operations
elements.documentation: Covers data related to air operations, including air mission planning, flight operations, airspace management, and air support.

elements.name: Maritime Operations
elements.documentation: Focuses on data for naval and maritime activities, including fleet management, maritime security, and sea-based logistics.

elements.name: Electromagnetic Operations
elements.documentation: Focuses on data for electromagnetic operations, spectrum management, and signal intelligence.

elements.name: Land Operations
elements.documentation: Encompasses data for land-based operations, such as maneuver planning, force deployment, and ground combat activities.

elements.name: Sustainment
elements.documentation: The support necessary to maintain and extend operational capabilities.

elements.name: Medical
elements.documentation: Contains medical records, casualty management data, and healthcare logistics.

elements.name: Personnel and Administration
elements.documentation: Focuses on human resources, personnel records, and administrative processes.

elements.name: Military Engineering
elements.documentation: Contains data on construction projects, fortifications, and mobility support.

elements.name: Budget and Finance
elements.documentation: Covers financial planning, budget execution, and cost analysis.

elements.name: Logistics
elements.documentation: Encompasses supply chain data, equipment readiness, transportation, and sustainment operations.

elements.name: Enablement
elements.documentation: Focuses on data required and generated for the preparation, support, and other foundational systems required to make operations feasible and effective.

elements.name: Force Protection
elements.documentation: Includes data related to personnel safety, physical security, and threat mitigation efforts.

elements.name: Training and Readiness
elements.documentation: Includes training schedules, readiness assessments, and certification data.

elements.name: Communication and Information Systems (CIS)
elements.documentation: Encompasses data and metadata associated with the planning, operation, and coordination of communication and information systems (CIS), and information and communications technology (ICT) service management.

elements.name: Environmental
elements.documentation: Addresses Meteorological and Oceanographic data and data related to terrain analysis, and environmental conditions affecting operations.

elements.name: Strategic Communications
elements.documentation: Includes data supporting public affairs, information campaigns, strategic communications, information environment assessment, and influence activities.

elements.name: Civil-Military Co-operation (CIMIC)
elements.documentation: Encompasses data for coordinating with civilian agencies, NGOs, and local populations.

elements.name: Intelligence and ISR
elements.documentation: Covers data related to intelligence, surveillance, reconnaissance (ISR), and threat analysis.

elements.name: Geospatial
elements.documentation: Encompasses a range of spatial data and information that represents real-world geographic features and locations, including vector data (points, lines, and polygons), raster imagery from satellites and aerial sources, digital elevation models, topographic maps, and precise geodetic coordinates. The data managed by this domain enables the visualization and analysis of spatial relationships.

elements.name: Manage Data Product Lifecycle
elements.documentation: The Manage Data Product Life Cycle process in a Data Mesh governs the creation, publication, maintenance, and retirement of data products, ensuring they are high-quality, discoverable, and compliant with governance standards. This process fosters collaboration between data producers and consumers to design products that meet both domain-specific and cross-domain needs. It leverages metadata-driven tools for versioning, monitoring, and enforcing policies related to security, access control, and compliance. By treating data as a product, the process ensures data assets remain reliable, relevant, and aligned with organizational goals, supporting the evolution of trusted and interoperable data ecosystems.

elements.name: Maintain Data Product
elements.documentation:
* Periodically review and update metadata, quality metrics, and SLAs.
* Monitor usage and collect consumer feedback for improvements.
* Address any identified issues or inconsistencies.

elements.name: Design Data Product
elements.documentation:
* Define requirements based on domain-specific and cross-domain needs.
* Identify data sources and metadata standards.
* Collaborate with consumers and governance bodies for alignment.

elements.name: Publish Data Product
elements.documentation:
* Attach metadata, access policies, and compliance tags.
* Ensure discoverability and usability through indexing and semantic alignment.
* Register the product in federated data catalogs.

elements.name: Data Resource
elements.documentation: A data resource is a collection of data objects or a data service designed to provide information required by consumers.

elements.name: Data Service (description)
elements.documentation: A description of a technical service or interface that provides access to data resource or insights designed to provide value required by consumers.

elements.name: Dataset
elements.documentation: A data set is a collection of data objects designed to provide information required by consumers.

elements.name: DM Data Product Offer
elements.documentation:
Data Products have associated Data Product Offers to broker effective discovery and access for data consumers.
Data Product Offers provide Data Producers with a formal means to express to Data Consumers how the data is formatted, structured, and made accessible. Data Product Offers define the structure, format, semantics, quality, and typical terms of use for exchanging data between a data provider and their consumers

Data Product Agreements outline the specific rules and expectations of the Data Product. They express the necessary protocols and/or applications required to consume the Data Product. The Data Product Agreements help manage dependencies and provide insights into data usage. They also help ensure that Data Products are consumed responsibly in alignment with data producer objectives as well as enterprise-level and local policies.

elements.name: DM Data Product Offer
elements.documentation:
A concrete set of rules, duties, and obligations that define the terms of use for a specific Data Product.
A Data Product Offer is a critical component supporting a Data Product, particularly in federated data-sharing environments. A DM Data Product Offer


 It serves as a formalized mechanism for specifying the structure, format, semantics, quality, and terms of access associated with the data, ensuring seamless interoperability between data producers and consumers. In federated environments, where data resides across multiple domains, the Data Product Offer facilitates effective discovery and consistent access, providing clarity on usage protocols and governance compliance. This enables trusted, scalable data exchanges while maintaining alignment with organizational policies and domain-specific requirements.

elements.name: Open Data Product Specification (ODPS) 3.0
elements.documentation: The Open Data Product Specification (ODPS) is a vendor-neutral, open-source machine-readable data product metadata model. It defines the objects and attributes as well as the structure of digital data products. The work is based on existing standards (schema.org), best practices and emerging concepts like Data Mesh.  ODPS 3.0 changes the data product metadata model towards a standalone model, which helps to decouple data product from the systems often directly associated with it. With help of the ODPS data product can be presented and described to the customer also as such without any need for a dataspace or other systems.

elements.name: Conform with NATO Data Product Profile
Data Catalog Vocabulary (DCAT) - Version 3
elements.documentation:
DCAT is an RDF vocabulary designed to facilitate interoperability between data catalogs published on the Web. This document defines the schema and provides examples for its use.
DCAT enables a publisher to describe datasets and data services in a catalog using a standard model and vocabulary that facilitates the consumption and aggregation of metadata from multiple catalogs. This can increase the discoverability of datasets and data services. It also makes it possible to have a decentralized approach to publishing data catalogs and makes federated search for datasets across catalogs in multiple sites possible using the same query mechanism and structure. Aggregated DCAT metadata can serve as a manifest file as part of the digital preservation process.

elements.name: Data Product Descriptor Specification (DPDS) 1.0.0
elements.documentation: The Data Product Descriptor Specification (DPDS) defines a declarative and technology-independent standard to describe a data product in all its components. It allows human agents (e.g. analysts, data scientists, etc..) and digital agents (e.g. other data products, BI tools, planes of the underlying data mesh ops platform, etc..) to operate, discover and access a data product. When properly defined, an external agent can understand and interact with the data product with a minimal amount of cognitive load and implementation logic.

elements.name: Platform Monitoring Services
elements.documentation: The Platform Monitoring Services provide functions to gather information on the actual utilization and performance of monitored Platform Services. These services monitor service communication based on service calls and message exchange to identify performance issues and determine current availability in order to ensure that any failures are detected proactively, isolated, analyzed, and resolved with as little impact on the end user as possible.

elements.name: Data Platform Performance Services
elements.documentation: Data Platform Performance Services provide comprehensive functions to monitor, analyze, and report on the performance and utilization of data platform components. These services track data product usage, pipeline interactions, and service communications to proactively identify performance issues, detect failures, and evaluate system availability. By integrating real-time monitoring with advanced analytics, these services offer insights into operational efficiency, pinpoint areas for improvement, and identify redundancies or gaps in the platform’s structure, enabling continuous optimization while ensuring the platform remains scalable and reliable.

elements.name: Data Flow Monitoring Services
elements.documentation: The Data Flow Monitoring Service provides comprehensive visibility into the movement and transformation of data across various components of the data platform. It continuously tracks data as it flows through data ingestion services and ETL pipelines and data repositories (i.e. data warehouses and data lakes), ensuring that data is accurately processed, transformed, and delivered in real-time. This service identifies bottlenecks, delays, or errors in data pipelines, offering actionable insights to optimize performance and reliability. By monitoring data lineage, it ensures traceability of data across its lifecycle, while also supporting anomaly detection and reporting to quickly address potential disruptions. The service helps maintain data quality, consistency, and compliance with governance policies by ensuring that data flows meet predefined standards and operational expectations.

elements.name: Data Access Services
elements.documentation:
Data Access Services allows for the consumption of data, ensuring the right policies as determined by Data Access Governance (DAG) is implemented. DAG determines, manages and monitors who has access to which data and how that data is classified, as well as provide an audit trail of access and permission activities. 

Two possible approaches are attribute-based and role-based access control. 
* Attribute-based access control (ABAC) is a logical access control methodology where authorization to access data is determined by evaluating attributes (metadata) associated with the subject, object, requested operations, and, in some cases, environment conditions against policy, rules, or relationships that describe the allowable operations for a given set of attributes. 
* Role-based access control (RBAC) is a policy-neutral access-control mechanism defined around roles and privileges. The permissions to perform certain operations are assigned to specific roles. Members or staff (or other system users) are assigned particular roles, and through those role assignments acquire the permissions needed to access data. 

Enforcement can take place within identity and access infrastructure or through dedicated Policy Enforcement/Control Points.

elements.name: Data Dictionary Services
elements.documentation:
Data Dictionary Services provide an organized, centralized platform for managing technical metadata—the definitions, names, and attributes of data elements within an information system or database. These services are designed to standardize how data is described and understood across an organization, ensuring a consistent interpretation of data elements within a specific context. By storing and documenting metadata related to the structure and meaning of data, Data Dictionary Services support both technical and non-technical users in comprehending the data they work with, aiding in more accurate data analysis, integration, and governance. In addition to managing the basic attributes of data (such as names, types, formats, and relationships), these services play a vital role in ensuring data quality, supporting data discovery, and facilitating the interoperability of data systems by clearly defining the accepted meanings and representations of each data element. Data Dictionary Services serve as a critical component of a data platform, providing a consistent, context-specific understanding of data elements. By managing technical metadata, they support data governance, compliance, and discovery efforts while facilitating data integration and interoperability across diverse systems. These services also integrate features of Metadata Registry and Repository Services, enhancing their scope and utility in the broader data management ecosystem.

Key Features:
* Technical Metadata Management: Data dictionaries provide a comprehensive collection of metadata that describes the technical aspects of data elements, such as field names, data types, formats, constraints, and relationships. This facilitates a clear understanding of the data being used across different systems.
* Context-Specific Definitions: These services store metadata that defines how each data element is used within specific databases or information systems, offering guidance on the meaning, representation, and interpretation of data in various operational contexts.
* Data Governance and Compliance Support: By maintaining detailed and accurate metadata, Data Dictionary Services ensure adherence to governance policies, including data privacy, access control, and compliance with regulations. They serve as an essential tool for auditing data usage and for supporting governance frameworks.
* Data Discovery and Search: Offers users the ability to search for and identify relevant data elements based on their metadata, enhancing data discoverability. By providing descriptions and definitions, data dictionaries allow users to understand and select the right data for their needs.
* Integration with Metadata Registry and Repository Services: These services also function as a form of metadata registry, storing and managing metadata consistently across an organization. They enable efficient data integration, lineage tracking, and mapping across systems, facilitating better data management practices.
* Change Management and Versioning: Provides mechanisms for tracking changes to data element definitions and attributes over time. This ensures that any changes in how data is defined, used, or interpreted are documented and reflected within the system, supporting version control and transparency.

elements.name: Domain Ownership/ Decentralized Stewardship
elements.documentation: This principle assigns responsibility for data to domain teams, ensuring the ownership of both analytical and operational data from a rests with the originating domains. Each domain, representing a specific area or function, is accountable for managing its own data, ensuring it is properly governed and conditioned for organizational use. Domain owners, typically the entities closest to the data sources with the most knowledge of the data and its consumers' needs, establish protections, sharing protocols, and governance standards within their domain. Data Domains create and deliver Data Products that are well-defined, accessible, trustworthy, interoperable, and secure, supporting both local and organizational data requirements.

elements.name: Data as a Product
elements.documentation: This principle applies product-thinking to data sharing, treating domain data as a high-quality, reusable, and discoverable asset that serves both internal and external consumers. Data domains are responsible for providing data that meets the needs of other domains, much like a public API. Each data product should be self-contained, governed, and designed for interoperability, ensuring it is trustworthy, accessible, and aligns with organizational goals. Managed by dato product teams, including domain experts and data stewards, data products must adhere to open standards to maximize scalability, usability, and integration across organizational boundaries.

elements.name: Self-Service Data Platform
elements.documentation: This principle adopts platform thinking to provide domain-agnostic tools, systems, and services that enable domain teams to seamlessly create, consume, and manage interoperable data products. Managed by a dedicated data platform team, the data platform supports enterprise-wide data discovery, federated governance, and monitoring. Domain teams publish their Data Products to an enterprise data catalog, and also contributing selected Data Products to Data Spaces within a broader data sharing ecosystem. By offering critical services through the organization’s Digital Backbone, the platform empowers domain teams to develop data products with the necessary IT infrastructure and capabilities while ensuring consistent governance and collaboration.

elements.name: Federated Computational Data Governance
elements.documentation: This principle promotes federated governance across the data mesh and wider data sharing ecosystem to ensure interoperability and compliance with organizational rules and industry regulations. By standardizing governance practices through automation, this principle enables a trustworthy, secure, and interoperable data environment, allowing local domain teams to adapt standards to their specific needs. It requires collaboration between data domain representatives and platform experts from security, compliance, legal, and other areas. Computational governance leverages software code to automate compliance processes, ensuring consistency, transparency, and scalability while improving agility and efficiency in maintaining governance. This approach, informed by open standards, ensures that data products within the ecosystem align with legal, policy, and security requirements while enabling cross-domain data insights.

elements.name: Mesh performance monitoring
elements.documentation: Mesh performance monitoring utilizes tools for properly computing quality metrics on the performance of the mesh by conducting behavior analytic data stream analytics to allow performance optimization and asset value determination, allowing the mesh to self-identify aberrations and, when possible, to be self-healing.

elements.name: Manage Data Platform Capabilities
elements.documentation: The Manage Data Platform Capabilities process in a Data Mesh ensures the development, deployment, and maintenance of self-service infrastructure to support decentralized data ownership and governance. It provides tools and services for data integration, quality management, cataloging, and discovery, while enforcing governance standards like metadata compliance and access control. This process enables domains to independently create and manage data products by leveraging shared, scalable capabilities, including API management, automated data pipeline orchestration, and observability for SLA monitoring. By balancing domain autonomy with Alliance-wide interoperability, this process ensures efficient, secure, and standardized data platform operations.

elements.name: Domain Data Owner
elements.documentation:
Responsible for managing data assets within a domain, ensuring their quality and compliance.
Oversees the creation and maintenance of data products.

elements.name: Data Governance Policy
elements.documentation:
In the context of a NATO Data Mesh, Data Governance Policies encompass rules, practices, and standards that guide how data is managed, accessed, and used across decentralized domains while maintaining organizational-wide alignment and compliance. It ensures that data products are consistently high-quality, secure, and interoperable, supporting decentralized ownership while aligning with overarching governance objectives. Data Governance Policies facilitate collaboration between federated teams by balancing domain-specific autonomy with global alignment, creating a trusted environment for data sharing and innovation. They support a scalable and adaptive governance model that evolves with organizational needs and technological advancements.

Key aspects include policies for:
* Access Control: Defining how users and systems securely access data based on roles and permissions.
* Metadata Standards: Mandating the inclusion of comprehensive metadata to ensure data discoverability and semantic interoperability.
* Quality Assurance: Setting benchmarks for data accuracy, completeness, and reliability.
* Compliance: Ensuring data handling adheres to regulatory requirements, such as legal, regulatory, and ethical guidelines.
* Lifecycle Management: Providing guidelines for creating, publishing, maintaining, and retiring data products.

elements.name: Widely implemented NATO Data standards that underpin interoperability across the Alliance.
Enhanced data quality and traceability across all data assets.
Improved collaboration within the Alliance and external entities, leading to efficient data-driven decision-making.
Collaborative development through both top‐down multinational consensus and bottom‐up expert proposals is essential.
Semantic interoperability is achieved through the use of common ontologies, taxonomies, and controlled vocabularies.
Data embedded within standards must be accessible, efficiently organized, and machine-readable.
Modern standards should support both operational and materiel requirements while being flexible enough to accommodate emerging technologies.
Standards must be quality managed, ensuring traceability, consistency, and continuous improvement.
Compliance with legal, regulatory, and international frameworks is non-negotiable.
NATO’s strategic objective to achieve interoperability and enhanced data-driven decision-making.
The need to harmonize data across diverse operational and functional areas in support of MDO.
The drive to leverage multinational expertise and industry partnerships in standardization efforts.
Regulatory and compliance requirements that demand consistent and secure data management.
A cohesive and agile data ecosystem that enables seamless and semantically enriched data exchange.
Identify Standardization Requirements
elements.documentation: The Standardization Requirements Identification Process gathers and refines standardization needs using both top‐down tasks and bottom‐up proposals from NATO expert groups. This process produces Information Requirements (IRs) that form the basis for further data standardization activities.

elements.name: Committee for Standardization
elements.documentation:
The Committee for Standardization (CS) is the senior NATO committee for Alliance standardization, composed primarily of representatives from all NATO countries. Operating under the authority of the North Atlantic Council (NAC), it issues policy and guidance for all NATO standardization activities. Its mission is to exert domain governance for standardization policy and management within the Alliance to contribute to Allies’ development of interoperable and cost-effective military forces and capabilities.

As the senior body responsible for coordinating standardization activities across the Alliance, the Committee for Standardization steers the development of the NATO policy for standardization and monitors its implementation. It facilitates the development, maintenance, management and implementation of NATO standards.

The Committee provides coordinated advice on overall standardization matters to the NAC, the Alliance’s principal political decision-making body.  It also provides standardization guidance and procedures to all NATO bodies as needed.

elements.name: Subject Matter Experts (SMEs)
elements.documentation: Subject Matter Experts (SMEs) contribute domain-specific expertise.

elements.name: Standards Custodian
elements.documentation: The Standards Custodianis responsible for translating finalized requirements into Information Exchange Specifications.

elements.name: Harmonize Data Standards
elements.documentation: The Data Harmonization Process maps domain-specific data standards to the common semantic framework provided by the CXCSRM, ensuring interoperability across Communities of Interest.

elements.name: Create Standardization Solution
elements.documentation: The Standards Development and Ratification Process transforms refined requirements into Information Exchange Specifications (IES) and culminates in ratified standards—such as STANAGs and Allied Publications—published by the NATO Standardization Office (NSO).

elements.name: Maintain Standards
elements.documentation: The Standard Maintenance and Review Process ensures that all standards remain current and relevant through continuous monitoring, validation, and feedback.

elements.name: Domain Upper Ontology for Defence and Security (CXCSRM)
Version Control Services
elements.documentation: Version Control Services help manage changes to source code over time, enabling multiple contributors to work on a project collaboratively. Version Control Services tracks changes to files, maintains a historical record of modifications, and allows users to revert to previous versions if needed.

elements.name: NATOTerms 2.0
elements.documentation: Acts as the central online platform that manages NATO’s lexicon and all semantic resources—including ontologies, taxonomies, and code lists—to maintain consistent, enriched metadata.

elements.name: Online Standards Development (OSD) Service
elements.documentation: Provides a collaborative digital environment for drafting, reviewing, and publishing SMART standards with intuitive authoring tools, guided structure, and automatic quality checks.

elements.name: Conformance Test Platform (INSPECT) Service
elements.documentation: The INSPECT Conformance Test Service enables vendors to automatically validate that their products and services conform to NATO data standards through an online testing platform.

elements.name: NATO Standardization Documents Database (NSDD)
elements.documentation: The NSDD Serves as the authoritative, web-based repository for all approved NATO standards, ensuring they are accessible and maintained for reference and compliance.

elements.name: Data Asset
elements.documentation: Content exposed for exchange via Data Endpoints according to a parametrized Data Service interface; Data Assets are expected to be focused, homogeneous, and consistent over time with regard to granularity, coverage, context, data structure, and conceptual classification.

elements.name: Data Contract
elements.documentation: A data contract defines the structure, format, semantics, quality, and terms of use for exchanging data between a data provider and their consumers. A data contract is implemented by a data product’s output port or other data technologies. Data contracts can also be used for the input port to specify the expectations of data dependencies and verify given guarantees.

elements.name: Access Policy
elements.documentation: Defines which Participants have access to the contained Data Asset.

elements.name: Data Usage Policy
elements.documentation: Defines the duties, obligations and rules that apply to the use of the contained Data Asset.

elements.name: Contract Definition
elements.documentation:
As a template for Data Contracts, a Contract Definition specifies
- the Access Policy on a Data Asset - which Participants have access to (e.g., "my partners", "any organization in Europe", "anyone", etc.)
- the Usage Policy - duties, rules, and obligations one must follow (e.g., "store data in Europe", "delete the data after 30 days", etc.)
A Contract Definition can be associated with many Data Assets.

elements.name: Connector
elements.documentation: A system that performs contract negotiation and asset sharing (data transfer or compute-to-data) on behalf of a participant.

elements.name: Application
elements.documentation: A custom system that performs some role in the dataspace. For example, a supply-chain parts tracking service.

elements.name: Federated Catalog Node
elements.documentation: A system that publishes assets provided by a participant in a dataspace. Publishing makes the assets available for discovery. A participant may choose to make an asset available to a subset of other participants using an access policy and impose usage requirements with an usage policy.

elements.name: Federated Catalog Crawler
elements.documentation: A system that discovers assets published by other participants in a dataspace. The result of a crawling operation is a collection of assets the crawling participant has access to. Access is determined by the provider participant and may include evaluation of access policy and usage policy against a set of verifiable credentials.

elements.name: NATO Data Space Catalog Content Profile
Offer
elements.documentation: A concrete Policy associated with a specific Dataset.

elements.name: DS::Agreement
elements.documentation:
A concrete Usage Policy associated with a specific Dataset that has been signed by both the provider and consumer Dataspace Participants. An (electronic data sharing) Agreement is a result of a Contract Negotiation and is associated with exactly one Dataset.

A Electronic Data Sharing Agreement (EDSA) comprises a Usage Policy that describes certain permission or obligation of a Data Space Resource. Electronic Data Sharing Agreements are expressed in a machine-readable format (according to the Dataspace's Usage Policy Language) and are verified as part of the establishment of a data transfer. In any case, a EDSA must always be regarded as an extension of an existing legal agreement between two Dataspace Participants, which can be overruled by them. As the Dataspace technology stack cannot sufficiently interpret legal texts, any EDSA must always be in line with the concluded Data Sharing Contract. Each contract between Dataspace Participants consists of a technical part and a non-technical part. The technical part focuses on the description of technical interfaces (Application Programming Interfaces) and the Usage Policy. The non-technical part focuses on legal aspects of the intended data exchange. For automatic negotiation of contracts and conditions standard contractual clauses and conditions are necessary (but not yet available today).

elements.name: DS::Agreement
elements.documentation:
Contractual agreement between a Data Provider and a Data Consumer regarding the exchange of data in the International Data Spaces.

A concrete Policy associated with a specific Dataset that has been signed by both the Provider and consumer Participants. An Agreement is a result of a Contract Negotiation and is associated with exactly one Dataset.

elements.name: Dataset
elements.documentation: Data or a technical service that can be shared by a Participant.

elements.name: DS::Offer
elements.documentation: A concrete set of rules, duties, and obligations that define the terms of use for a specific Dataset.

elements.name: dcat:Catalog
elements.documentation: A curated collection of metadata about resources.

elements.name: dcat:DataService
elements.documentation: A collection of operations that provides access to one or more datasets or data processing functions.

elements.name: dcat:Dataset
elements.documentation: A collection of data, published or curated by a single agent, and available for access or download in one or more representations.

elements.name: odrl:Offer
elements.documentation:
A Policy that proposes a Rule over an Asset from an assigner.
An Offer Policy MUST contain at least one Permission or Prohibition rule and a Party with Assigner function (in the same Permission or Prohibition). The Offer Policy MAY contain a Party with Assignee function, but MUST not grant any privileges to that Party.

elements.name: odrl:hasPolicy
elements.documentation: An ODRL conformant policy expressing the rights associated with the resource.

elements.name: dcat:Distribution
elements.documentation:
A specific representation of a dataset. A dataset might be available in multiple serializations that may differ in various ways, including natural language, media-type or format, schematic organization, temporal and spatial resolution, level of detail or profiles (which might specify any or all of the above).
Note: This represents a general availability of a dataset. It implies no information about the actual access method of the data, i.e., whether by direct download, API, or through a Web page. The use of dcat:downloadURL property indicates directly downloadable distributions.

elements.name: odrl:Policy
elements.documentation:
A non-empty group of Permissions and/or Prohibitions.
A Policy may contain multiple Rules.

elements.name: dcat:Resource
elements.documentation: A record describing the registration of a single resource (e.g., a dataset, a data service) that is part of the catalog.

elements.name: dcterms:title
elements.documentation: A name given to the resource.

elements.name: Data Provider
elements.documentation: A Participant Agent that offers a Data Resource.

elements.name: DS::Contract Negotiation
elements.documentation: A Contract Negotiation (CN) involves two parties, a Provider that offers one or more Datasets under a usage contract and Consumer that requests Datasets. A CN is uniquely identified through an IRI. Each CN requires a newly generated IRI, which may not be used in a CN after a terminal state has been reached. A CN progresses through a series of states, which are tracked by the Provider and Consumer using messages. A CN transitions to a state in response to an acknowledged message from the counter-party. Both parties have the same state of the CN. In case the states differ, the CN is terminated and a new CN has to be initiated.

elements.name: Conform with NATO Data Space Contract Negotiation Profile
Data Sharing Contract
elements.documentation: A legally valid Data Sharing Contract is the foundation of any business transaction. A Data Space cannot, and does not intend to, replace legal contracts or licensing agreements. Instead, the DS provides a technical framework for technically enforced data sharing agreements in addition to existing, legally binding contracts.

elements.name: Data Space Registration completed
elements.documentation: Identity registration of Provider & Consumer completed, dataspace connectors are configured incl. certificates.

elements.name: Usage Contract established
elements.documentation: Usage Contract between Provider & Consumer for sharing Data Asset established.

elements.name: DS::Catalog Entry
elements.documentation: A single entry in a DS Catalog representing a Data Product (incl. Dataset or Data Service) and its Offers that is advertised by a Data Provider participant.

elements.name: DS::Contract Offer
elements.documentation: Contract Offers are dynamically generated and contain the relevant dataset(s) and the usage policy/Electronic Data Sharing Agreement (EDSA).

elements.name: Asset
elements.documentation: Metadata of a Dataset or Service Interface that describes the dataset that could be shared.

elements.name: transfer data
elements.documentation: A set of interactions between a Provider and Consumer that give access to a Dataset under the terms of an Agreement. It is an instantiation of the state machine of a Transfer Process Protocol.

elements.name: Conform with NATO Data Space Transfer Protocol Profile
Certification Authority
elements.documentation: Governance body certifying components and entities seeking admission to the International Data Spaces; aside from having the final word on granting or denying a certificate, it is responsible for maintaining the Certification Scheme (including its catalog of requirements), overseeing and approval of Evaluation Facilities, and ensuring compatibility of evaluation procedures carried out by Evaluation Facilities.

elements.name: Data Space Participant
elements.documentation: A Dataspace Participant is a member of one or more Dataspaces that provides and/or consumes Datasets. A Dataspace Participant registers Participant Agents that perform tasks on its behalf.

elements.name: Vocabulary Provider
elements.documentation: Provides access to metadata about available data resources, enriched using standardized vocabularies. Acts as a central repository and registry for participants to discover shared vocabularies and metadata descriptions.

elements.name: Clearing House
elements.documentation: The Clearing House is an intermediary that provides clearing and settlement services for all financial and data exchange transactions. In the International Data Spaces, clearing activities are separated from any broker services, since these activities are technically different from maintaining a metadata repository. As already stated above, it might still be possible that the role Clearing House and other intermediary roles are assumed by the same organization, as both roles require acting as a trusted intermediary between the Data Supplier and the Data Customer.

elements.name: Data Provider
elements.documentation: Entity making data technically available in a Dataspace for being transmitted to a Data Customer on behalf of the Data Owner. To submit metadata to a Metadata Broker, or exchange data with a Data Consumer, the Data Provider uses software components that are compliant with the Data Centric Reference Architecture for the Alliance.

elements.name: Service Broker
elements.documentation: Intermediary managing a metadata repository that provides information about the Data Sources available in the International Data Spaces; multiple Broker Service Providers may be around at the same time, maintaining references to different, domain-specific subsets of Data Endpoints.

elements.name: DS::Core Participants
elements.documentation:
Core Participants are involved and required every time data is exchanged in the Allied Data Spaces. Roles assigned to this category are Data Supplier and Data Customer. The role of a Core Participant can be assumed by any organization that owns, wants to provide, and/or wants to consume or use data.

Benefit for participants in the Allied Data Spaces is created by these roles as they create, potentially own and possibly provide data as well as receive, process and most likely at some point in time delete data.

elements.name: DS::Intermediaries
elements.documentation: Intermediaries act as trusted entities and are commonly considered as "platforms". They assume a rather central role compared to the great number of data suppliers and customers, though multiple, especially competitive platforms of the same role may and shall exist.

elements.name: Data Intermediary
elements.documentation:
A platform operator that assumes mainly the data-related basic roles Data Provider/Data Consumer and Metadata Broker.
Assuming the basic role of a Data Provider or Data Consumer, the Data Intermediary is responsible for the execution of the data exchange on behalf of the Data Owner or User respectively. Providing a Data Consumer with data is, hence, the main activity of the Data Provider.
To facilitate a data request from a Data Consumer, the Data Intermediary would provide a Data Broker with proper metadata about the data. Acting as a Metadata Broker, the Data Intermediary stores and manages information about the data sources available in the Allied Data Spaces. An organization offering metadata brokering in the Allied Data Spaces may assume other intermediary basic roles at the same time (e.g. Service Broker, Clearing House or Identity Authority, see below). Assuming further basic roles consequently means additional tasks a participant has to execute.
The activities of the Metadata Broker mainly focus on receiving and providing metadata. The Metadata Broker must provide an interface for Data Creators to send their metadata. The metadata should be stored in an internal repository for being queried by Data Consumers in a structured manner. After the Metadata Broker has provided the Data Consumer with the metadata about a certain Data Provider, it is not involved in the subsequent data exchange process.

elements.name: Identity Provider
elements.documentation: Intermediary offering services to create, maintain, manage and validate identity information of and for Participants in the International Data Spaces.

elements.name: Credential Issuer
elements.documentation: A Credential Issuer issues verifiable credentials used by Participant Agents to allow access to Datasets and verify usage control.

elements.name: Service Intermediary
elements.documentation: A platform operator providing metadata on services, the services itself (i.e. app including computing time as a trustee), or both.

elements.name: Data Customer
elements.documentation: Core Participant in Dataspaces and Data Meshes requesting data and data products provided by a Data Provider. Data Consumer utilize data products for analytics, decision-making, or operational processes and provide feedback to improve data quality and relevance.

elements.name: Vocabulary Provider
elements.documentation: Acts as a central repository and registry for participants to discover shared vocabularies and metadata descriptions. Provides maintenance facilities for editing, browsing and downloading vocabularies and related documents; mirrors a set of external third-party vocabularies ensuring seamless availability and resolution.

elements.name: Conform with NATO Data Space Vocabulary Profile
DS::Data Request
elements.documentation: Contains AssetID, ContractID and DataDestination that describes where the Data Resource should be disseminated to.

elements.name: DS::Connector (Data service)
elements.documentation:
A Dataspace Participant Agent that produces Agreements and manages Dataset sharing.

A Connector must respond to a respective request by providing a JSON-LD object containing an array of supported versions with at least one item. 
This data object must comply to the JSON Schema and the SHACL Shape.
The requesting Connector may select from the endpoints in the response. If the Connector can't identify a matching Dataspace Protocol Version, it must terminate the communication.

elements.name: Data Space Participant Agent
elements.documentation: A Dataspace Participant Agent performs tasks such as publishing a Catalog or engaging in a Transfer Process. In order to accomplish these tasks, a Participant Agent may use a verifiable presentation generated from a credential obtained from a third-party Credential Issuer. A Dataspace Participant Agent may also use an ID token issued by a third-party Identity Provider. Note that a Dataspace Participant Agent is a logical construct and does not necessarily correspond to a single runtime process.

elements.name: DCAT Dataservice
elements.documentation: Describes data which could be shared and the Service Interface how to retrive the data.

elements.name: DS::Metadata Broker Service
elements.documentation: The DS Metadata Broker Service is a comprehensive connector that provides the necessary interfaces for communicating with any other Data Spaces connector. More specifically, it is capable of handling messages from Data Space connectors, indicating a status update, such as new data being available. Self-descripting metadata of connectors are automatically indexed and can be optionally restricted by means of usage control policies. Search functionality encompasses a full-text search, filter options based on the International Data Spaces information model, and full queries using SPARQL, the standardized query language in the semantic web. Human user friendly access is granted via a web interface.

elements.name: Asset Address
elements.documentation: Describes datasource for Dataset

elements.name: DCAT Catalog
elements.documentation: A collection of entries representing Data Products (incl. Datasets or Data Services) and their Offers that is advertised by a Provider Participant.

elements.name: ORDL Offer
elements.documentation: Represents Rules that are being offered from assigner Parties. An Offer is typically used to make available Policies to a wider audience, but does not grant any Rules.

elements.name: DS::Catalog Service
elements.documentation: A Participant Agent that makes a Catalog accessible to Participants.

elements.name: Data Space Registry
elements.documentation: A technology system that maintains the state of Participants in a Dataspace.

elements.name: DS::Connector
elements.documentation:
A Participant Agent that produces Agreements and manages Dataset sharing.
Dedicated communication server for sending and receiving data in compliance with the general Connector specification; different types of Connectors can be distinguished (Base Connector vs. Trusted Connector, or Internal Connector vs. External Connector).

elements.name: Participant Agent
elements.documentation: A technology system that performs operations on behalf of a Participant that offers a Dataset.

elements.name: Data Space Platform
elements.documentation: Collection of digital services to implement a data space.

elements.name: Trust
elements.documentation: Trust is the basis of NATO Dataspaces. Each participant is evaluated and certified before being granted access to the trusted data sharing ecosystem

elements.name: Security
elements.documentation:
All components of NATO Dataspaces rely on state-of-the-art security measures. Apart from architectural specifications, 
security is mainly ensured by the evaluation and certification of each technical component used in NATO Dataspaces.

elements.name: Sovereignty
elements.documentation: In line with the central aspect of ensuring data sovereignty, a data owner in NATO Dataspaces attaches usage restriction information to their data before it is transferred to a data consumer. To use the data, the data consumer must fully accept the data owner’s usage policy.

elements.name: Data Ecosystem
elements.documentation: The architecture of Data Space does not require central data storage capabilities. Instead, it pursues the idea of decentralization of data storage, which means that data physically remains with the respective data owner until it is transferred to a trusted party. This approach requires a comprehensive description of each data source and the value and usability of data for other orgnizations, combined with the ability to integrate domain-specific data vocabularies. In addition, brokers in the ecosystem provide services for real-time data search.

elements.name: Standardized Interoperability
elements.documentation:
The Data Space Connector, being a central component of the architecture, is implemented in different variants and can 
be acquired from different vendors. Nevertheless, each Connector is able to communicate with any other Connector (or other technical component) in the ecosystem of data spaces.

elements.name: Extensibility
elements.documentation:
NATO Dataspaces allow to inject apps into the Connectors in order to provide services on top of data exchange processes. This includes services for data processing, data format alignment, and data exchange protocols, for example. Furthermore, data analytics services can be provided by remote execution of algorithms.
NATO Dataspaces enable the creation of novel, data-driven services that make use of data apps. It also fosters new models for these services by creating domain-specific broker solutions. In addition, NATO Dataspaces provide templates and other methodological support for participants to use when specifying usage restriction information and requesting legal information.

elements.name: Conform with the requirements of DIN SPEC 27070:2020-03
Conform with Resource Description Framework 1.1 Profile
Conform with the JSON-based Serialization for Linked Data 1.1
Conform with Data Catalog Vocabulary (DCAT) - Version 3
Conform with Open Digital Rights Language 2.2 Profile
Provide NATO Data Space conformance test suite
elements.documentation: Provide automated conformance test suite for all normative requirements

elements.name: DCAT Dataset
elements.documentation: A description of a resource or a collection of resources. The Asset can describe any form of identifiable resource, such as data/information, content/media, applications, services, or even physical artefacts.

elements.name: Dataset Element
elements.documentation: Describes data which could be shared

elements.name: DS::Policy
elements.documentation: A set of rules, duties, and obligations that define the terms of use for a Dataset. Also referred to as "Policy".

elements.name: DS::Catalog Asset
elements.documentation: The Catalog Asset can describe any form of identifiable and shareable resource, such as Data Products (incl. Datasets or Data Services) , content/media, applications, services, or even physical artefacts.

elements.name: Distribution
elements.documentation:
A specific representation of a Dataset. A Dataset might be available in multiple serializations that may differ in various ways, including natural language, media-type or format, schematic organization, temporal and spatial resolution, level of detail or profiles (which might specify any or all of the above).
This represents a general availability of a dataset. It implies no information about the actual access method of the data

elements.name: ODRL Agreement
elements.documentation: Representation of Rules that have been granted from assigner to assignee Parties. An Agreement is typically used to grant the terms of the Rules between the Parties.

elements.name: RDF 1.1 Concepts, Resource Description Framework (RDF) 1.1 Concepts and Abstract Syntax
RDF Primer, Resource Description Framework (RDF) Primer
NATO Data Space Data Transfer Protocol Profile - Mandatory
A JSON-based Serialization for Linked Data 1.1
elements.documentation:
JSON is a useful data serialization and messaging format. This specification defines JSON-LD 1.1, a JSON-based format to serialize Linked Data. The syntax is designed to easily integrate into deployed systems that already use JSON, and provides a smooth upgrade path from JSON to JSON-LD. It is primarily intended to be a way to use Linked Data in Web-based programming environments, to build interoperable Web services, and to store Linked Data in JSON-based storage engines.

This specification describes a superset of the features defined in JSON-LD 1.0 [https://www.w3.org/TR/json-ld/#bib-json-ld10] and, except where noted, documents created using the 1.0 version of this specification remain compatible with JSON-LD 1.1.

elements.name: Requirements and reference architecture of a security gateway for the exchange of industry data and services
elements.documentation:
This standard defines a Security Gateway for establishing virtual cross-company data and service spaces. Based on the principles of data sovereignty, the Security Gateway facilitates secure exchange of data and allows providing and using trustworthy data services.
The Security Gateway is available in three different variants, specifying different levels of security and protection. It is suited for being used by companies from industries such as finance, healthcare, IT, telecommunications, logistics, or Industrie 4.0 (Industrial
Internet).
The requirements described in this document relate to the technical features of the Security Gateway. The document does not address the requirements regarding development or secure operation of this component, nor does it relate to other components of the architecture, such as the App Store, the Clearing Entity, or the Broker.

The three different levels of security are: Base, Trust, Trust+. The “base” profile meets basic security requirements for communication across company boundaries. A connector that has been certified according to the “trust” profile provides additional security features such as strict isolation of the service containers and mutual verification of integrity. A “trust+” profile connector even provides protection against manipulation by malicious administrators. These security levels comply with ISO/IEC 62443 (particularly ISO/IEC 62443-4-2) but have been extended by including additional requirements deemed necessary for the IDS ecosystem. That makes DIN SPEC 27070 the first initiative specifying requirements regarding a secure gateway for cross-company data exchange in the manufacturing industry.

elements.name: Conform with the "Basic" security requirements of DIN SPEC 27070:2020-03
Conform with the "Trust" security requirements of DIN SPEC 27070:2020-03
Conform with the "Trust Plus" security requirements of DIN SPEC 27070:2020-03
ODRL Information Model 2.2
elements.documentation:
The Open Digital Rights Language (ODRL) is a policy expression language that provides a flexible and interoperable information model, vocabulary, and encoding mechanisms for representing statements about the usage of content and services. 
The ODRL Information Model describes the underlying concepts, entities, and relationships that form the foundational basis for the semantics of the ODRL policies.

elements.name: Open Digital Rights Language 2.2 Profile - Mandatory
ODRL Vocabulary & Expression 2.2
elements.documentation:
The Open Digital Rights Language (ODRL) is a policy expression language that provides a flexible and interoperable information model, vocabulary, and encoding mechanisms for representing statements about the usage of content and services. 
The ODRL Vocabulary and Expression describes the terms used in ODRL policies and how to encode them.

elements.name: ODRL Ontology 2.2
elements.documentation: The Open Digital Rights Language (ODRL) is a policy expression language that provides a flexible and interoperable information model, vocabulary, and encoding mechanisms for representing statements about the usage of content and services.

elements.name: Open Digital Rights Language 2.2 Profile
elements.documentation:
Adopted as a W3C recommendation in 2018, Open Digital Rights Language (ODRL) Version 2.2  provides a framework for defining permissions, prohibitions, and duties associated with data assets. Its flexibility and interoperability support various policy requirements, including licensing, privacy controls, and usage rights. It has been implemented in several industries, including publishing, financial services, and digital media, where there’s a need to standardize access and control over digital content.
The Open Digital Rights Language 2.2 Profile combines the relevant standards and guidance for ODRL Version 2.2.

elements.name: International Dataspace Protocol 2024-1
elements.documentation:
The Dataspace Protocol standard is a set of specifications designed to facilitate interoperable data sharing between entities governed by usage control and based on Web technologies. These specifications define the schemas and protocols required for entities to publish data, negotiate Agreements, and access data as part of a federation of technical systems termed a Dataspace.

The Dataspace Protocol specification is organized into the following documents:
* Dataspace Model and Dataspace Terminology documents that define key terms.
* Common Functionalities and their Binding in HTTPS declares cross-cutting functions as, e.g., the declaration of supported versions of this Dataspace Protocol.
* Catalog Protocol and Catalog HTTPS Binding documents that define how DCAT Catalogs are published and accessed as HTTPS endpoints respectively.
* Contract Negotiation Protocol and Contract Negotiation HTTPS Binding documents that define how Contract Negotiations are conducted and requested via HTTPS endpoints.
* Transfer Process Protocol and Transfer Process HTTPS Binding documents that define how Transfer Processes using a given data transfer protocol are governed via HTTPS endpoints.

This specification does not cover the data transfer process as such.

elements.name: Data Catalog Vocabulary - Version 3
elements.documentation:
DCAT is an RDF vocabulary designed to facilitate interoperability between data catalogs published on the Web. This document defines the schema and provides examples for its use.
DCAT enables a publisher to describe datasets and data services in a catalog using a standard model and vocabulary that facilitates the consumption and aggregation of metadata from multiple catalogs. This can increase the discoverability of datasets and data services. It also makes it possible to have a decentralized approach to publishing data catalogs and makes federated search for datasets across catalogs in multiple sites possible using the same query mechanism and structure. Aggregated DCAT metadata can serve as a manifest file as part of the digital preservation process.

elements.name: NATO Data Space Catalog Content Profile - Mandatory
Conform with NATO Data Space Catalog Content Profile
odrl:Permission
elements.documentation: The ability to perform an Action over an Asset.

elements.name: odrl:Prohibition
elements.documentation: The inability to perform an Action over an Asset.

elements.name: Transfer Request Message
elements.documentation: The Transfer Request Message is sent by a Consumer to initiate a Transfer Process.

elements.name: NATO Data Space Transfer Protocol Profile
Transfer Start Message
elements.documentation: The Transfer Start Message is sent by the Provider to indicate the data transfer has been initiated.

elements.name: Transfer Suspension Message
elements.documentation: The Transfer Suspension Message is sent by the Provider or Consumer when either of them needs to temporarily suspend the Transfer Process.

elements.name: Transfer Completion Message
elements.documentation: The Transfer Completion Message is sent by the Provider or Consumer when a data transfer has completed. Note that some Connector implementations may optimize completion notification by performing it as part of their wire protocol. In those cases, a Transfer Completion Message does not need to be sent.

elements.name: Transfer Termination Message
elements.documentation: The Transfer Termination Message is sent by the Provider or Consumer at any point except a terminal state to indicate the Transfer Process should stop and be placed in a terminal state. If the termination was due to an error, the sender may include error information.

elements.name: DS::Data Transfer Process Binding:HTTPS
elements.documentation: This specification defines a RESTful API over HTTPS for the Transfer Process Protocol.

elements.name: NATO Data Space Data Transfer Protocol Profile - Recommended
DS::Data Transfer Specification
elements.documentation: This specification defines a RESTful API over HTTPS for the Transfer Process Protocol.

elements.name: Contract Request Message
elements.documentation: The Contract Request Message is sent by a Consumer to initiate a CN or to respond to a Contract Offer Message sent by a Provider.

elements.name: NATO Data Space Contract Negotiation Profile
NATO Data Space Contract Negotiation Profile - Mandatory
Contract Offer Message
elements.documentation: The Contract Offer Message is sent by a Provider to initiate a CN or to respond to a Contract Request Message sent by a Consumer.

elements.name: Contract Agreement Message
elements.documentation: The Contract Agreement Message is sent by a Provider when it agrees to a contract. It contains the complete Agreement.

elements.name: Contract Agreement Verification Message
elements.documentation: The Contract Agreement Verification Message is sent by a Consumer to verify the acceptance of an Agreement.

elements.name: Contract Negotiation Event Message
elements.documentation: When the Contract Negotiation Event Message is sent by a Provider with an eventType property set to FINALIZED, an Agreement has been finalized and the associated Dataset is accessible. The state machine is transitioned to the FINALIZED state.

elements.name: DS::Contract Negotiation Binding:HTTPS
elements.documentation: This specification defines a RESTful API over HTTPS for the Transfer Process Protocol.

elements.name: NATO Data Space Contract Negotiation Profile - Recommended
DS::Contract Negotiation Specification
elements.documentation: This document outlines the key elements of the Contract Negotiation Protocol.

elements.name: Contract Negotiation Termination Message
elements.documentation: The Contract Negotiation Termination Message is sent by a Consumer or Provider indicating it has cancelled the CN sequence. The message can be sent at any state of a CN without providing an explanation. Nevertheless, the sender may provide a description to help the receiver.

elements.name: odrl:Agreement
elements.documentation:
A Policy that grants the assignee a Rule over an Asset from an assigner.
An Agreement Policy MUST contain at least one Permission or Prohibition rule, a Party with Assigner function, and a Party with Assignee function (in the same Permission or Prohibition). The Agreement Policy will grant the terms of the Policy from the Assigner to the Assignee.

elements.name: odrl:Assignee
elements.documentation: The Party is the recipient of the Rule.

elements.name: odrl:Assigner
elements.documentation: The Party is the issuer of the Rule.

elements.name: Catalog Request Message
elements.documentation: The Catalog Request Message is message sent by a Consumer to a Catalog Service. The Catalog Service must respond with a Catalog, which is a valid instance of a DCAT Catalog.

elements.name: NATO Data Space Catalog Query Profile
NATO Data Space Catalog Query Profile - Mandatory
Dataset Request Message
elements.documentation: The Dataset Request Message is message sent by a Consumer to a Catalog Service. The Catalog Service must respond with a Dataset, which is a valid instance of a DCAT Dataset.

elements.name: DS::Catalog Binding:HTTPS
elements.documentation: This specification defines a RESTful API over HTTPS for the Catalog Protocol.

elements.name: NATO Data Space Catalog Query Profile - Recommended
Conform with NATO Data Space Catalog Query Profile
DS::Catalog Specification
elements.documentation: The Catalog Protocol defines how a Catalog is requested from a Catalog Service by a Consumer using an abstract message exchange format. The concrete message exchange wire format is defined in the binding specifications.

elements.name: NATO Data Space Vocabulary Profile - Recommended
NATO Data Space Vocabulary Profile - Mandatory
RDF 1.1 Turtle, Terse RDF Triple Language
elements.documentation:
The Resource Description Framework (RDF) is a general-purpose language for representing information in the Web.
This document defines a textual syntax for RDF called Turtle that allows an RDF graph to be completely written in a compact and natural text form, with abbreviations for common usage patterns and datatypes. Turtle provides levels of compatibility with the N-Triples [N-TRIPLES] format as well as the triple pattern syntax of the SPARQL W3C Recommendation.

elements.name: OWL 2, Web Ontology Language Document Overview (Second Edition)
elements.documentation:
The OWL 2 Web Ontology Language, informally OWL 2, is an ontology language for the Semantic Web with formally defined meaning. OWL 2 ontologies provide classes, properties, individuals, and data values and are stored as Semantic Web documents. OWL 2 ontologies can be used along with information written in RDF, and OWL 2 ontologies themselves are primarily exchanged as RDF documents.
This document serves as an introduction to OWL 2 and the various other OWL 2 documents. It describes the syntaxes for OWL 2, the different kinds of semantics, the available profiles (sub-languages), and the relationship between OWL 1 and OWL 2.

elements.name: SKOS, Simple Knowledge Organization System Reference
elements.documentation:
The Simple Knowledge Organization System (SKOS) is a common data model for sharing and linking knowledge organization systems via the Web.
Many knowledge organization systems, such as thesauri, taxonomies, classification schemes and subject heading systems, share a similar structure, and are used in similar applications. SKOS captures much of this similarity and makes it explicit, to enable data and technology sharing across diverse applications.
The SKOS data model provides a standard, low-cost migration path for porting existing knowledge organization systems to the Semantic Web. SKOS also provides a lightweight, intuitive language for developing and sharing new knowledge organization systems. It may be used on its own, or in combination with formal knowledge representation languages such as the Web Ontology language (OWL).
This document is the normative specification of the Simple Knowledge Organization System. It is intended for readers who are involved in the design and implementation of information systems, and who already have a good understanding of Semantic Web technology, especially RDF and OWL.
For an informative guide to using SKOS, see https://www.w3.org/TR/skos-primer/ .

elements.name: Code List Representation (genericode) Version 1.0
elements.documentation: The OASIS Code List Representation format, “genericode”, is a single semantic model of code lists and accompanying XML serialization (supported by a W3C XML Schema https://docs.oasis-open.org/codelist/genericode/v1.0/os/xsd/genericode.xsd) that can encode a broad range of code list information. The serialization is designed to IT-enable the interchange or distribution of machine-readable code list information between systems. Note that genericode is not designed as a run-time format for accessing code list information, and is not optimized for such usage. Rather, it is designed as an interchange format that can be transformed into formats suitable for run-time usage, or loaded into systems that perform run-time processing using code list information.

elements.name: Qualified Dublin Core XML Schemas, version 2008-02-11
elements.documentation:
To represent a Dublin Core™ metadata description set in an XML document, the constructs and components of the description set must be represented as components in the XML document, i.e. as XML elements and XML attributes, XML element names and XML attribute names, and as XML element content and XML attribute values.This set of schemas supports the encoding of Qualified Dublin Core™ metadata records, including the use of element refinements and encoding schemes, following the conventions described in Guidelines for implementing Dublin Core™ in XML. The three "base" schemas declare XML elements to represent DC elements and element refinements, and define complexTypes to represent DC encoding schemes: 
These three schemas declare XML elements to represent the Dublin Core™ elements and element refinements and a number of complexTypes to represent encoding schemes:
1) Schema: dc.xsd
Schema location: http://dublincore.org/schemas/xmls/qdc/dc.xsd - Target XML Namespace: http://purl.org/dc/elements/1.1/
2) Schema: dcterms.xsd
Schema location: http://dublincore.org/schemas/xmls/qdc/dcterms.xsd - Target XML Namespace: http://purl.org/dc/terms/
3) Schema: dcmitype.xsd 
Schema location: http://dublincore.org/schemas/xmls/qdc/dcmitype.xsd - Target XML Namespace: http://purl.org/dc/dcmitype/

elements.name: Credential Issuer
elements.documentation: A Credential Issuer is a trusted technology system that issues verifiable credentials for a Participant and Participant Agents.

elements.name: Enable the creation of innovative, cross-organizational processes while guaranteeing that data owners maintain full control over how their data and models are accessed and utilized.
Data Space Roles and Processes
elements.documentation: A Dataspace member that provides and/or consumes Datasets.

elements.name: Negotiating Contract
elements.documentation: While a Connector Self-Description basically contains descriptive information about available data assets, these also include Usage Control information in form of a Contract Offer. A Contract Offer describes under what conditions the Data Provider is willing to make its data available to the Data Consumer. This can range from simple access restrictions to complex pre- and post-duties. In a (semi-)automated negotiation process performed by the Usage Control frameworks of the participating DS Connectors, the Data Consumer and the Data Provider need to agree on a Data Sharing Contract.

elements.name: Data Space Applications and Technology
elements.documentation: A set of technical services that facilitate interoperable Dataset sharing between entities.

elements.name: DS::Clearing House
elements.documentation:
Intermediary providing clearing and settlement services for all financial and data exchange transactions within the International Data Spaces.

The IDS Clearing House consists of an IDS Connector and bases all its functions on a logging service that records information relevant for clearing and billing as well as usage control. The information sent to the Clearing House is defined in the Process Layer.
The Clearing House uses this information to provide a Clearing and Settlement Service on the basis of usage contracts and helps with the automization of payments between Data Provider and Data Consumer. It can also use this information to provide a Billing Service to allow the Data Space Operator the billing of the participants. The UC Claim Validation service uses the logged usage control data to allow the validation of usage claims on resources.

elements.name: DS::Identity Provider
elements.documentation:
A trusted technology system that creates, maintains, and manages identity information for a Participant and Participant Agents.
An Identity Provider is a trust anchor that generates ID tokens used to verify the identity of a Participant Agent. Multiple identity providers may operate in a Dataspace. The types and semantics of ID tokens are not part of this specification. An Identity Provider may be a third-party or a Participant itself (for example, in the case of decentralized identifiers).

elements.name: DS::Metadata Broker
elements.documentation:
