A Metadata Broker is a core intermediary component of a data space that facilitates the discovery and management of metadata, specifically Self-Descriptions of Data Space resources and connectors. It acts as a registry where participants publish metadata about their data assets and services, enabling other participants to discover relevant data offerings. The broker provides indexing, querying, and search functionalities, allowing data consumers to find suitable data sources without needing prior knowledge of the providers' locations.

A Dataspace Metadata Broker is a Dataspace Connector, which contains an endpoint for the registration, publication, maintenance, and query of Self-Descriptions. The Metadata Broker stores Self-Descriptions, which are structured according to the Data Space Information Model, typically encoded in formats like RDF/JSON-LD, and makes them searchable through standard interfaces. However, it does not actively transfer data between providers and consumers; it solely handles metadata. This ensures semantic interoperability and compliance with the Dataspace framework while maintaining the sovereignty of data providers over their resources. The broker itself is a specialized type of Dataspace Connector, requiring compliance with the Dataspace Connector specification to guarantee trust and security in its operations.

elements.name: DS::Vocabulary Hub
elements.documentation: Server providing maintenance facilities for editing, browsing and downloading vocabularies and related documents; mirrors a set of external third-party vocabularies ensuring seamless availability and resolution.

elements.name: DS::App Store
elements.documentation: Secure platform for distributing Data Apps; features different search options (e.g. by functional or non-functional properties, pricing model, certification status, community ratings, etc.).

elements.name: Data Product Specification
elements.documentation:
To describe the metadata of a data product, it is common to have a data product specification file in the repository of the data product, or it is generated through the deployment process by the data platform.
A formal data product specification can act as a foundation for automation and to provide metadata to other systems, such as an enterprise catalog or a data product catalog.

elements.name: DS::Policy
elements.documentation: A set of rules, duties, and obligations that define the terms of use for a Dataset. Also referred to as "Usage Policy".

elements.name: Data Mesh Platform
elements.documentation: Collection of digital services to implement a data mesh.

elements.name: Trusted Data Sharing
elements.documentation: Trusted Data Sharing enables the secure and controlled exchange of data between participants, supported by trust-establishing frameworks and mechanisms. In federated ecosystems, participants integrate their Data Meshes under a unified regulatory framework, such as agreed Security and Data Management Policies, to facilitate scalable collaboration and ensure compliance with governance standards, security measures, and interoperability requirements. When no pre-established frameworks exist, data spaces offer dynamic environments where participants negotiate sharing rules and usage rights case by case. These spaces rely on technologies like IDS Connectors, smart contracts, and metadata brokering to enforce policies and uphold data sovereignty. Both approaches prioritize interoperability, regulatory compliance, and trust, enabling secure collaboration across diverse ecosystems.

elements.name: Internet
elements.documentation: The Internet serves as the overarching connectivity backbone, enabling the exchange of data between disparate systems, entities, and nodes. It operates across a global federation of autonomous systems and networks, facilitating seamless interaction. As a conceptual infrastructure layer, the Internet supports communication channels, application integrations, and service delivery mechanisms, ensuring scalability, resilience, and ubiquitous access. It interconnects stakeholders, systems, and processes, acting as a crucial enabler of distributed ecosystems and collaborative services.

elements.name: Machine Learning Platform
elements.documentation: A managed, end-to-end environment that supports the development, training, deployment, and operationalization of machine learning models. It provides integrated tools, scalable compute resources, and automation capabilities for data preparation, model experimentation, training, hyperparameter tuning, and performance monitoring. Designed to streamline the entire machine learning lifecycle, such platforms enable collaboration across teams, ensure reproducibility, and support continuous integration and delivery of ML models into production environments.

elements.name: Machine Learning Hub
elements.documentation: A technology component that facilitates the secure, efficient, and collaborative training and sharing of machine learning models, combining Distributed Machine Learning, Federated Machine Learning and Trusted ML Model Sharing. It provides a controlled environment for creating and sharing ML models while ensuring trust, accountability, and compliance. During training, large-scale tasks are distributed across multiple systems to enhance computational efficiency, while decentralized devices train models locally and share updates to protect data privacy. This scalable and interoperable hub fosters collaboration between diverse participant whilst upholding data sovereignty. It also enables participants to co-create and deploy innovative machine learning solutions effectively.

elements.name: Distributed Machine Learning
elements.documentation: Distributed Machine Learning (DML) involves partitioning large-scale machine learning tasks across multiple machines or processors to enhance computational efficiency and manage extensive datasets. By distributing the workload, DML accelerates training processes and allows for the handling of models that are computationally intensive. This approach is particularly beneficial when dealing with massive datasets that are challenging to process on a single machine.

elements.name: Federated Machine Learning
elements.documentation: Federated Machine Learning (FML) is a subset of DML that focuses on training machine learning models across decentralized devices holding local data, such as smartphones or IoT devices, without transferring this data to a central server. Instead, each device trains a local model on its data and shares only the model updates (e.g., gradients) with a central server, which aggregates these updates to build a global model. This methodology enhances data privacy and security, as raw data remains on local devices.

elements.name: Trusted ML Model Sharing
elements.documentation: A controlled environment for sharing pre-trained models or components, maintaining trust and accountability through mechanisms like secure versioning and provenance tracking. Within a Trusted ML Model Sharing environment participants can collaborate on ML projects, share resources efficiently, and achieve scalable solutions while aligning with ethical and regulatory standards.

elements.name: NDBB RA Increment 1
elements.documentation: Increment 1 contains the initial set of services that have been agreed to be part of the NATO Digital Backbone. For each of those services, the RA identifies existing standards and standard profiles for their implementation. In addition, the RA identifies key interoperability points for interaction with or between the services, and related standards and standard profiles.

elements.name: NDBB RA Increment 1 Platform Layer
elements.documentation: The Digital Backbone Platform Layer contains the platform services from the C3 Taxonomy and related interaction points that are part of the Digital Backbone Increment 1.

elements.name: Message Brokering Services
elements.documentation:
The Message Brokering Services act as an intermediary between Message  Publishers and Message Consumers in order to permit the Message Consumer to subscribe to Messages produced by Publishers.

Within this Message Publish-Subscribe pattern, senders of messages (called Publishers) do not send messages directly to specific receivers (called Subscribers), but instead send them to Message Brokering Services for further distribution to registered Subscribers.

elements.name: Message Routing Services
elements.documentation:
The Message Routing Services are services that can dynamically route messages at run time based on different criteria, e.g. message content or metadata or for load-balancing purposes. The routing logic shall be configurable.
The Message Routing Services can be also used to provide one-to-many message delivery by multiplying a message and sending it to many recipients, e.g. this can be used to implement multicast messages.

elements.name: Message Caching Services
elements.documentation: The Message Caching Services provide functionality to conditionally store messages sent between producers and consumers. The messages can be later served to consumers if they need to resynchronize their state or were unavailable and lost some messages. The cache can support synchronous (request/response) and asynchronous (fire and forget, publish/subscribe) communication.

elements.name: Message Proxying Services
elements.documentation:
The Message Proxying Services are services that act as an intermediary for other services, hiding their actual location and implementation from the service consumers. The proxy services can communicate on a behalf of the underlying service. They offer a capability to expose a virtual endpoint of the underlying service. They supports the loose coupling and service abstraction principles of the SOA design.

The Message Proxying Services tend to sit at the boundaries of organizations, either internal boundaries (between sites, before WAN links) or external boundaries (such as the Internet). They provide a number of benefits over the use of directly communicating through a router.  A number of security features may be activated, such as content checking, authentication and authorization, auditing and anonymity (as the identity of the client machine can be hidden).

The Message Proxy Services can communicate with Message Caching Services to avoid having to make calls across sub-optimal WAN links, and can use other techniques (such as compression) to improve performance.

elements.name: Choreography Services
elements.documentation:
The Choreography Services provide the means to model the compositions of multiple technical services into so called choreographies and to specify the interfaces and protocols implemented by services participating in a choreography.

Choreography is a set of autonomous activities that have a defined pattern of behaviour with respect to each other. There is no single activity that directs the other activities in choreography. Choreography distributes the control and relies on the ability of its component activities to understand and respond to events. Choreography treats services as peers that interact based on an agreement, rather than imposing a single-point-of-entry brokering pattern on top of them. In a choreography scenario composition is understood as the collaborative exchange that takes place based on the description of messages exchange and the interaction of a set of services seen from a global perspective.

Choreographies are not executable in a sense that there is no central controller service that could be executed.

Choreography mechanisms are used to specify the coordination agreement and behaviour of each service in choreography, including the external interfaces exposed by the services involved and the protocol implemented by each of the services involved, including order of messages being exchanged and specification of services that these messages will be exchanged with.
These interfaces of services involved and message exchange protocol followed can be used to generate stubs for the actual service implementation (e.g. to be used and exposed by orchestration). However, choreography does not include the internal details of services involved.

elements.name: Protocol Transformation Services
elements.documentation:
The Protocol Transformation Services mediate between communication parties by adjusting the way in which data is exchanged between both parties. Protocol Transformation Services enable the use of different protocols for handling information between information providers and consumers over a possibly heterogeneous network. Protocol Transformation Services are important when different types of communication patterns are being used (e.g. static, deployable or mobile) that would require special protocols to ensure that the information is being transferred in the most efficient possible way.

Protocol transformation services mediate between various transport protocols, which for example in a web services setting usually comprise single protocols like HTTP, HTTPS, TLS, SMTP and FTP, but also entire message-oriented middle-ware solutions like IBM's WebSphere MQ or JMS. We speak of protocol virtualization if a protocol mediation services actually offers to consume a service over a range of different transport protocols.

elements.name: Transaction Services
elements.documentation:
The Transaction Services allow multiple individual operations to be linked together as a single, indivisible action. All operations in a transaction are either completed without error or none of them are; if some of the operations are completed but errors occur when the others are attempted, the transaction-processing system "rolls back" all of the operations of the transaction (including the successful ones) thereby erasing all traces of the transaction and restoring the system to the consistent, known state that it was in before processing of the transaction began.

In scenarios when usual transactional properties (like atomicity, consistency, isolation, and durability) are too strong or unimplementable (e.g. in complex business processes), some limited transactional properties must be satisfied to guarantee a process is not left in an inconsistent state. For example compenzating activities can bring the process to a consistent state, albeit not necessarily identical as the state before the process started.

elements.name: Message Queueing Services
elements.documentation: The Message Queueing Services provide message queues as intermediary buffers, allowing services and consumers to process messages independently by remaining temporally decoupled. Thus they supports asynchronous communication.

elements.name: Orchestration Services
elements.documentation:
The Orchestration Services provide the means to coordinate the execution of multiple technical services in such a way that the coordinated whole of technical services appears as a single, aggregate technical service responding to a single individual request. Such an aggregation could be said to implement a business process that is characterized by the fact that it runs within own organization boundaries, with the own organization having full control over the execution of the process.

Orchestration describes one particular component activity of the composition that oversees and directs the other component activities. An orchestration has one and only one direction activity. In a service-oriented software solution, the component services of an orchestration are software services performed by software programs.

A service composition described by an orchestration is again a service itself and can be re-used in further compositions.

An orchestration coordinates the other sub-services in a step-wise manner, i.e. it specifies the partial order of all the steps that each of the sub-services has to do in order to produce the desired outputs in a co-operative, joined way.

An orchestration can be (and usually is) stateful as the coordination of sub-services immediately requires to keep track of states. The other sub-services which are controlled by the orchestration do usually not interact with each other directly and do not have to be stateful.

elements.name: NDBB RA Increment 1 Infrastructure Layer
elements.documentation: The Digital Backbone Infrastructure Layer contains the infrastructure services from the C3 Taxonomy and related interaction points that are part of the Digital Backbone Increment 1.

elements.name: Domain Name Services
elements.documentation: The Domain Name Services provide access to a hierarchical distributed naming system for computers, services, or any resource connected to the Internet or a private network. Domain Name Services associate various information with domain names assigned to each of the participating entities. Most importantly, Domain Name Services translate domain names meaningful to humans into the numerical identifiers associated with networking equipment for the purpose of locating and addressing these devices worldwide.

elements.name: Remote Access Services
elements.documentation: The Remote Access Services enable authorized individuals to remotely access the user interface of a computing resource for the purpose of installation, configuration, monitoring, metering, auditing or process management.

elements.name: Virtualized Networking Services
elements.documentation: The Virtualized Networking Services Network virtualization allow IT managers to consolidate multiple physical networks, divide a network into multiple segments or create software-only networks between virtual machines (VMs). The goal of network virtualization is to improve the agility to direct appropriate network resources to VMs in data center environments and the ability to consolidate or segment networks. Virtual networks can be created in two ways -- inside or outside the server. External software uses switches, adapters and the network to aggregate physical local area networks (LANs) into a single logical LAN, or to break a physical LAN into multiple virtual LANs (VLANs). Internal software allows virtual machines (VMs) to exchange data on a host without an external network.

elements.name: Network Load Balancing Services
elements.documentation: The Network Load Balancing Services distribute workload across the network, to multiple processing resources, network links, central processing units, disk drives, or other resources, to achieve optimal resource utilization, maximize throughput, minimize response time, and avoid overload. Using multiple components with load balancing, instead of a single component, may increase reliability through redundancy.

elements.name: Intrusion Detection Services
elements.documentation: The Intrusion Detection Services provide the means to gather information on malicious activity and/or relevant policy violations. They focus on the identification of possible incidents and intrusive events, on the registration and logging of corresponding activities and system behavior, and the generation of reports. The main objective is to identify problems with information security and management policies, document existing threats, and deter individuals from violations.

elements.name: Caching Services
elements.documentation:
The Caching Services accelerate service requests by retrieving content saved from a previous request, allowing organizations to significantly reduce their upstream bandwidth usage and costs, while simultaneously increasing performance. This means that the requested resource does not need to be downloaded from a remote server, possibly over a connection with limited bandwidth, but can be retrieved from a store located on the local LAN. Caching Services do this by keeping local copies of requested resources and serving those to the client rather than fetching them from the original server. If the resource is not already present in the cache, then it is retrieved from the requested URL, and a copy is written to the local store.
Caching Services are often used by Proxy Services, and are indeed often collocated with them. However, they are separate, and an entire caching infrastructure can be built independent of proxy services.

elements.name: Infrastructure Cryptography Services
elements.documentation:
The Infrastructure Cryptography Services supports the use of ciphers including encryption and decryption processes to ensure confidentiality and integrity of data.

Typically, asymmetric cryptography is used for authenticated key exchange, symmetric encryption for data confidentiality, and cryprographic hash functions and digital signatures for data integrity.

elements.name: Host Configuration Services
elements.documentation: The Host Configuration Services provide the configuration parameters required by a host to complete a subscription to a network.  The required parameters are determined by the network being subscribed to, but may include the host address, sub-net mask, name server and others.

elements.name: Proxying Services
elements.documentation:
The Proxying Services handle HTTP message exchanges on behalf of other entities. From the perspective of the partner in the message exchange, it is communicating with the proxy, and is not necessarily aware that this is the case.

There are three main types of Proxying Services:
# Forward Proxy - in this case, the user's client (which may be an Internet Browser or Services client) is configured to use the specific proxy in order to make requests on its behalf. The remote service provider sees the request as coming from the proxy, and not from the original client.
# Reverse Proxy - in this case, the proxy is handling requests from clients as if it were the server. The request is sent to the proxy, which may return a response, or may forward the request to the actual server for further processing.
# Transparent Proxy - a transparent proxy acts in much the same way as a forward proxy, but the client requires no configuration to use it. As far as the client is concerned, it is communicating directly with the server, as the communications are intercepted at the network layer rather than the application layer.

Proxying Services tend to sit at the boundaries of organizations, either internal boundaries (between sites, before WAN links) or external boundaries (such as the Internet).

elements.name: Distributed Time Services
elements.documentation: The Distributed Time Services provide synchronized time co-ordination as required among distributed processes when executed on different infrastructure segments and across time zones.

elements.name: NDBB RA Increment 1 Communications Layer
elements.documentation: The Digital Backbone Communications Layer contains the communications services from the C3 Taxonomy and related interaction points that are part of the Digital Backbone Increment 1.

elements.name: Transport Cryptography Services
elements.documentation: The Transport Cryptography Services provide encryption capabilities required to secure (encrypt and decrypt) transfer of data over a variety of end-to-end transports supported over and/or within the "Core Network" (e.g. WAN, PCN).

elements.name: IPv4 Routed Access Services
elements.documentation:
The IPv4 Routed Access Services provide the delivery or exchange of IP version 4 packets, subject to dynamic, destination- or policy-based routing, based on different routing protocols. The user's IP v4 address range is assigned by the provider of the Access Service, and it is provided to the Host via a DHCP service.

Each routing protocol is associated to a service type (i.e. an implementation option for the provider). Examples of IPv4 Routed Access Services implementation options are Static routing, Link-state Unicast routing  (e.g. RIP, EIGRP), Distance-vector Unicast routing (OSPF), Path-vector Unicast routing (BGP), Policy-based Unicast routing (PBR), Multicast routing, and Mobile Ad-hoc Networking (MANET, e.g. OLSR-based).

elements.name: Packet-based Broadcast Services
elements.documentation: The Packet-based Broadcast Services provide the dissemination of IP multicast packets.

elements.name: Packet Routing Services
elements.documentation:
The Packet Routing Services provide static or dynamic routing and forwarding of Internet Protocol (IP) version 4 packets, based on dynamic, destination- or policy-based protocols.

Similar as with IPv4 Routed Access Services, each routing protocol is associated to a service type (i.e. an implementation option for the provider). Examples of IPv4 Routed Access Services implementation options are Static routing, Link-state Unicast routing (e.g. RIP, EIGRP), Distance-vector Unicast routing (OSPF), Path-vector Unicast routing (BGP), Policy-based Unicast routing (PBR), Multicast routing, and Mobile Ad-hoc Networking (MANET, e.g. OLSR-based). On top of those, other applicable service types, related to the provider edge of a transport network, are Traffic Engineering Services (e.g. MPLS-TE), and Virtual Routing and Forwarding Services.

elements.name: Voice Access Services
elements.documentation: The Voice Access Services provide the delivery or exchange of voice information over packet-based, frame-based, circuit-based, or digital (link-based) access services, through adaptation (e.g. encoding and compression) the capability for voice appliances like VoIP phones, microphones, handsets, etc.

elements.name: Packet-based Transport Services
elements.documentation:
The Packet-based Transport Services provide the transport of Internet Protocol (IP) packets between two or more end-points, involving forwarding between packet-based routers using destination-based or policy-based routing protocols natively or over Virtual Private Network (VPN) tunnels. In these services the routing is performed on a per-packet basis. The services' "unit" is the packet flow, a flow of packets sharing a given attribute coded in the packet header (e.g. source, destination address or type of service).

Packet-based Transport Services can interface to Transmission Services through various possible Cross-layer Adaptation Functions (CLAF) such as Packet Optical Transport (P-OTS), based on the transport of IP packets over fibre using Multiprotocol Label Switching Transport Profile (MPLS-TP).

The same breakdown provided under the Packet-based Access Services class applies to the Packet-based Transport Services class. Only certain service types like Session-based VPNs are not applicable in this Transport Services context as those can only be initiated from the user end-point devices and applications (e.g. a browser running on a laptop, in turn served by a packet-based access service on its network interface).

elements.name: IPv6 Routed Access Services
elements.documentation:
The IPv6 Routed Access Services provide the delivery or exchange of IP version 6 packets, subject to dynamic, destination-based routing, based on different routing protocols (each protocol is associated to a Service Type, i.e. an implementation option). The user's IP v6 address range is assigned by the provider of the Access Service.

Each routing protocol is associated to a service type (i.e. an implementation option for the provider). Examples of IPv6 Routed Access Services implementation options are Static routing, Link-state Unicast routing  (e.g. RIP, EIGRP), Distance-vector Unicast routing (OSPF), Path-vector Unicast routing (BGP), Policy-based Unicast routing (PBR), Multicast routing, and Mobile Ad-hoc Networking (MANET, e.g. OLSR-based).

elements.name: VPN Access Services
elements.documentation:
The Virtual Private Network (VPN) Services provide the delivery or exchange of IP version 4 or version 6 packets, subject to dynamic, destination-based routing, over a network of virtual links (tunnels). The user's IP address range is independent of the provider of Access Services.

VPN Services can be considered emulated IPv4 or IPv6 (tunneled in IPv4) routed services, as the routing is constrained to the IP tunnels, which act as point-to-point, virtual interfaces, agnostic to the multi-hop nature of the supporting transport network. Implementation examples are Packet-based VPN services (tunnelling over packet-based access), GRE-based VPN (such as L2TP-based VPN and IPSec VPN), and session-based VPN services (SSH-based or SSL-based tunnelling over session-based access).

elements.name: Data quality planning
elements.documentation: Part of data quality management focused on setting data quality objectives and specifying necessary operational processes and related resources to achieve the quality objectives

elements.name: Data quality control
elements.documentation: Part of data quality management focused on fulfilling data quality requirements

elements.name: Data quality assurance
elements.documentation: Part of data quality management focused on providing confidence that data quality requirements will be fulfilled

elements.name: Data quality improvement
elements.documentation: Part of data quality management focused on increasing the ability to fulfil data quality requirements

elements.name: Data governance
elements.documentation: Development and enforcement of policies related to the management of data

elements.name: Data exchange
elements.documentation: Concerning the representation, transmission, reception, storage, and retrieval of data

elements.name: Data sharing
elements.documentation: Access to or processing of the same data by more than one authorized entity

elements.name: Data usage
elements.documentation: Any activity involving data

elements.name: Data use
elements.documentation: Handling or dealing with data for a specific purpose

elements.name: Data publication
elements.documentation: Form of data sharing that makes data discoverable by any entity

elements.name: information
elements.documentation: Data that are processed, organized and correlated to produce meaning

elements.name: master data
elements.documentation: data that represent content features of their data objects which are the lasting base for certain recurrent processes, can be repeatedly used in a variety of operations and so remain stable for a longer time

elements.name: referable data
elements.documentation: Data (digital or not) that is persistently stored and which is referred to by a persistent identifier

elements.name: citable data
elements.documentation: Referable data that has undergone registration  and quality assessment and can be referred to as citations in publications and as part of research objects

elements.name: active data
elements.documentation: Data that denotes virtual units of data objects which are created dynamically by executable code

elements.name: metadata
elements.documentation: Data about other data, documents, or records that describes their content, context, structure, data format, provenance, and/or rights attached to them

elements.name: real-time data
elements.documentation: Data being received, processed and stored at the time of its occurrence with only small delays

elements.name: dynamic data
elements.documentation: Data the content of which is changing frequently and at asynchronous moments.

elements.name: confidential data
elements.documentation: Data to which only a limited number of persons have access and which are meant for restricted use

elements.name: sensitive data
elements.documentation: Data with potentially harmful effects in the event of disclosure or misuse

elements.name: big data
elements.documentation: Extensive datasets/collections/linked data primarily characterized by big volume, extensive variety, high velocity (creation and use), and/or variability that together require a scalable architecture for efficient data storage, manipulation, and analysis

elements.name: science data
elements.documentation: Data as the basis or the outcome of endeavours of science

elements.name: data
elements.documentation: Reinterpretable representation of information in a formalized manner suitable for communication, interpretation, or processing.

elements.name: ISO 5127::digital data
elements.documentation: Data in the form of a structured sequence of bits/bytes that represents information content

elements.name: reference data
elements.documentation: Domain and community standardized data objects that define the set of permissible values to be used to populate other data objects

elements.name: anonymized data
elements.documentation: Personal data modified in such a way that direct reference to data subjects is eliminated.

elements.name: mass data
elements.documentation: Great quantities of data as the result of usually automatic measuring or collecting methods having been applied

elements.name: personal data (personally identifiable information PII)
elements.documentation: Data relating to an identified or identifiable individual

elements.name: research data
elements.documentation: Data collected, observed, or created, for purposes of data analysis to produce original research information and results

elements.name: geodata
elements.documentation: Data describing geographical entities, their names, relative positions, and other features

elements.name: knowledge
elements.documentation: Maintained, processed, and interpreted information.

elements.name: data store
elements.documentation: Organized and persistent collection of data and information that allows for its retrieval

elements.name: Information system
elements.documentation: An assembly of equipment, methods and procedures and, if necessary, personnel, organized to accomplish information processing functions.

elements.name: Communication system
elements.documentation: An assembly of equipment, methods and procedures and, if necessary, personnel, organized to accomplish information transfer functions.

elements.name: System
elements.documentation: An assembly of doctrines, methods, personnel, procedures, equipment or facilities organized to accomplish specific functions.

elements.name: Communication
elements.documentation: Process by which information is exchanged between individuals or systems sing a common system of symbols, signs, or behaviour.

elements.name: message
elements.documentation: Information prepared for communication purposes.

elements.name: Identification
elements.documentation: Process of recognizing an entity in a particular domain as distinct from other entities.

elements.name: entity
elements.documentation: Something capable of being uniquely identified.

elements.name: instance
elements.documentation: Entity that has unique identity, a set of operations can be applied to it, and state that stores the effects of the operations.

elements.name: data medium
elements.documentation: Medium in or on which data can be recorded and from which data can be retrieved.

elements.name: medium
elements.documentation: Physical substance or material (paper, film, magnetic tape, optical disc, etc.) that carries or communicates information content.

elements.name: domain
elements.documentation: Distinct area of human knowledge.

elements.name: characteristic
elements.documentation: Abstraction of a property.

elements.name: concept
elements.documentation: Unit of knowledge created by a unique combination of characteristics.

elements.name: object
elements.documentation: Anything perceivable or conceivable.

elements.name: property
elements.documentation: Feature of an object.

elements.name: material object
elements.documentation: Object which has physical extension in space and time.

elements.name: Conceputal object
elements.documentation: Object which is the product of the mental activity of applying abstraction in human mind activities.

elements.name: media format
elements.documentation: Way an information object is presented.

elements.name: data format
elements.documentation: Predetermined arrangement of data on a data medium.

elements.name: input format
elements.documentation: Data format for data entry.

elements.name: output format
elements.documentation: Data format enabling data to be extracted from a given computer application.

elements.name: internal format
elements.documentation: Data format for the processing of data within the software running on a computer.

elements.name: software
elements.documentation: All or part of the programmes, procedures, rules, and associated documentation of an information processing system.

elements.name: hardware
elements.documentation: All or part of the physical components of an information processing system.

elements.name: data element
elements.documentation: unit of data that is considered in context to be indivisible

elements.name: data attribute
elements.documentation: Single data element related to a data object such as in a database.

elements.name: data type
elements.documentation: Class of data, characterized by the members of the class and the operations that can be applied to them.

elements.name: record (set of data)
elements.documentation: Part of a document or document containing a structured and internally organized set of self-contained but related data on one person or other object, selected and presented for a predefined specific purpose.

elements.name: Carrier
elements.documentation: Mechanism by which information is transported over distance or time.

elements.name: Asset
elements.documentation: Anything that has value to the organization.

elements.name: organization
elements.documentation: Unique framework of authority within which a person or persons act, or are designated to act, towards some purpose.

elements.name: Data repository
elements.documentation: Set of files or databases combined with a storage system, a processing system and a retrieval system.

elements.name: Database
elements.documentation: Collection of machine-readable information organized so that it can be easily accessed, managed and updated.

elements.name: record (document)
elements.documentation: Document(s) containing recorded information created, received and maintained as evidence and as an asset by an organization or person, in pursuit of legal obligations or in the transaction of business

elements.name: relation
elements.documentation: Intellectual connection between two or more elements.

elements.name: generic relation
elements.documentation: Relation between two concepts where the intension of one of the concepts includes that of the other concept and at least one additional delimiting characteristic.

elements.name: partitive relation
elements.documentation: Relation between two concepts where one of the concepts constitutes the whole and the other concept a part of that whole.

elements.name: intension
elements.documentation: Set of characteristics which makes up the concept.

elements.name: equivalence relationship
elements.documentation: Relationship between two terms in a thesaurus that both represent the same concept.

elements.name: associative relationship
elements.documentation: Relationship between a pair of concepts that are not related hierarchically but share a strong semantic connection.

elements.name: instance relationship
elements.documentation: Relationship between a general concept, such as a class of things or events, and an individual instance of that class, which is often represented by a proper name.

elements.name: formal relation
elements.documentation: Relation between the terms of an indexing language.

elements.name: semantic relation
elements.documentation: Relation between concepts and between class symbols and their meanings.

elements.name: hierarchical relation
elements.documentation: Semantic relation between a pair of concepts of which one has a scope falling completely within the scope of the other.

elements.name: sequential relation
elements.documentation: Associative relationship based on spatial or temporal proximity.

elements.name: Facet
elements.documentation: Grouping of concepts of the same inherent category.

elements.name: Category
elements.documentation: Primary division of a classification system or of a main class of a general classification system.

elements.name: structured data
elements.documentation: data which are organized based on a pre-defined (applicable) set of rules

elements.name: data model
elements.documentation: graphical, lexical or combined representation of data, specifying their properties, structure, and interrelationships

elements.name: data analytics
elements.documentation: composite concept consisting of data acquisition, data collection, data validation, data processing, including data quantification, data visualization, and data interpretation

elements.name: unstructured data
elements.documentation: Data which are characterized by not having any structure apart from that record or file level.

elements.name: transaction data
elements.documentation: data representing a business transaction

elements.name: streaming data
elements.documentation: data passing across an interface from a source that is operating continuously.

elements.name: data variability
elements.documentation: changes in transmission rate, format or structure, semantics, or quality of datasets

elements.name: data variety
elements.documentation: range of formats, logical models, timescales, and semantics of a dataset

elements.name: data velocity
elements.documentation: rate of flow at which data is created, transmitted, stored, analysed or visualised

elements.name: data veracity
elements.documentation: completeness and/or accuracy of data

elements.name: data volatility
elements.documentation: characteristic of data pertaining to the rate of change of these data over time

elements.name: data volume
elements.documentation: extent of the amount of data relevant to impacting computation and storage resources and their management during data processing

elements.name: distributed data processing
elements.documentation: data processing in which the performance of operations is dispersed among the nodes in a computer network

elements.name: data processing
elements.documentation: systematic performance of operations upon data

elements.name: partially structured data
elements.documentation: data that has some organization

elements.name: vertical scaling
elements.documentation: act of increasing the performance of data processing through improvements to processors, memory, storage, or connectivity

elements.name: horizontal scaling
elements.documentation: providing a single logical unit through the connection of multiple hardware and software

elements.name: data science
elements.documentation: extraction of actionable knowledge from data through a process of discovery, or hypothesis and hypothesis testing

elements.name: application programming interface (API)
elements.documentation: boundary across which application software uses facilities of programming languages to invoke services

elements.name: ISO2382::digital data
elements.documentation: data represented by digits, possibly together with special characters and the space character

elements.name: classified data
elements.documentation: Data to which access is restricted by administrative means varying according to the degree of data protection or information protection sought

elements.name: open data
elements.documentation: Data available/visible to others and that can be freely used, re-used, re-published and redistributed by anyone

elements.name: administrative metadata
elements.documentation: Metadata about the maintenance and housekeeping for digital data or digital documents, including records.

elements.name: content metadata
elements.documentation: Metadata about the specific contents recorded in a digital document, including a record.

elements.name: document
elements.documentation: recorded information or material object which can be treated as a unit in a documentation process.

elements.name: technical metadata
elements.documentation: Metadata covering the technical requirements and preconditions for processing a digital document, including a record.

elements.name: dependency
elements.documentation: Relationship between two modelling elements  in which a change to one modelling element (the independent element) will affect the other modelling element (the dependent element)

elements.name: topical metadata
elements.documentation: Metadata covering the technical requirements and preconditions for processing a digital document, including a record.

elements.name: system metadata
elements.documentation: Metadata representing digital entity properties that are generated by the data management system.

elements.name: descriptive metadata
elements.documentation: Metadata that describes a resource for purposes like discovery and identification, such as creator, title and subject,

elements.name: structural metadata
elements.documentation: Metadata that indicates how compound objects are put together.

elements.name: provenance metadata
elements.documentation: Metadata that indicates the relationship between two versions of data objects and is generated whenever a new version of a dataset is created.

elements.name: contextual metadata
elements.documentation: Metadata needed for interpreting the relevance of data such as files in a collection.

elements.name: file (set of records)
elements.documentation: set of related records (either analogue or electronic) kept together.

elements.name: authenticity metadata
elements.documentation: Metadata that conveys information needed to link a data object to its original source.

elements.name: citation metadata
elements.documentation: Metadata that provides an unambiguous identifier to the data cited, its location, and means of access.

elements.name: intrinsic data
elements.documentation: Data which can be read off a material object or document directly through its physical presence.

elements.name: extrinsic data
elements.documentation: Data about a material object or document which cannot be obtained directly from it but only from the communication about it.

elements.name: linked data
elements.documentation: Data which make connections among data available.

elements.name: data quality
elements.documentation: Degree to which a set of inherent characteristics of data fulfils requirements

elements.name: data quality characteristic
elements.documentation: category of data quality attributes that bears on data quality

elements.name: data quality measure
elements.documentation: Variable to which a value is assigned as the result of measurement of a data quality characteristic

elements.name: data quality model
elements.documentation: Defined set of characteristics which provides a framework for specifying data quality requirements and evaluating data quality

elements.name: data accuracy
elements.documentation: Quality of data in respect of the represented value agreeing with the corresponding true value to a degree necessary for an intended purpose.

elements.name: quality
elements.documentation: Degree to which a set of inherent characteristics of an object fulfils requirements.

elements.name: data completeness
elements.documentation: Quality of a data set in respect of the content being all that is necessary for an intended purpose.

elements.name: vocabulary
elements.documentation:
terminological dictionary that contains designations and definitions from one or more domains or subjects

Note: A vocabulary may be monolingual, bilingual or multilingual.

elements.name: data dictionary
elements.documentation: Collection of data dictionary entries that allows lookup by entity identifier

elements.name: data dictionary entry
elements.documentation: Description of an entity type containing, at a minimum, an unambiguous identifier, a term and a definition

elements.name: data specification
elements.documentation: Set of requirements covering the characteristics of data being fit for one or more particular purposes

elements.name: data object
elements.documentation: Collection of data that have a natural grouping and may be identified collectively

elements.name: training data
elements.documentation: Data used to train a machine learning model

elements.name: data set collection
elements.documentation: Curated collection of one or more data sets

elements.name: data representation
elements.documentation: Format, data type, character set and other characteristics used to represent data

elements.name: data set distribution
elements.documentation: Specific available form of a data set or data set collection

elements.name: data environment
elements.documentation: Set of conditions under which data processing or the data process occurs

elements.name: data usage framework
elements.documentation: Framework that sets out the characteristics which should be assessed by the entity in possession of the data and captured within the metadata description

elements.name: data sharing agreement
elements.documentation: Documented agreement that defines, guides and protects data sharing

elements.name: metamodel
elements.documentation: Data model that specifies one or more other models, such as data models, process models, ontologies, etc.

elements.name: exchange format
elements.documentation: machine-readable data format for representing information that is intended to facilitate exchange of the information between different applications

elements.name: storage format
elements.documentation: data format for the hosting of digital data in the main store of a computer or in a long-term storage data repository

elements.name: presentation format
elements.documentation: data format for the configuration of the display of digital data on the screen interface in a manner interpretable for human users

elements.name: data level objective
elements.documentation: commitment that a data holder or a data user makes for a specific, quantitative characteristic of a data set, where the value follows the interval scale or ratio scale

elements.name: data qualitative objective
elements.documentation: commitment that a data holder or a data user makes for a specific, qualitative characteristic of a dataset, where the value follows the nominal scale or ordinal scale

elements.name: authoritative data
elements.documentation: data issued by an organization that is the originator of the data or that is a legal authority

elements.name: ICT Service Management Policy
elements.documentation:
This policy establishes mandatory principles in terms of provision, use, and control, of services provided within NATO through an approach based on the Information Technology Infrastructure Library (ITIL).  The policy is intended as a tool to enable stakeholders to coordinate, deliver and use an efficient and effective set of services matching the needs of the NATO Enterprise while exercising financial responsibility.
This policy encompasses the management and support of all ICT services delivered within the NATO Enterprise, regardless of their source or funding. This includes, among others, NATO-owned, NATO-leased, nationally and commercially provided ICT services.

elements.name: C3 Capabilities and ICT Services Lifecycle Management Policy
elements.documentation:
This policy aims to establish a framework for the development, implementation, use and evolution of NATO C3 Capabilities and ICT Services, the roles and responsibilities of the various stakeholders in this process, with a specific focus on NATO organisations participating in the NATO Enterprise. 
This policy provides the highest level mandatory principles for the management of C3 Capabilities and ICT Services (including CIS Security and Cyber Defence) through a structured lifecycle approach.
The scope of this policy covers the full lifecycle of all C3 Capabilities and ICT Services, which will be used by entities of NATO regardless of their source and funding.

elements.name: Waveform Policy
elements.documentation:
The Waveform Policy provides the principles aimed at improving wireless interoperability through the exploitation of software defined radio technologies and introduction of modern interoperable waveforms. 
A long term goal of the Alliance shall be to build a library or repository, distributed or centzed, of waveform specifications, waveform functional and interoperability reference software that is accessible by NATO and NATO Nations. NonNATO Nations shall have access to this repository on a case-by-case basis, to be agreed by the C3 Board. As a short term solution, NATO shall create a registry of waveform artifacts .
This policy provides guidance than can be used by NATO Nations and other entities that need to interoperate within Alliance Operations, Missions and Exercises using wireless capabilities with regards to waveform standards.

elements.name: C3 Interoperability Policy
elements.documentation:
The aim of the C3 Interoperability Policy is to define and mandate the measures necessary to achieve  and maintain C3 Capabilities and ICT Services interoperability to match Alliance needs. This policy identifies the principles and main responsibilities for ensuring the development and effective use of C3 Capabilities and ICT Services for achieving 
interoperability, supporting the sharing of information across the main dimensions: technical, procedural and human.
technical, procedural and human.

elements.name: Federation of Communication Services Policy
elements.documentation:
Federating national and NATO networks increases survivability and resiliency and achieves savings and efficiencies. The federation of national and NATO networks will also help to develop mutual trust, enhance interoperability and improve the standardisation process, especially where it concerns standardised information exchange and sharing.
The aim of the Federation of Communication Services Policy is to establish the principles for federating networks among NATO and national service providers to support NATO processes, tasks or missions. This policy addresses the establishment of federated networks by NATO Bodies and Nations.
Nations.

elements.name: Software Policy
elements.documentation:
The aim of the NATO Software Policy is to improve the quality, cost effectiveness, Alliance-wide interoperability, national use and security of NATO acquired and operated software. Software should be highly re-usable, full life cycle supported for Alliance needs.
The NATO Software Policy applies to software, specifically developed by NATO or acquired for NATO, that is either the property of NATO or where NATO has unlimited rights to use and distribute. Such software is referred to as NATO Software. For Alliance interoperability it is recommended that Nations apply this policy for multinational acquisitions.

elements.name: C3 Capabilities Implementation Policy
elements.documentation:
With the Alliance C3 Strategy the North Atlantic Council (NAC) approved the adoption of the Adopt, Buy, Create (ABC) concept for the procurement of C3 capabilities and ICT Services. The intent of the ABC concept is to encourage use of existing solutions in lieu of developing new ones wherever feasible. This policy elaborates this concept, encouraging the reuse of existing technology, applications and services, wherever feasible. The expected result will be reduced cost, reduced risk and reduced time spent on development and implementation of solutions.

C3 Capabilities Implementation Policy aims to reduce delivery time, risk and lifecycle costs while preserving, or enhancing, performance and interoperability of C3 Capabilities and ICT Services. This policy provides the principles to facilitate the development, evaluation, selection and implementation of proposals to address C3 Capabilities and ICT Services requirements, for the acquisition of the most effective and timely solutions.

The scope of this policy covers the full spectrum of C3 Capabilities and ICT Services that will be used by NATO Enterprise Organisations.
.

elements.name: Enterprise Architecture Policy
elements.documentation: The Alliance C3 Strategy explicitly calls for a NATO Enterprise Architecture (EA) discipline. The Enterprise Architecture Policy establishes the key objectives and principles for NATO’s Enterprise Architecture discipline, identifies common architecture types and re-usable components as well as NATO EA-related roles and responsibilities. This policy is mandatory for all entities involved in the definition and implementation of static and deployable C3 Capabilities and ICT Services for the NATO Enterprise, and is recommended for implementation in NATO Nations and by federation partners.

elements.name: Cloud Computing Policy
elements.documentation:
The adoption of a cloud computing approach is necessary for the efficient delivery of C3 Capabilities and the consumption of ICT Services. This approach delivers infrastructure, platforms and software using service models and will support the Service Management Policy and the Enterprise Approach’s ambitions.
The Cloud Computing Policy introduces a new business model that treats infrastructure as a shared commodity that is accessed as a service. It sets the principles for providing, maintaining and using a shared, service-oriented computing infrastructure (cloud) to achieve greater availability, flexibility, agility, security and mobility while seeking to reduce costs.
This policy outlines the principles for service providers, customers and users of the NATO Enterprise to apply a cloud computing approach.

elements.name: Green IT Policy
elements.documentation: The Green IT Policy provides principles on defining, acquiring, operating and disposing of C3 Capabilities and ICT Services in an environmentally sustainable manner. The scope of this Policy covers the full spectrum of C3 Capabilities and ICT Services used within the NATO Enterprise.

elements.name: Internet Protocol Version 6 (IPv6) Policy
elements.documentation:
As NATO and national networks become increasingly federated, the plans to migrate to IPv6 need to be aligned and harmonised. In particular, Industry and service providers are starting to reduce efforts in further development of IPv4-based solutions in favour of IPv6, and are increasingly transitioning to IPv6 capabilities.
The Internet Protocol Version 6 (IPv6) Policy provides the principles for a smooth and gradual migration towards IPv6 
in order to harmonise the migration to IPv6, in alignment with Industry and Service Providers, of the NATO Enterprise. 
The scope of this Policy covers the NATO Common Funded C3 Capabilities and ICT Services and is mandatory for the NATO Enterprise. NATO nations are encouraged to use this policy as a basis for their national IPv6 migration.

elements.name: NATO Information Management Policy (NIMP)
elements.documentation: This policy establishes the basic principles of information management to be applied by NATO nations and NATO civil and military bodies. The NIMP establishes a framework to ensure that information is handled effectively, efficiently and securely in order to serve the interests of NATO. This includes managing all aspects of information throughout its life-cycle.

elements.name: Information technology - Big data - Overview and vocabulary
Information and documentation - Foundation and vocabulary
Information technology - Data usage - Terminology and use cases
Cloud Computing and distributed platforms - Data sharing agreement (DSA) framework
Metadata registries (MDR) - Part 33: Metamodel for data set registration
Data quality - Part 51: Data Governance: Exchange of data policy statements
OPA Rego
elements.documentation: Rego is the policy language used by OPA and there are various integrations that make working with the language easier.

elements.name: Open Data Contract Standard (ODCS) 3.0.0
elements.documentation: This standard describes a structure for a data contract.

elements.name: YAML 1.2.2
elements.documentation: YAML is a human-friendly, cross language, Unicode based data serialization language designed around the common native data types of dynamic programming languages. It is broadly useful for programming needs ranging from configuration files to internet messaging to object persistence to data auditing and visualization.

elements.name: JSON-LD 1.1
elements.documentation: JSON is a useful data serialization and messaging format. This specification defines JSON-LD 1.1, a JSON-based format to serialize Linked Data. The syntax is designed to easily integrate into deployed systems that already use JSON, and provides a smooth upgrade path from JSON to JSON-LD. It is primarily intended to be a way to use Linked Data in Web-based programming environments, to build interoperable Web services, and to store Linked Data in JSON-based storage engines.

elements.name: Metadata registries (MDR) - Part 31: Metamodel for data specification registration
Metadata registries (MDR) - Part 3: Registry metamodel and basic attributes
NATO Core Data Framework (NCDF) Application Programming Interfaces (APIs)
Analytical Support
elements.documentation:
The processes detailed here represent the activities that are conducted to support the organization's decision making process outside of specific operational contexts. Elements of the Analytical Support processes may be implemented as components of a wider function or domain process, but except where there are analytical processes specific to certain environments, analytical functions here are described as enabling processes for the entire organization.

There is significant relationship between the Analytical Support processes and the Data Science Processes, and these have been represented independently as far as organizational structure permits.

elements.name: Platform CIS Security Services
elements.documentation: The Platform CIS Security Services provide a foundation to implement uniform, consistent, interoperable and effective web service security. They also provide the necessary means to implement and enforce CIS Security measures at the platform level.

elements.name: Platform Guard Services
elements.documentation: The Platform Guard Services provide the means to connect networks of different information security and management policies and usage areas to control traffic flow in-between the networks following a set of predefined rules for platform services. The intended function is to allow automated data exchange between two network enclaves that belong to different information domains. The services enable a cross-domain information exchange by mediating traffic flows, while offering sufficient protection against the unintended leakage of confidential information and possible degradation of integrity of resources by enforcing an appropriate access control policy.

elements.name: Security Token Services
elements.documentation: The Security Token Services (STS) provide the means to identify providers responsible for issuing security tokens, which may or may not be structured as XML. These security tokens are issued after entity authentication to the STS, and are used to pass entity identity information to other services (Relying Parties) which trust the STS and its tokens.

elements.name: Data Tokenization
elements.documentation:
Tokenization is a privacy-enhancing technology (PETs), which replaces sensitive data with substitute values called tokens. Tokens are stored in a separate, encrypted token vault that maintains the relationship with the original data outside the production environment. When an application calls for the data, the token is mapped to the actual value in the vault outside the production environment. Tokens can represent substitute values in various ways. 
For example, they can retain the format of the original data while revealing only the last few digits. The same token can also represent each instance of the original data.

elements.name: Data Anonymization
elements.documentation: Anonymization is a privacy-enhancing technology (PETs), which is designed to make it impossible (or extremely impractical) to connect personal data to an identifiable person. Organizations can then use, publish, and share that data without requiring permission. Anonymization permanently replaces sensitive data with a substitute value.

elements.name: Data Masking
elements.documentation:
Masking is a privacy-enhancing technology (PETs), whereby sensitive information is replaced by random characters in the same format as the original data, but without a mechanism for retrieving the original values. This is a common practice in test environments, which require realistic-looking data but cannot be populated with actual customer or employee data. Masking is essentially permanent tokenization.
Masking can also be used to control access to sensitive data based on entitlements. This approach, known as dynamic data masking, allows authorized users and applications to retrieve unmasked data from a database, while providing masked data to users who are not authorized to view the sensitive information.

elements.name: Architecting
elements.documentation: The processes for Architecting support the development of architectures for many purposes, and their development can be described as both a process and a discipline. Architecting is a practice for conducting enterprise analysis, design, planning, and implementation, using a holistic engineering approach at all times, for the implementation of strategy. Architecting applies principles and practices to guide organizations through the business/mission, information, application and technology changes necessary to implement their strategies and to develop capabilities that deliver solutions that can meet an organization’s needs to achieve its mission.

elements.name: Architecture Governance
elements.documentation:
The Architecture governance process establish standards and policies related to one or more architectures of interest and their development, and to monitor and facilitate the alignment of the architecture(s) to stakeholder concerns, policies and standards, including organizational and environmental constraints. The process includes the following activities:
* Prepare for and plan the architecture governance effort
* Monitor, assess and control the architecture governance activities
* Establish architecture collection objectives
* Make architecture governance decisions
* Monitor and assess compliance with governance directives and guidance
* Review implementation of governance directives and guidance

elements.name: Architecture Management
elements.documentation:
Architecture Management is a process to plan, run and monitor architectures along their life cycle.
The objective is to have the architectures developed according to enterprise governance direction with regards to stakeholders’ expectations. These activities include:
* Establishing capability for management of one or several architectures in the scope of responsibility, and the related activities,
* Establishing plans for conducting architecture management activities according to the architecture governance directions,
* Providing guidance and direction for architecting activities,
* Monitoring and assess architecture development with management direction, and
* Deciding on necessary corrective actions and iterate.
This process is normally lead in different organizations of the defence enterprise where architecture developments are taking place. It strongly depends on the kinds of architecture (e.g. Enterprise, Capability, Solution level) being developed.

elements.name: Architecture Description
elements.documentation:
Architecture description process includes the following main activities:
* Analyse the problem situation (purpose, scope and objectives),
* Identify the stakeholders, their concerns and needs,
* Formalize and classify key requirements from collected needs,
* Identify the potential solutions,
* Identify architecture viewpoints according to stakeholders’ concerns,
* Develop models and views of candidate architectures from these viewpoints,
* Provide the rationale of the potential solutions with regard of requirements and motivation data. In particular, ensure their traceability to motivation data,
* Review architecture candidates with stakeholders and get their approval, and
* State relations between candidate architectures and design and other downstream activities.

elements.name: Architecture Conceptualization
elements.documentation:
Architecture Conceptualization Processes generate architecture alternatives, to select one or more alternatives that address stakeholder concerns and meet relevant requirements, and to express them in a set of consistent views.
Architecture conceptualization process include the following activities:
* Prepare for and plan the architecture conceptualization effort
* Monitor, assess and control the architecture conceptualization activities
* Characterize problem space
* Establish architecture objectives and critical success criteria
* Synthesize potential solution(s) in the solution space
* Characterize solutions and the tradespace
* Formulate candidate architecture(s)
* Capture architecture concepts and properties
* Relate the architecture to other architectures and to relevant affected entities
* Coordinate use of conceptualized architecture by intended users

elements.name: Architecture Evaluation
elements.documentation:
Architecture Evaluation determines the degree to which the architecture meets architecture objectives and addresses stakeholder concerns. Architecture Evaluation process includes the following activities:
* define evaluation purpose, scope and objectives,
* identify the stakeholders of the architecture evaluation, and their concerns or questions,
* determine evaluation criteria (according to stakeholders’ concerns/questions) with their relative importance (priorities, weights, etc.),
* determine techniques, methods and tools for performing the evaluation,
* evaluate the architecture,
* collect and understand required information (metrics), and
* formulate the findings and recommendations.

elements.name: Establish Business Model Governance
elements.documentation:
Creating and implementing a strategy, responsibilities and control mechanisms for managing business models that are timely, efficient and cost-effective.

Business model governance includes Force Performance Management: the relevance of activities of the military force in relation to strategic outcomes and the overall political priorities. The Performance of military forces in delivering its activities in terms of:
* Efficiency –- the extent to which expected outputs are produced with the least amount of input (resources).
* Effectiveness -– the extent to which the Fleet is achieving its expected outcomes.
* Economy –- the extent to which costs of resources used are minimized but having regard for quality and quantity.

elements.name: Maintain Business Models
elements.documentation: Revising and updating business models to reflect the changes in the services, product inventory, market behavior, available resources or accrued expenses. Determine how and when to modify the accepted business model in response to incoming feedback.

elements.name: Develop Business Models
elements.documentation: Creating a model that describes the goals of an organization and the business processes needed to achieve those goals. This involves information gathering, securing necessary approvals and authorizations, integrating with preexisting models, including the general business concept and business strategy, and formally accepting the model as the basis for organization's day-to-day operations.

elements.name: Composition Services
elements.documentation:
The Composition Services provide the means to access and fuse data and behavior on demand, and return a single result to the consumer. The services can, from queues and/or in batch, provide a set of data transforms and routings to transactions that can serve machine-to-machine business processes.

A service composition is a coordinated aggregate of services. The consistent application of service-orientation design principles leads to the creation of services with functional contexts that are agnostic to any one business process. These agnostic services are therefore capable of participating in multiple service compositions. Services are expected to be capable of participating as effective composition members, regardless of whether they need to be immediately enlisted in a composition.

There are two aspects of composition: composition synthesis is concerned with synthesizing a specification of how to coordinate the component services to fulfil the client request; and orchestration, is concerned with how to actually achieve the coordination among services, by executing the specification produced by the composition synthesis and by suitably supervising and monitoring that execution.

elements.name: Mediation Services
elements.documentation: The Mediation Services provide a middle layer between incompatible producers of information and consumers of information. Mediation services process the data of the information producer and transform it into a representation which is understandable for the consumer. In doing so Mediation Services bridge the gap between both parties, enabling interaction between them which has not been possible beforehand.

elements.name: Core Services
elements.documentation:
Core services represent a collection of community of interest (COI)-independent technical services at the back-end of communications and information systems (CIS) capabilities, focused on the technical functionality to implement service-based environments using infrastructure, architectural and enabling building blocks. Core services provide these building blocks so that these generic, common capabilities do not have to be implemented by individual applications or other services.

In the context of the C3 Taxonomy, COI services are facilitating user applications and COI services and connecting these to communications and networking services in the back-end. The second layer of the associated taxonomy specifies three main categories:
* Business Support Services -- to provide the means to facilitate other service and data providers on the enterprise network by providing and managing underlying capabilities for collaboration and information management. These services are enablers used by other services and users across the whole network-enabled enterprise, acting as "building blocks" for developing more sophisticated COI services and applications. Therefore, they are COI-independent and they must be available to all enterprise members.
* Platform Services -- to provide a foundation to implement services in a loosely coupled environment, where flexible and agile service orchestration is a requirement. They offer generic building blocks for implementation (e.g. discovery, message busses, orchestration, information abstraction and access, etc.) and can be used as a capability integration platform in a heterogeneous service-provisioning ecosystem.
* Infrastructure Services -- to provide the foundation to host infrastructure services in a distributed and/or federated environment in support of NATO operations and exercises. They include computing, storage and high-level networking services that can be used as the basis for data centre or cloud computing implementations. The Infrastructure Services are aligned with "Infrastructure as a Service" (IaaS) concepts that are used and promoted by industry as part of their cloud computing developments.

elements.name: Data Platform Services
elements.documentation:
Data Platform Services enable faster access to trusted data across distributed environments by utilizing active metadata, knowledge graphs, semantics and machine learning (ML) capabilities of data integration (as well as other data management tools, including data catalogs and data governance). The Data Platform Services must be able to deliver data in various styles (not just batch, but a combination of batch with data virtualization, streaming, messaging or API-based delivery styles). Data Platform Services integrate data from different sources to provide consistency across multiple environments or systems, or technologies leveraged in an enterprise. 

Data Platform Services aim to provide an abstraction layer above all of the different services and systems that it touches to create more fluidity across data environments, by accessing data in place or support its consolidation where appropriate. Data Platform Services abstract away the technological complexities engaged for data movement, transformation and integration, making all data available across the enterprise. Data Platform Services utilize continuous analytics over existing, discoverable and inferenced metadata assets to support the design, deployment and utilization of integrated and reusable data across different data storage technologies and multiple environments, including hybrid and multi-cloud platforms.
Data Platform Services continuously identify, connect and analyze data and metadata from disparate sources to discover unique, operationally-relevant relationships between the available data points.

A data fabric architecture provides flexible, reusable and augmented data management (i.e. better semantics, integration and organization of data) through metadata. Metadata drives the fabric design. Compared to traditional approaches, active metadata and semantic inference are key new aspects of a data fabric to discover new insights. A data fabric utilizes continuous analytics of existing, discoverable and inferenced metadata assets to support the design, deployment and utilization of integrated and reusable data objects regardless of deployment platform. It can include automated orchestration for data access, data integration, data quality, use of knowledge graphs, and even data utilization and usage recommendations. A data fabric utilizes as much metadata as is available from any other contributing data management platform or tools. 

Fabric designs evolves over time. Initially, existing systems can passively participate in the fabric design by sharing their metadata. A metadata-driven data fabric has significant potential to reduce data management efforts, including design, deployment and operations, supporting the DevSecOps approach for modern software development. When the fabric design matures, participating systems actively adapt to the alerts and recommendations generated by the fabric through data analytics, data and AI orchestration.

elements.name: Data Orchestration Services
elements.documentation:
Data Orchestration Services execute data pipelines that are taking siloed data from multiple data storage locations, coordinating the combining, verifying, organizing and storing of that data and making it available for data analysis tools. Data orchestration services evolved from manual orchestration efforts with an emphasis on automation, conceptualization and analytics to support optimization.

While data orchestration services operate within larger workflows, the actual work they accomplish can vary from between different implementations. By and large, however, these tasks fall into five primary parts:
* Collecting and Preparing Data -- More often than not, data must be structured and prepared before it enters or moves through a system. This includes performing checks for integrity and correctness, applying labels and designations, or enriching new third-party data with existing database information.
